### Post by hasofanten on 2011-10-20
Hello everyone!

I have been looking at the new asus zenbook 13.3" model (ux31) and i would like to run ubuntu on it.

I am not an experienced ubuntu user, I started using it at school and have come to like alot.

The question I have is if anyone know if ubuntu will run on the zenbook. I have been trying to find out in more detail what hardware the zenbook use but I haven't found anything. :(
The only specification I have been able to find is in the link below.
[http://zenbook.asus.com/product/?c=spec](http://zenbook.asus.com/product/?c=spec)

Also, I was wondering about the standby features of ubuntu. The zenbook is able to be up and runing only 2 seconds after waking it from hibernation, but that is when windows is installed. Does anyone know if this 2-second-running is a feature of the zenbook, windows or both? Again, I have been trying to find information about this but haven't really found anything.

What do you guys think? Will ubuntu run on the zenbook and if so, will it be able to utalize the fast wakeup feature?

Thanks in advance!
:)

/Haso

### Post by RR123RR on 2011-10-21
Multitouch is broken, but, more importantly, sleep is totally broken.

Going to sleep will basically kill the laptop, you'll have to shutdown by holding down the power key.
And on battery, it seems to be running at full power for some reason and randomly change the  brightness from time to time.
Finally, after switching to the external screen by mistake, I couldn't bring the screen back, I had to shutdown.

There are probably a few other quirks...
 (oh, the external USB Ethernet doesn't seem to work by default either but that's external, so not exactly directly related even though it's bundled with the laptop).

Other than that, it's a sweet little machine though, I can't wait for it to be well supported! :-)

### Post by Frederik Seidelin on 2011-10-27
Hey all,

I know that this probably doesn't apply to this model as it's a different motherboard, BIOS, video card, and what-not. However, it'd be silly to remain silent for that reason if some of this could actually help you in narrowing down the causes of the problems.

I own an ASUS U33Jc laptop (the one made of bamboo), and it has problems with suspend, multitouch, and the processor steaming ahead at 2.53 GHz instead of reducing the clockspeed to the more frugal (and quite sufficient) 1.2 GHz.

Although I can't remember which parts from where, I've used bits and pieces from the links below in making my PC work adequately:

[LIST]
[*][http://linux.slashdot.org/story/11/10/07/237222/Kernel-Bug-Means-Linux-Power-Usage-Remains-High](http://linux.slashdot.org/story/11/10/07/237222/Kernel-Bug-Means-Linux-Power-Usage-Remains-High)
[*][http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=ubuntu_linux_epb&num=1](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=ubuntu_linux_epb&num=1)
[*][http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1615564&highlight=u33jc](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1615564&highlight=u33jc)
[*][http://www.varesano.net/blog/fabio/linux%20howto%20two%20fingers%20scrolling%20regular%20synaptics%20notebook%20touchpad](http://www.varesano.net/blog/fabio/linux%20howto%20two%20fingers%20scrolling%20regular%20synaptics%20notebook%20touchpad)
[*][https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/CPU_Frequency_Scaling](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/CPU_Frequency_Scaling)
[/LIST]
Unfortunately, it would seem that ASUS isn't that Linux-friendly. Previous to this one, I had an Eee 1000, which didn't work OTB either. :-\

Good luck! :-)

### Post by shakabra on 2011-11-05
I just figured out the suspend problem with the help of this: [http://askubuntu.com/questions/22237/when-will-we-get-sandy-bridge-support]("http://askubuntu.com/questions/22237/when-will-we-get-sandy-bridge-support")

Do this:

1. ```
sudo gedit /etc/pm/config.d/unload_module
``` 
2. Add 
```
SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci_hcd"
``` 
to that file. Save and exit.
3. Reboot
4. Suspend
5. Resume 
6. Profit

Notes:

1. This unloads the USB 3 module before a suspend. I'm pretty sure that it gets reloaded during resume, but that's just a guess. I haven't tested that.

2. If /etc/pm/config.d/unload_module doesn't exist just create it. That's what I did.

3. I don't know if this breaks anything else. I just did this about 5 minutes ago.

Hopefully this works for everyone and we can check this off of the list of issues.

*******************EDIT***************************
**[COLOR="Red"]THIS DIDN'T ACTUALLY WORK. I JUMPED THE GUN. MUST'VE PRESSED HIBERNATE INSTEAD OF SUSPEND. DISREGARD. SORRY. :-([/COLOR]**

*******************NUTHEREDIT********************
It kind of works. Sometimes it will resume. Sometimes not. If I use "fn+F1" it seems to work more often. I don't know. I'm going to go read some logs.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-06
> **RR123RR said:**
> 
With this modification (according to the address I found in lsusb -v):

```
+EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
-EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1a.0 0000:00:1d.0"
+XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
-XHCI_BUSES="0000:04:00.0"
```

I get my ssh prompt  back after resume instead of a total crash but I'm still not getting my screen back... any idea?

I followed the instructions and used your addresses. 
I also did what Shakabra did. 
I have also installed the jupiter power management tool..

My suspend and resume apparently works properly now - but only when plugged in. When on battery only the video goes wild on resume.

I slightly patched the script to reapply disabling of tap-to-click.. so I have:

in /etc/pm/sleep.d/20_zenbook_ux31
```
#!/bin/sh

EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"

case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        ;;
esac

```

and in /etc/pm/config.d/unload_module
```
SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci_hcd"

```

And as mentioned, I have the jupiter power management tool running..

When I shut the lid, it suspends and the power LED blinks in a similar way to how I recall it did under Windows7.. when I open the lid, I get the good ol' lock-screen prompt. 

Anyone able to reproduce this?

EDIT: The video sometimes goes haywire when resuming with the power connected.. so there's still something odd going on

- Installed jupiter to manage the CPU modes
- Added the unload of the USB3 module
- Added the script I previously posted to remove serial modules when suspending

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-08
Hey all,

I've installed a 3.1 kernel. 

In combination with the aforementioned USB3 unloader script I'm getting 100% sleep and resume reliability.

I've closed and opened the lid 12 times so far and it's slept and woken properly (and on battery only)..

I'll keep doing it whilst I work (on another machine) to see if it is stable :)

EDIT: I've done it 20 or 30 times and it still resumes perfectly.. I'll stop now and concentrate on work - hopefully someone else can reproduce this success

EDIT: Instructions for kernel update:
```

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.1-oneiric/linux-headers-3.1.0-030100_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-headers-3.1.0-030100_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_all.deb

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.1-oneiric/linux-headers-3.1.0-030100-generic_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-headers-3.1.0-030100-generic_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_amd64.deb

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.1-oneiric/linux-image-3.1.0-030100-generic_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-image-3.1.0-030100-generic_3.1.0-030100.201110241006_amd64.deb

```

 My DH52 is a regular one from J&R from the first batch.

So in the BIOS I _now_ have (anything that could be related to suspend):
* Wake On Lid Open [Enabled]
* Enter Intel(R) AT Supsend Mode [Disable]
* UEFI Boot [Enabled]

(/etc/pm/sleep.d)>ls -l 20_custom-asus-ux31 
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 857 2011-11-09 00:11 20_custom-asus-ux31

```
#!/bin/sh
EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook UX31 touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        ;;
esac
```And I'm running an up to date Ubuntu 64-bit 11.10... :)

With 3.1+vbox my sound is dead and it's the virtualbox driver that is crashing, though I strangely got the screen back when I typed "reboot" from ssh (after not getting the screen back when waking up!) but I had to force the shutdown, it crashed...

### Post by shakabra on 2011-11-10
Ok, I think I got it...

I added 

```
SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci-hcd ehci-hcd"
```

to /etc/pm/config.d/unload_modules and it is suspending and resuming perfectly now and I no longer have the "Fail to train DP" error message.

So I have:

1. Upgraded kernel to 3.1
2. Added script mentioned by GodzillaMonster to /etc/pm/sleep.d
3. Added script to /etc/pm/config.d 

Previously I only had 

```
SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci-hcd"
``` in /etc/pm/config.d

Seems to work now. I will keep you guys posted about anything else funky that comes up.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-10

I removed the stuff in /etc/pm/config.d since I was getting error messages in pm-suspend.log from the sleep.d script, since I think it was removing the device before running the script, which made the script fail.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-10
> **shakabra said:**
> All right GodzillaMonster IDKWTF is going on here, but you're right it does work with just the sleep.d script now. I should have kept better notes of the changes or this computer is just messing with me IDK.

Also I cleared my pm-suspend.log and did ```
cat /var/log/pm-suspend.log | grep xhci_hcd  
``` and came up with nothing.

I have a flimsy theory...

The original config.d script with SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci-hcd" causes the suspend script to throw an error.. so both USB modules weren't being disabled by the sleep.d script reliably

The second SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci-hcd ehci-hcd" might not actually work properly.. or it works in a different way that lets the sleep script function. I thought initially it would do the same thing as the sleep script, but I tried just the SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci-hcd ehci-hcd" and not the sleep.d script, and nothing works.

Finally, I have an unfounded suspicion that your setting your monitor output may have set up the situation where you could in fact succeed with the sleep.d script.

We need someone with a fresh install to:

[LIST=1]
[*]Add the sleep script
[*]Update the kernel to 3.1
[/LIST]

Then: if suspend doesn't work, try the Jupiter-set-monitor-output quirk.

(ps: do you have jupiter remnants in /etc/pm/power.d ?)

- Created a file /etc/pm/config.d/unload_modules with the contents

```

SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci_hcd"

```(I know others had lines about ehci too; I initially had that, but removed - no ehci module is loaded anyway)

- Installed Jupiter and try all three of the display options

The laptop is now going to sleep correctly when the lid is closed, but when I open it again the display is blank.  Pushing keys may cause the screen to dim/brighten, but there's nothing actually on the display.  The machine responds to ping, and I can log in via SSH, but nothing I do will get the screen to re-appear, even killing X.

For me, there's an even bigger problem - the internal fan seems to be constantly running and full speed.  Presumably as a result of this, the battery is only lasting ~ 3 hours - even when the CPU is clocked down to 800Mhz.  Perhaps we should deal with one issue at a time and try and sort out sleep first.

### Post by shakabra on 2011-11-10
Ironmantis,

Enter your BIOS by pressing DEL during power-on. In the "Boot" tab find an option something like "BBS Priorities" or something. Find your CD drive and bring it to the top of the list with the + button (SHIFT & =/+). Then save changes and exit. Upon reboot your drive should be recognized and booted from.

### Post by Bashtoni on 2011-11-11
OK, after some messing about with xset, I found that setting the screen off via DPMS and then back on again caused the display to re-appear.

I've therefore come up with this hacky fix.  This works for me but is a bit ugly; I'm sure there must be a way to do this which will work multi-users, and probably avoiding xset altogether.

Here's my /etc/pm/sleep.d/20_custom-asus-ux31 file.  For anyone with the same issue, paste the below into that file, edit the Xauthority line to include your homedir instead of mine, and chmod +x the file.

```

#!/bin/bash
EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook UX31 touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        # Hacky workaround to fix display after suspend
        export DISPLAY=":0"
        export XAUTHORITY="/home/sam/.Xauthority"
        xset dpms force off
        xset dpms force on
        ;;
esac

```

---

### Post by yodogg on 2011-11-13
Hello All,

Thanks for the 20_custom-asus-ux31 script.  In my case, moving to kernel  3.1 was not necessary.  I commented out the touch-to-tap section, as I  found that enabling the feature did not adversely affect sleep/resume.

So my   /etc/pm/sleep.d/20_custom-asus-ux31 file looks like this:

```

#!/bin/bash
EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook UX31 touchpad
        # echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        # echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        # Hacky workaround to fix display after suspend
        export DISPLAY=":0"
        export XAUTHORITY="/home/yossof/.Xauthority"
        xset dpms force off
        xset dpms force on
        ;;
esac

```On the other hand, once realizing that the wifi was spotty, and often  dropping the connection, I did switch to kernel 3.1.  This solved the  issue.  Unfortunately, compiling the usb-ethernet driver with kernel 3.1  failed ( it compiled successfully in the previous kernel.) So for now i  don't have the ethernet functionality.

The usb-ethernet driver can be found here:
[www.asix.com.tw/FrootAttach/driver/AX88772B_772A_760_772_178_LINUX_Driver_v4.1.0_Source.tar.bz2]("http://www.asix.com.tw/FrootAttach/driver/AX88772B_772A_760_772_178_LINUX_Driver_v4.1.0_Source.tar.bz2")

Another issue for me is that the computer is generally running hot (59  C). Running powertop shows that some usb devices are running at 100  percent, eg. bluetooth (which is off).  So i'm still investigating these  issues.  Jupiter is installed but so far I have not seen its benefit.  I  know there is a jupiter version for eee asus pc's which include  functionality for its super hybrid engine.  Since the UX21/31 use asus'  super hybrid engine 2, we may need to wait for jupiter to catch up.


Cheers,

    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook UX31 touchpad
        # echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        # echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        # Hacky workaround to fix display after suspend
        export DISPLAY=":0"
        export XAUTHORITY="/home/yossof/.Xauthority"
        xset dpms force off
        xset dpms force on
        ;;
esac

```On the other hand, once realizing that the wifi was spotty, and often  dropping the connection, I did switch to kernel 3.1.  This solved the  issue.  Unfortunately, compiling the usb-ethernet driver with kernel 3.1  failed ( it compiled successfully in the previous kernel.) So for now i  don't have the ethernet functionality.

The usb-ethernet driver can be found here:
[www.asix.com.tw/FrootAttach/driver/AX88772B_772A_760_772_178_LINUX_Driver_v4.1.0_Source.tar.bz2]("http://www.asix.com.tw/FrootAttach/driver/AX88772B_772A_760_772_178_LINUX_Driver_v4.1.0_Source.tar.bz2")

Another issue for me is that the computer is generally running hot (59  C). Running powertop shows that some usb devices are running at 100  percent, eg. bluetooth (which is off).  So i'm still investigating these  issues.  Jupiter is installed but so far I have not seen its benefit.  I  know there is a jupiter version for eee asus pc's which include  functionality for its super hybrid engine.  Since the UX21/31 use asus'  super hybrid engine 2, we may need to wait for jupiter to catch up.


Cheers,

Jupiter won't ever catch up if no-one sends me data about SHE2. ;)

Does anyone know how the kernel support for it works yet?

---

### Post by ironmantis7x on 2011-11-13
> **Bashtoni said:**
> OK, after some messing about with xset, I found that setting the screen off via DPMS and then back on again caused the display to re-appear.

I've therefore come up with this hacky fix.  This works for me but is a bit ugly; I'm sure there must be a way to do this which will work multi-users, and probably avoiding xset altogether.

Here's my /etc/pm/sleep.d/20_custom-asus-ux31 file.  For anyone with the same issue, paste the below into that file, edit the Xauthority line to include your homedir instead of mine, and chmod +x the file.

```

#!/bin/bash
EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook UX31 touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        # Hacky workaround to fix display after suspend
        export DISPLAY=":0"
        export XAUTHORITY="/home/sam/.Xauthority"
        xset dpms force off
        xset dpms force on
        ;;
esac

```

Looks like this may have worked.  I will give it some more testing and see what happens ...

thanks ALL!!

ironmantis7x

No CPU throttles by turning off instruction set features that I know of.

The first two i686 refer to processor and hardware platform, the i386 refers to OS.  What you're seeing is perfectly normal.

The issue seems to be devices that should be sleeping (and probably are under Windows) are awake 100% of the time under Linux.

Are there any Windows tools which show power usage in watts?  powertop is reporting between 10W and 15W discharge rate in Linux, which seems pretty high to me.

### Post by Galdrapiu on 2011-11-16
I needed some EHCI-BUS more (see second line) to get the suspend script finaly running on my UX21E  

/etc/pm/sleep.d/20_custom-asus-ux21 


```

#!/bin/bash
EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1a.0 0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook Sentelic touchpad
        #echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        #echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        # Hacky workaround to fix display after suspend
        export DISPLAY=":0"
        export XAUTHORITY="/home/mpilloud/.Xauthority"
        xset dpms force off
        xset dpms force on
        ;;
esac

```Thanks for all your Input ... it's slowly get's to be fun with my UX21

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-19
> **nomego said:**
> Hmm I guess that would work fine if a mouse is used, but what I really want is to disable it when typing.

This option should appear in the "Mouse and touchpad" settings but it doesn't, and I guess it's because it is a Sentelic touchpad.
Syndaemon won't run either.

Regarding kernels, I've successfully booted 3.1.1 from the site mentioned, but 3.2 rc1 and rc2 will not boot. I'm guessing this is related to the ASPM fixes but I'm not sure how to debug this aspect of the kernel.

There's another thread that covers this issue, though it is also in this thread hidden somewhere.

To disable the touch-to-tap-to-click-to-tap, put this in /etc/rc.local
```

echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
```

And be sure to keep the same lines as above in the suspend script mentioned in this thread.

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-19
Well I guess I'll have to permanently remove that feature then.
I'd like to be able to tap-to-click, just not when I'm typing. Oh well.

I tried the xhci-unload suspend script with xset dpms from a page or two back and it works perfectly.

I have the following contribution myself to apply the tunables recommended by powertop when on battery (/etc/pm/power.d/zenbook-powersave):

```

#!/bin/sh
# Powertop tunables

case $1 in
        true)
                ## VM writeback timeout
                echo 1500 > /proc/sys/vm/dirty_writeback_centisecs

                ## Autosuspend for USB devices
                ### USB2.0-CRW (Generic)
                echo auto > /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.7/power/level
                ### Bluetooth USB Host Controller (Atheros Communications)
                echo auto > /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.8/power/level

                ## Runtime PM for PCI devices
                ### Atheros Communications Inc. AR9485 Wireless Network Adapter
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:02:00.0/power/control
                ### Fresco Logic Device 1009
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:03:00.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family 6 port SATA AHCI Controller
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1f.2/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family USB Enhanced Host Controller #1
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1d.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 2nd Generation Core Processor Family DRAM Controller
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:00.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family MEI Controller #1
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:16.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family High Definition Audio Controller
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1b.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family PCI Express Root Port 1
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.0/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family PCI Express Root Port 2
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.1/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family PCI Express Root Port 4
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.3/power/control
                ### Intel Corporation 2nd Generation Core Processor Family Integrated Graphics Controller
                echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/power/control

                ## SATA link power management
                echo min_power > /sys/class/scsi_host/host0/link_power_management_policy

                # 50% screen brightness on battery
                echo 5 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness
        ;;
        false)
                # 90% screen brightness on AC
                echo 9 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness
        ;;
esac

exit 0

```

This script is run on startup automatically so no need to call it from rc.local if starting on battery.

Also, these get reset automatically when AC is inserted:

[LIST]
[*]Wireless Power Saving for interface wlan0
[*]Enable SATA link power management for /dev/sda
[*]Power Aware CPU scheduler
[*]VM writeback timeout
[*]Enable Audio codec power management
[/LIST]

Also I noticed a significant drop in Watt-usage when running the 3.1.1 kernel with parameter i915.i915_enable_rc6=1

All of the above power saving efforts above increases estimated battery life when almost idle from about 3 to 8 hours.

My next step will be to try to apply the ASPM-patches to a 3.2rc2 kernel in hopes that it might also make it boot :)

Other things to look into for better power efficiency might be:
[LIST]
[*]pcie_aspm=force
[*]i915.i915_enable_fbc=1
[*]vi915.lvds_downclock=1
[/LIST]

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-22
Galdrapiu: Well, that is a completely different shutdown.
If the only change you've done is the powertop script, try commenting this line out or removing it from the script:

```

### Intel Corporation 2nd Generation Core Processor Family Integrated Graphics Controller
echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/power/control

```

It's usually graphics issues that can cause X to crash hard..

You should be able to just insert/remove AC for the change to take effect (or run the script with the argument 'true') but if the crash still occurs, please reboot and evaluate again (and report back here!).

Regarding the shutdown freeze, does it happen every time?
Can you boot the computer on battery and turn it off from the login screen and it still stalls?
If so, the best approach would probably be to comment out all lines, enable one at a time and try to shut down.
If you find a line that can cause this issue by itself, enable all others and try again - there might be several settings or combinations of settings that can cause this..


So basically we've currently found 4 bugs with regards to this hardware:

[LIST=1]
[*]Some i915 KMS regression in 3.2 rc1/rc2
[*]Sudden shutdowns with Intel RC6 enabled (regardless of pcie_aspm=force?)
[*]A bug in the powersaving features of a device driver causing X to crash
[*]A bug in the powersaving features of a device driver causing shutdowns to freeze/stall (could be same as above)
[/LIST]

---

### Post by Galdrapiu on 2011-11-22
> **nomego said:**
> 
Regarding the shutdown freeze, does it happen every time?
Can you boot the computer on battery and turn it off from the login screen and it still stalls?
If so, the best approach would probably be to comment out all lines, enable one at a time and try to shut down.
If you find a line that can cause this issue by itself, enable all others and try again - there might be several settings or combinations of settings that can cause this..


Hi Nomego. 

For the shutdown freeze the following line is responsible. 
```

### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family USB Enhanced Host Controller #1   
echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1d.0/power/control

```It is reproducable on my UX21E and happens every time during shutdown while running on battery. 
Commenting this line out solves the shutdown freeze problem  (4).

Thanks for your differentiated contribution
Galdrapiu

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-22
Galdrapiu: Great work figuring what was causing the shutdown freeze!
I've commented on this in the script on the wiki, but kernel developers should be notified as well. bugzilla.kernel.org seems down at the moment though.
Regarding the X-shutdowns, it seems like you're the only one with that specific problem, check /var/log/Xorg.0.log, dmesg, ~/.xsession-errors and other logs for hints.

I guess that brings us down to two issues, KMS in 3.2 and RC6 causing sudden, unexpected shutdowns.

GodzillaMonster: What kernel parameters are you using now?
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/38117/battery-life-decreased-after-upgrade-to-11-04/38194#38194](http://askubuntu.com/questions/38117/battery-life-decreased-after-upgrade-to-11-04/38194#38194) has some pretty interesting information on the two additional paramters. (fbc and lvds_downclock)

ironmantis7x: Would you mind helping out by installing the 3.0 kernel from [http://zinc.canonical.com/~cking/powermanagement/mjgaspmfix/oneiric](http://zinc.canonical.com/~cking/powermanagement/mjgaspmfix/oneiric) and following the test procedure at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/PowerManagement](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/PowerManagement) ?

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-24
My Micro HDMI -> HDMI cable arrived as well and it worked perfectly.
Even sound through HDMI worked great after setting a HDMI-profile for the sound card in Sound Settings.

Also, I've been mailing the LKML the issues we're having, and I have good news for one issue - a fix for the shutdown problem with the powersave-script is released and queued for the 3.1 kernel: [http://www.spinics.net/lists/stable-commits/msg14520.html](http://www.spinics.net/lists/stable-commits/msg14520.html)
So this should be fixed in 3.1.3.

Regarding 3.2 not booting with KMS, the problematic commit has to be figured out by us, so I'll look into how one would go about compiling vanilla kernels from git so we can bisect the kernel without ubuntu patches. Unless someone else wants to do this? :)

Finally, regarding RC6 I haven't received a response, but when I do, it can be seen on [https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/11/23/507](https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/11/23/507) as well.

EDiT: Updated wiki with additional keyboard function information, HDMI audio note, powersave script comment.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-24
> **ocir30 said:**
> It has been said in another earlier post but I remark again.

So often, the wireless network turns off and reconnects, too it receives a low signal. At the moment, I work with Ubuntu 11.10 amd64, kernel 3.1.1 and option "aspm = force". With kernel 3.0 the wireless turns off more often. 

Any solution?

I also confirm that with the "i915.i915_enable_rc6 = 1" shuts down the computer from time to time.

Cheers,

The bars on the toolbar are always low (2 or 3), but for me that hasn't resulted in any wireless drop-outs or stability issues. I just imagined that the calibration for the signal strength was incorrect.

Presumably your aspm setting is actually "pcie_aspm=force" and you also have  acpi_osi=\"Linux\" in your GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT variable.

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-25
Ok, I have now bisected between 3.1 and 3.2 rc1 (5200 commits later..) and the first commit that breaks KMS is:

```

commit dc22ee6fc18ce0f15424e753e8473c306ece95c1
Author: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
Date:   Fri Oct 14 12:43:50 2011 -0400

    drm/i915/dp: Remove eDP special cases from bandwidth checks
    
    These were just working around the math being wrong.

```

Sadly, I realize that I forgot the charger at work, so I won't be able to do any more work on this during the weekend.

---

I have upgraded to kernel 3.1.2 from 3.0 to get my wireless to work okay.

But in kernel 3.1.2 my touchpad i recognized as:

I: Bus=0011 Vendor=0002 Product=0001 Version=0063
N: Name="PS/2 Logitech Wheel Mouse"
P: Phys=isa0060/serio4/input0
S: Sysfs=/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/input/input9

And in kernel 3.0 it is:

I: Bus=0011 Vendor=0002 Product=0001 Version=0000
N: Name="PS/2 Elantech Touchpad"
P: Phys=isa0060/serio4/input0
S: Sysfs=/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/input/input9
U: Uniq=
H: Handlers=mouse0 event9 
B: PROP=0
B: EV=7
B: KEY=70000 0 0 0 0
B: REL=3

When I run the 2 disable echo commands:
echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
Is says "No such file or directory" to both of them.

the folder looks like this:
ls -l /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/

--w------- 1 root root 4096 2011-11-25 20:47 drvctl
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root    0 2011-11-25 20:47 id
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root    0 2011-11-25 19:33 input
-r--r--r-- 1 root root 4096 2011-11-25 20:47 modalias
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root    0 2011-11-25 20:47 power
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 4096 2011-11-25 20:47 protocol

### Post by darfe on 2011-11-25
> **nomego said:**
> Ok, I have now bisected between 3.1 and 3.2 rc1 (5200 commits later..) and the first commit that breaks KMS is:

```

commit dc22ee6fc18ce0f15424e753e8473c306ece95c1
Author: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
Date:   Fri Oct 14 12:43:50 2011 -0400

    drm/i915/dp: Remove eDP special cases from bandwidth checks
    
    These were just working around the math being wrong.

```

Sadly, I realize that I forgot the charger at work, so I won't be able to do any more work on this during the weekend.

I reverted this patch

[http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux-2.6.git;a=commitdiff;h=dc22ee6fc18ce0f15424e753e8473c306ece95c1]("http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux-2.6.git;a=commitdiff;h=dc22ee6fc18ce0f15424e753e8473c306ece95c1")

like 
```
	
...
...
        }

	if (!is_edp(intel_dp) &&
	    (intel_dp_link_required(intel_dp, mode->clock)
	     > intel_dp_max_data_rate(max_link_clock, max_lanes)))
		return MODE_CLOCK_HIGH;

	if (mode->clock < 10000)
...
...

```

And added the 
```
  
...
...    
         if (is_edp(intel_dp)){
         /* okay we failed just pick the highest */
...
...

```

And got the screen working on 3.2-rc3 with GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash i915.modeset=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 pcie_aspm=force"

> **Oblong_Cheese said:**

If you are using that script, then perhaps try removing it and rebooting.

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-11-28
> **GodzillaMonster said:**
> I have used a USB3 drive and a multitude of other devices on my USB3 port.

My port did stop functioning when I used the /etc/pm/power.d/zenbook-powersave script posted here, but I don't use that script anymore.

If you are using that script, then perhaps try removing it and rebooting.

Or, if you are using the script, try commenting out the following line in the script and rebooting:

```

### Fresco Logic Device 1009
echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:03:00.0/power/control

```

I just did a simple test with an USB-drive, but that powered it up for me.

EDIT: Pasted the wrong lines.

### Post by broeker on 2011-11-29
Fan issues:

I've got the i7/256G model currently set up as a dual boot with W7 and 11.10 on a separate partition. 

In Windows, the fan often turns off completely. For example if I start reading a web page the fan will turn off and go completely silent. If I start browsing or open an application it will kick in again until things go idle.

In Ubuntu, the fan is running 100% of the time even with no browser or other applications open. I can walk away for 10 minutes and when I get back the fan is still humming along. Granted it is very quiet but annoying and I'd imagine a battery drain. 

Any ideas? 

ETA: I should mention that even when the screen powers down after a 15 minutes idle time the fan STILL continues to hum along.

p.s. I've been a loyal Thinkpad user for 10+ years until this machine seduced me. And, man, I really want to love it but between the non-functioning multi-touch and the constant fan operation I'm very close to returning for a T420s or the X220. They both have their own flaws (bad screen on t420 and low-res on the 220) but at least I can count on a nearly flawless Linux experience.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-11-29
> **broeker said:**
> Thanks I did notice Bashtoni's earlier reply on this. 

Yes, I'm running the updated BIOS (207) and also did a full apt-get upgrade and everything else I can think of. Not sure about Bashtoni but I am on the i7/256M model running 32-bit Ubuntu. 

Thanks for the help -- really hoping to solve the problems on this machine and right now the temp/fan tops my list for sure.

ETA -- I have tried three different kernels - same symptoms on 3.0 and 3.1 while 3.2 boots into a dead screen.

Most of us (I think) are running the 64bit kernel.

I've got the i7/128G UX31E running the 3.1 kernel.. sensors shows:

```
$ sensors
acpitz-virtual-0
Adapter: Virtual device
temp1:        +61.0°C  (crit = +103.0°C)

asus-isa-0000
Adapter: ISA adapter
temp1:        +61.0°C 
```

I think my fan is running but very very quietly... almost inaudible unless you stick your ear over the vent.

One thought is that your fan itself is actually faulty, or buzzing on something, or obstructed. So when it is on at any speed, it make the same loud noise. If it is obstructed, or rubbing, it might not spin at the right speed too, meaning your machine might run slightly hotter.

Is it possible for you to make the machine hotter under windows, and see if you hear gradual transitions in the fan's noise, or if you hear it suddenly become noisey?

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-01

As a status update, I've compiled a new 3.2 kernel from Linus' master tree with the problem commit reverted and booted with the RC6 feature.

With only Gnome Shell and a terminal with powertop running, with powersave script and 10% screen brightness, the Watt usage is around 4.7W(!). Estimated battery life almost 7 hours with 65% left.

Also, I read that plans are to enable "RC6 deepest" (aka RC7) which would enable additional powersavings.

The unexpected shutdown bug is most likely still present and I'm trying to get response regarding this on LKML, but my focus right now lies in getting the final 3.2 kernel to boot on our Zenbooks without reverting commits.

Regardless, those are some promising numbers!

### Post by j-lo on 2011-12-02
> **nomego said:**
> Oblong_Cheese: So it doesn't work without the script either? I don't have a USB3-device to test, sorry.


As a status update, I've compiled a new 3.2 kernel from Linus' master tree with the problem commit reverted and booted with the RC6 feature.

With only Gnome Shell and a terminal with powertop running, with powersave script and 10% screen brightness, the Watt usage is around 4.7W(!). Estimated battery life almost 7 hours with 65% left.

Also, I read that plans are to enable "RC6 deepest" (aka RC7) which would enable additional powersavings.

The unexpected shutdown bug is most likely still present and I'm trying to get response regarding this on LKML, but my focus right now lies in getting the final 3.2 kernel to boot on our Zenbooks without reverting commits.

Regardless, those are some promising numbers!

According to the intel-gfx list, the problem with rc6 seems to be related to VTd support, aka. virtualization. Adding "intel_iommu=off" to kernel boot line disables VTd, and should make rc6 stable, according to the reports.

### Post by Wulf_de on 2011-12-02
3.1.1 versus 3.2rc3-Kernel:
It's simply amazing! 

1)
My UX31e has a significant fall on power comsumption!
Before: 18W on idle, now roundabout 9W!

2)
Core temperature decreased by ~10°C on Idle.
Now I seldom hear the cooling fan. On 3.1.1, the fan was rotating the whole time.
With a cpu eating process, 80°C is reached after a longer time.
After that process, the temperature falls rapidly.

Unfortunately, we have to wait for January for the stable release. You have to compile 3.2 on your own. Here is a useful howto:
[http://www.explodingpenguin.tv/2011/06/07/installcompile-linux-kernel-3-0-in-ubuntu/](http://www.explodingpenguin.tv/2011/06/07/installcompile-linux-kernel-3-0-in-ubuntu/)
(Please mind to use the source for 3.2 rc3, not 3.0)

Good luck or enough patience ;)

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-04
GodzillaMonster: Were you the one figuring out the suspend/resume fix?

I'm trying to post threads on all kernel-related issues on LKML, this is the current status:

1. Modeset issue with 3.2. Bad commit identified, first patch evaluated, exact computer specs sent to Keith P @ Intel since he wants to buy an identical machine for troubleshooting. Awaiting patches to try or information that he bought the machine and solved the issue :)

2. RC6 causing sudden shutdowns. Work is being done on fixing DMAR+RC6 by kernel devs, but booting with RC6 on and IOMMU off on the Zenbook still causes unexpected shutdowns, even if darfe reported less of them. (Although my machine shut down when I wrote this the first time.. sigh.)
Awaiting further information from devs.

3. XHCI runtime power management causing the port to disable (Charger+ still works). Reported today, awaiting information.

4. XHCI module needs to be unloaded/loaded on suspend/resume. Reported together with #3 in case they might be related.

5. Intel graphics needs DPMS force off/on after resume. Not sure where this should be fixed - kernel, X or Ubuntu?

6. Sentelic touchpad. Reuben Bond has begun work but is waiting for information from Sentelic on multi-touch related documentation.

Solved issues:

1. EHCI power savings preventing shutdown - should be fixed in newest 3.0/3.1/3.2 kernels.


Are there any other issues that we should report upstream to have them fixed "properly" without workaround scripts?

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-12-04
> **nomego said:**
> GodzillaMonster: Were you the one figuring out the suspend/resume fix?

<snip>

Are there any other issues that we should report upstream to have them fixed "properly" without workaround scripts?

Not entirely me. I was working with a couple of others to get the very basic parts working.

The underlying issue we had was the USB3 modules were not being unloaded when suspending. This prevented suspend entirely. By manually disabling them on suspend the machine suspended properly.

We also figured that acpi_oci="Linux" was required.

After this, the video didn't resume properly after suspend for anybody. The solutions for this is a little fuzzy. I'm not 100% sure why, but for me this was fixed (apparently) purely by updating to the 3.1 kernel. For others, the manual kick of X on resume was required.

I've left my own investigations there for the moment since I've got a stable machine that suspends and resumes.

Happy to build some newer kernels and validate some of the work you are doing, but I need my workload to ease off a bit first.

EDIT: Just to be clear, my video resumes 100% successfully without the dpms force in the suspend scripts. I think I am in a minority in this regard.

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-05
> This doesn't signify any problem with Ubuntu or with the Linux kernel. What the message indicates is that your BIOS is querying the operating system to see whether or not Linux is running, and Linux is ignoring this query. The reason for this is that often a BIOS will offer a limited feature set to the operating system when it responds that it is Linux. Linux generally supports all the same features as Windows, so Linux developers consider this query to be a bug in the firmware and instead report that the operating system is Windows so that the full set of features for the machine will be available. The message can be safely ignored.

It would be interesting to know what might change with that option, but I think I might rather continue without letting the BIOS know I run Linux :)

I'm going to do some more experimenting with SUSPEND_MODULES, it might behave differently with 3.2. I have removed the xset dpms stuff and resume works perfectly, I'll update the wiki but we should figure out why it works and not..

### Post by Oblong_Cheese on 2011-12-05
Hi all,

Has anyone managed to get two external monitors working with this laptop?

The Intel HD 3000 graphics chipset does support dual independent displays but I am not sure if that is with the caveat that the internal display is disabled as well.

When trying to set up two external displays an error about unable to set CRTCs is given. The command "xrandr -q --verbose | grep CRTCs" shows that CRTCs 0 and 1 are available to the systems four physical displays (internal, VGA, HDMI, LVDS). Obviously two goes into four only twice so it makes sense that only two physical displays can be operated simultaneously.

That lead me to try disabling the internal display while enabling the two external displays. I then discovered there is currently a problem with the i915 driver in Linux where when you disable the internal display, the entire graphics adapter is also disabled and no display is possible on any screen.

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-06
dbodner: There are Ubuntu-compiled kernels at [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/), however, you will have to boot with i915.modeset=0 with those kernel and thus not get hardware acceleration. You could install one to test the touchpad in 2D, or clone Linus' git repo and revert the problematic commit, or wait until it's fixed.

Oblong_Cheese: I have not tried that but will get myself a DP-cable sooner or later, I did however experience:

```

[12246.453334] [drm:i915_hangcheck_elapsed] *ERROR* Hangcheck timer elapsed... GPU hung
[12246.453346] [drm] capturing error event; look for more information in /debug/dri/0/i915_error_state
[12246.457795] [drm:i915_wait_request] *ERROR* i915_wait_request returns -11 (awaiting 5810478 at 5810475, next 5810479)

```

on Ubuntu kernel 3.1.4 while watching a movie on HDMI.

But please report your issue to LKML if you think it's a bug.

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-07
Suor: What's unstable/laggy about 11.10? 32 or 64 bit? Any custom kernels and/or parameters?

Galdrapiu: To figure out how X interprets the different buttons, you can run the command "xev" which will tell you what known/unknown keysyms are pressed or when the mouse is moved. It does, however, not react to Fn+F9, so I'm not sure how that interrupt is handled.


Also, I've been making additional research for the suspend script.
Now with kernel 3.2 and the powersave script (although I tried the Fresco controller in auto and on), this is the only part of the suspend script I need:

```

#!/bin/bash
EHCI_BUS="0000:00:1d.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB bus off
        echo -n $EHCI_BUS > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB bus back on
        echo -n $EHCI_BUS > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook Sentelic touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        ;;
esac

```

So I guess the suspend-issues with xhci_hcd has been solved in 3.2.

Also, it would probably be enough to use SUSPEND_MODULES="ehci_hcd" - if ehci_hcd was compiled as a module. Default Ubuntu configuration is to compile it into the kernel and the modunload() function of pm-utils only cares about modules in /proc/modules, not unbinding devices from drivers.

### Post by iron4o on 2011-12-07
So I've made this 64bit Ubuntu 11.10 play very nicely with my UX31E. It consumes power in the realm of 6-9W, suspends and hibernates flawlessly while keeping the temperature between 46C and 50C. Because of that it is completely silent, too! The fan turns on only when I do something more than browsing and programming. Here's what I did:

 * Installed the 3.1.3 kernel from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.1.3-oneiric/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.1.3-oneiric/) with i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 boot parameter
 * Used this sleep.d script: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Suspend.2C_Hibernate.2C_Shut-down_and_Reboot](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Suspend.2C_Hibernate.2C_Shut-down_and_Reboot)
 * Slightly modified [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Power_Optimizations](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Power_Optimizations) - commented out `echo auto > /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.8/power/level` bit as I don't see such a device. Also uncommented `echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1d.0/power/control` line
 * Used the suspend/hibernate script from the above help page which you guys kindly wrote! Thx!
 * I set my cpufreq to 'powersave'. I just don't trust the 'ondemand' thing :) Make this script run after user login if you want to:
```
#!/bin/bash

for cpu in "0" "1" "2" "3" 
do  
    cpufreq-selector -g powersave -c $cpu
done

```
 * My backlingt brightness is in the range of 50-70%
 * And I am using gnome3 instead of unity. If you try something like xfce or gnome2 you would probably get more from your battery

That said there are few things that still bother me. The touchpad first - It is recognized as a PS/2 mouse. I've read around here that the Elantech touchpad works without any problems. Have I missed something? Does its multitouch work only in the 3.2 kernel? Isn't there a way to make it work in the 3.1 kernels?

Secondly there are these few lines in the dmesg at boot:
```

[    1.434455] [drm] Supports vblank timestamp caching Rev 1 (10.10.2010).
[    1.434456] [drm] Driver supports precise vblank timestamp query.
[    1.434479] vgaarb: device changed decodes: PCI:0000:00:02.0,olddecodes=io+mem,decodes=io+mem:owns=io+mem
[    1.437795] [drm:intel_dp_i2c_aux_ch] *ERROR* too many retries, giving up
[    1.440777] [drm:intel_dp_i2c_aux_ch] *ERROR* too many retries, giving up

```
Any idea why? Is it happening to anyone else? I've googled around a bit but no luck so far.

And the last one - I see in powertop `Radio device: asus-nb-wmi` keeping to 100% usage. Is that normal?

UPDATE: I just experienced a sudden shutdown while using this configuration. Guess it is not so stable. Fortunately I don't see any graphic glitches. Many people report such in [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/818830](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/818830) after booting with i915 RC6 on.

Looking at the new release of intel graphics (new but older of what it's being currently used in the thread I suspect):

[http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html)

I found a kernel parameter thay may be worth trying:

> 
Semaphores are not enabled by default on all generation of graphics cards. Enablement of semaphores solves several stability issues on Ivy Bridge graphics cards, such as GPU hangs, and improved stability and performance on Sandy Bridge generation of graphics cards. The drawbacks are occasional stability issues on some systems with VTd enabled. Semaphores can be enabled manualy via the i915.semaphores=1 kernel parameter. 


Do anyone know if it's enabled by default in kernels newer than 3.1?

thanks!

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-08
ironmantis7x: If you always want to have powersave governor, regardless of being on AC or battery, you can just do this:

```

echo 'GOVERNOR="powersave"' | sudo tee /etc/default/cpufrequtils

```

That will set the governor to powersave when the computer starts up.
(To revert, just delete /etc/default/cpufrequtils and reboot.)

Another approach is to use powersave governor while on battery and ondemand when on AC - I have updated the powersave script on the wiki to do just that. (I chose to use cpufreq-set instead of cpufreq-selector since the former is much leaner and part of cpufrequtils, and the latter is part of gnome-applets, links against 3-4 times as many libraries and times out for me after setting the governor.)

Also, anyone using the USB->Ethernet adapter, please update the wiki with the link to the new version if that works as well as the 4.1 version.

Also, everyone please try my suspend/resume script from [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11520724&postcount=241](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11520724&postcount=241) and report whether it works and what kernel version. For me, that works as well as the one on the wiki but reduces the time it takes to suspend and resume.

### Post by tabuvudu on 2011-12-09
i915.semaphores=1 + disabling VTd in BIOS may have cut it.  Full day without random shutdowns. UX21E with core i7 2677M here with Elantech touchpad.

My config:
1.  Ubuntu 11.10 64 bit
2.  Kernel 3.2 RC4 from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/")
3.  kernel config in /etc/default/grub: quiet splash pcie_aspm=force i915.modeset=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1   The machine froze a couple of times (not the sudden shutdown, but freeze requiring hard reboot).  I deleted the iommu option from the suggestions above and it seems stable after that.
4.  In the BIOS options disable the VTd.  I have an unmodified default BIOS, dont know the nuber though.  Can check if important.
5.  Scripts for sleep (without the hacky workaround) and pm.  Thanks for providing that.

I have been using the machine for he full day.  No problems after disabling iommu kernel options. Seems quite stable and now at last a usable machine.  

best regards

PS. I had tried default ubuntu kernel 3.0, 3.1 and Opensuse with default kernel 3.1.  Shutdowns happened on each configurations at least once.  3.2rc4 is much better in terms of multitouch support and power consumption.

---

### Post by Zapiens on 2011-12-10
> **nomego said:**
> Zapiens: So I take it you have an Elantech touchpad?

Yes you are right about that.

> **nomego said:**
> That patch you supplied is what you get if you run "git revert dc22ee6fc18ce0f15424e753e8473c306ece95c1".

My bad, now I see the commit in the log, and can thus revert it.

nomego: How the ... did you manage to get power consumption down to 4.7W?

Power consumption is strange. I check the power drain by:

```
cat /proc/acpi/battery/BAT0/state | grep rate
present rate:            9884 mW
```

The power consumption takes a while to stabilize it seems, went away and got some coffee, back again and it was down to 7200mW. Still far from 4700mW. This by running the power script. When I reduce brightness to 10% it gets down to 6200mW. Where do I get the last 1500mW?

By using <fn>F5 and setting backlit to lowest value I got below 6000mw for the first time:

```
cat /proc/acpi/battery/BAT0/state | grep rate
present rate:            5782 mW

```

Strange, pressing the keys <fn>F5 <fn>F6 does not affect the value of /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness

The sporadic shutdowns I have encountered in rc4 and the commits up to and including rc5. Shut downs so far has for me happened after 8-10 hours uptime, numerous suspend and wake up inbetween.

/Z

### Post by tabuvudu on 2011-12-11
My experiences concerning the Elantech touchpad:

1.  in Ubuntu default kernel 3.0 is only recognised as pointing device, no multitouch available.  This is also true for latest 3.0.14.  However both hard buttons work correctly.
2.  In series 3.1 kernels recognised as a touchpad device, multitouch available.  Right click button does not work, but can be emulated through two-finger tap.   Vertical and horizontal scroll work if enabled in configurations.  The best support can be available through KDE configurator, there for instance a three-finger tap can be configured as middle-button.
3.  In series 3.2 touchpad support is identical to 3.1 described above.

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-12
Zapiens: I get around 6W at its lowest when booting with:

```
i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1
```

However, when I add

```

i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force

```

it can go as low as 4.7W. This is on 3.2.

---

### Post by Zapiens on 2011-12-12
Thanks nomego, that did the trick:
```
# cat /proc/cmdline 
BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-3.2.0-rc5-ux31+ root=UUID=5c616756-6fd9-4462-9084-6b1f3823e3d5 ro quiet splash i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 vt.handoff=7
```
Now I am happy with the consumption :-):

```
# cat /proc/acpi/battery/BAT0/state | grep rate
present rate:            4599 mW

```

---

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-12
thcourbon: I'm in discussions with Keith P who actually have ordered a Zenbook for himself to take a look at that regression and an RC6 fix.
I've tried his first patch but it didn't fix it completely.

The resume issue is because of ehci_hcd needed to be unbound, the reply I got from LKML regarding this is a link to an Asus Laptop Suspend discussion at [http://thread.gmane.org/gmane.linux.kernel/1222803](http://thread.gmane.org/gmane.linux.kernel/1222803)

You're very welcome to continue pushing that issue, you can see the bare minimum needed a page or two back (without unloading xhci_hcd and running xset).

Also I've made a "Bugs and issues" section on the wiki with issues being worked on and solved, please update that section when you have anything to add!

### Post by iron4o on 2011-12-14
So I have compiled 3.2rc5 kernel (after reverting the problematic commit) and booted it with
```

i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force

```
and disabled VTd.

I have noticed few things: First - as before the Elantech touchpad would work kind-of-sort-of-from-time-to-time. Second, the machine looked very stable and I had no trouble using it 2-3 hours - it was cool, quiet etc, all the good stuff. The third thing I've noticed is that it became VERY unstable after I hooked up a VGA monitor. Sudden shutdowns after 5 to 10 minutes of usage. Every time the shutdown was preceded by a second of screen corruption.

So I guess the following should be removed from the wiki(as long as I haven't missed anything that is): 
```

No sudden shutdowns have yet been reported with these kernel parameters on kernel 3.2 with VTd turned off in BIOS.

```
from [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Kernel_parameters](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Kernel_parameters)

> **Bashtoni said:**
> I had to remove the lvds downclock or it crashed within a few minutes of boot.
Will certainly try that but I doubt it was the problem in my case.

### Post by TastyTravels on 2011-12-15
I just got my UX31 zenbook about 10 days ago. Out of the box, I am very impressed. I was able to setup Ubuntu 11.10 flawlessly so now I'm dual booting the Windows7 it came with and Ubuntu.

I read all the message boards and reviews I could for weeks before I bought this machine. I have not had ANY problems with my touchpad (other than I wish the buttons didn't also act like the rest of the pad, i.e. touching that area moves the cursor when I'm just trying to click). I do have the Elantech pad.

I use this machine mostly as a Ruby on Rails development machine. Adobe CS5 on the windows side, and RoR and everything else on the Ubuntu side.

The power management software on the windows side seems to be doing an incredibly great job. Windows reporting 9+hours, but when on my Ubuntu side I can only get a max of 4 hours. Disappointing, I guess I need to learn a lot more about power management settings in Ubuntu, although I did do all the settings recommended in the wiki.

I updated the bios to 209, but I wasn't having any problems before that.

All of that said, I do have to say there is one problem I wish could be fixed that would make this an awesome Ubuntu machine too... I do have the wake problems others have when opening the lid, I simply get a black screen. Did anybody figure this out yet? I read through the thread and saw many posts that claimed to fix it only to later retract their statement.

The machine is so fast, I don't mind shutting it down each time instead of 'sleep' by closing the lid. But it would be nice to simply close it for a second like when somebody is talking to you, or need to move it, and then be able to just open it again to resume work. Any actual success on this yet?

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-12-17
Hi all!

Great collaborative effort here.

Just pitching in with some observations:

Running 11.10 with the pre-built 3.2-r6 kernel mentioned just above, with only these boot parameters:

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_osi=\"Linux\" pcie_aspm=force"

I've no observable faults so far and have power usage of roughly 6W

I had 15W until I disabled the VTd option in the BIOS (currently v207).

The display driver seems faster than the 3.1 kernel I was using, but I've no figures to prove it.

---

### Post by mbernasocchi on 2011-12-17
changing GRUB DEFAULT to:
```
i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force
```
solved the external monitor problem.
Still no pad found

---

Anyway, am now running Ubuntu 11.10 with kernel 3.2.0-rc6 and can get as  low as 4.8W with gnome-shell. I have not run it for long, so I can not  say if it is stable or not.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-12-18
> **ocir30 said:**
> As previous posts, I also thank all those involved in this thread.

Reading the last posts, I've encouraged to try new kernel in Ubuntu 11.10 amd64, ~/kernelppa/mainline/v3.2-rc6-precise, BIOS 209 and the last parameters "i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force". 

I've seen as battery life prediction changed from 1:25h (kernel 3.0.13) to 3:25h. But, about 2 minutes suffer system crashes.
I'll keep waiting.

Hi Ocir30,

I wonder if you could try simpler boot options first, then build them up if things are stable? 

I'm running with 
```

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_osi=\"Linux\" pcie_aspm=force"
```

and things are very stable - I have BIOS 207, though I'm not sure that matters. 

I did turn off VTd in the BIOS since it appeared to improve battery usage.

Just for completeness sake, and to make sure you got all kernely things...

I grabbed the v3.4-rc6-precise kernel from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/) using the following shell script:

```

RELEASE=v3.2-rc6-precise
VERSION=3.2.0-030200rc6-generic_3.2.0-030200rc6.201112162235
HVERSION=3.2.0-030200rc6_3.2.0-030200rc6.201112162235

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/${RELEASE}/linux-headers-${HVERSION}_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-headers-${HVERSION}_all.deb

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/${RELEASE}/linux-headers-${VERSION}_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-headers-${VERSION}_amd64.deb

wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/${RELEASE}/linux-image-${VERSION}_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-image-${VERSION}_amd64.deb

```

---

### Post by Zapiens on 2011-12-18
> **Sichroteph said:**

Hmm did you install the 64 bit version rc6? Install this:

[http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.2-rc6-precise/linux-image-3.2.0-030200rc6-generic_3.2.0-030200rc6.201112162235_amd64.deb](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.2-rc6-precise/linux-image-3.2.0-030200rc6-generic_3.2.0-030200rc6.201112162235_amd64.deb)


And use:
> dag@vinasha:~/work/linux$ grep GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT  /etc/default/grub
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash i915.modeset=1 pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1"

If you still have problem, maybe we have different hardware or something is broken.

It runs a lot more stable now:

> dag@vinasha:~/work/linux$ uptime
 20:07:37 up 1 day, 18:37,  2 users,  load average: 0.14, 0.06, 0.06

### Post by tarnishednite on 2011-12-18
So after a few days I feel like I finally have most of this working.

I followed the guide at [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook)

First time around I installed 11.10 32bit - which I noticed had a quite a few problems straight out of the box. Biggest issue I had was not being able to view available wireless networks.

And before you say anything - I had the 32bit readily available.

After installing 64bit things were looking up. I made all of the necessary configuration changes then built kernel 3.2rc5 from the git repo after checking out ed4a51842a9d9e618d4f4c31349b15b974dba5df
I also applied the previously discussed bluetooth fixes before building the kernel.

After all this - the touchpad is working and my battery life estimates are much much better looking.

The only problem that I am having now is that I am unable to get the bluetooth to work. I have the option to turn on bluetooth, but the only thing that happens then is I have the option to turn it off. I don't have the ability to change visibility or add a device.

### Post by Magnetizer on 2011-12-19
Dear all,

thanks for the great work of sorting most things out. My girlfriend is thinking about buying a new laptop which has to be small, light, fast and working great. Besides, it should run Ubuntu nearly flawlessly. No multitouch is ok for the moment but freezes, shutdowns or no network connection are not.

She used to have Apple computers but is absolutely not a Apple fangirl...;-) She likes OSX better than Windows, but she likes Linux even more (and most definitely, so do I). So the primary OS will be Ubuntu and perhaps the other OS will survive or will be deleted in the end.

I am thinking about 2 alternatives:

1) Asus UX21 with i7 core and 128GB SSD
2) Macbook Air with an addtional 2 Gigs of RAM

which cost about the same and perform about the same. I know there are differences in quality of screen and camera (Mac +1) and processor (Asus +1) and so on but that is not what bothers me at the moment.

Personally, I would like her to go for the Asus (because I never installed Linux on a Mac) but I am asking myself how stable it is at the moment and what it will be when Ubuntu 12.04 arrives.

From this thread and 
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook)
compared to
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MacBookAir4-2](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MacBookAir4-2)

I get the impression that Ubuntu 11.10 will run flawlessly on the Mac (yes, it took some time as well) and that I should expect some trouble with the Asus. Should I really?? Is Ubuntu ready for prime time on the Asus or will it freeze/shutdown more than one a week?

Does anybody have experienced Ubuntu on Mac in comparison to Ubuntu on the Asus? 

Besides the stability, do I forget some vital aspects which I should take into consideration for the decision making?

It would be nice to get some ideas...and please do not start a discussion of which brand is better as I really do not care.

Thanks for your help.

With the last kernel (3.2-rc6) and the VTd instruction disabled in BIOS the only serious (to me) issue is the trackpad (I have an Elantech-geared model). the bottom part behave like all the pad, right-click and multi-touch gesture often misses and the movement are kind of sloppy. ### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-19
> **a_kaldenhoven said:**
> I'm still interested in more feedback with regard to the stability question. If everyone is positive about it, this will make my decision easy...;-)
I'm not yet sure about that. I have read all posts of this thread and I don't remember one that clearly states that the sudden shutdowns are gone completely - which is not surprising, as we don't know of any direct way to test whether the bug exists or not.

Personally, about 2 weeks ago I had only one sudden shutdown in three days with RC6 on kernel version 3.1 with VTd enabled. (Others seem to have the sudden shutdowns after a few minutes using this configuration...)
Then I disabled VTd (with the kernel parameter, not in the BIOS). According to reports, that reduces the sudden shutdowns, but doesn't solve it completely (don't know if that applies also if VTd is deactivated in BIOS). Myself, I got no more sudden shutdowns, but the UX31 isn't my primary machine, so I'm not using it that much.
Now I'm running on kernel 3.2-rc6 (with ASPM, RC6, semaphores, fbc, lvds_downclock), which is also stable so far.

I'm waiting for someone who says that with RC6 and all the other options enabled (and VTd disabled), he experienced no sudden shutdowns for at least two weeks - and before, with his typical usage and the RC6 option, it happened several times a day.
Or, which I don't hope, someone who reports that with all these options the crash happened anyway... :(

In the next few weeks I'll also get the chance to use the UX31 a lot more (also with external monitor via HDMI), hopefully I can report back with good or bad news. Or someone else can :)

Regards
Aaron

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-20
Regarding RC6 and stability, this is what I've gotten from Keith P:

> RC6 should be enabled by default now when VT-d is disabled. We've
discovered that RC6 and VT-d are mutually incompatible.

If you're forcing RC6 on, you should stop doing that and check to see if
VT-d is enabled in your BIOS. Disabling that in the BIOS, or forcing it
off on the kernel command line with intel_iommu=off should get RC6
working. Eric was running with RC6 enabled and not seeing any troubles.

I'm not sure how to verify that RC6 gets turned on if you boot without it, /sys/module/i915/parameters/i915_enable_rc6 will return -1. Although I guess if power usage when idle is around 6W it should be on..

I have still experienced crashes though, and yesterday I had freezes like someone else had reported as well.
AC or battery + wifi, doing a clean "repo sync" for the Android AOSP source without actively using the computer made it freeze three times in a row no matter what kernel options I passed. Could be some issue with the wifi-driver I guess..

---

### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-20
> **nomego said:**
> Regarding RC6 and stability, this is what I've gotten from Keith P:
[...]

I can confirm that RC6 gets now enabled by default if VT-d is disabled.

Test configuration:
Ubuntu 11.10 (64 bit), kernel 3.2-rc6, UX31, wireless active, no USB devices, display brightness at lowest setting, power usage in idle mode:

BIOS: VT-d on - 10.9W (no options)
BIOS: VT-d on - 10.6W (pcie_aspm=force)
BIOS: VT-d off - 5.6W (no options)
BIOS: VT-d off - 4.6W (pcie_aspm=force)
BIOS: VT-d off - 4.6W (pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_fbc=1 i915.lvds_downclock=1 i915.semaphores=1)

> **nomego said:**
> I have still experienced crashes though
With RC6 enabled and VT-d disabled? The crashes where the laptop powers down suddenly? How often does it happen?
Many questions, sorry, but I hope we can track it down.

Regards
Aaron

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-20
Additional information regarding our kernel parameter tweaks:

> 
> Would you recommend enabling semaphores or not?

They should be enabled now, unless you've disabled them.

> LVDS downclock? FBC?

You don't have an LVDS, so you can't really play with that, and FBC
should be disabled by default as it's not very useful


So I guess we're left with disabling VT-d in BIOS and playing with pcie_aspm=force or not.

I'm going to try 3.2-rc6 with VT-d disabled in BIOS and no special kernel options for a while and see if I can reprocude the crashes.

When booting with all options, I did get a temporary freeze that reported this:

```

[ 6585.457832] ata1.00: exception Emask 0x0 SAct 0x0 SErr 0x50000 action 0x6 frozen
[ 6585.457839] ata1: SError: { PHYRdyChg CommWake }
[ 6585.457843] ata1.00: failed command: READ DMA
[ 6585.457850] ata1.00: cmd c8/00:08:88:68:6f/00:00:00:00:00/ea tag 0 dma 4096 in
[ 6585.457851]          res 40/00:00:00:00:00/00:00:00:00:00/00 Emask 0x4 (timeout)
[ 6585.457855] ata1.00: status: { DRDY }
[ 6585.457860] ata1: hard resetting link
[ 6585.785681] ata1: SATA link up 6.0 Gbps (SStatus 133 SControl 300)

### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-20
I just want to summarize the kernel options with this post, I tried to get an overview myself.

**pcie_aspm=force**
ASPM is not enabled by default, at least not yet. As far as I know this is ongoing work. No problems caused by this option known.
See also: [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=linux_aspm_solution&num=1](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=linux_aspm_solution&num=1)

**i915.modeset=1**
Is enabled by default, therefore unnecessary. It was only required for older 3.2-rc kernels (rc5 and below, because of a regression which is now fixed).

**i915.i915_enable_rc6=1**
Is only enabled by default in the 3.2 release candidate kernels and only if VTd is disabled, but not in 3.1 or the final 3.2 kernel. Significant power usage improvements. Causes problems with VTd enabled (and, it seems, for some users also with VTd disabled).
See also:
- [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html)
- RC6 disabled in final 3.2 release: [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTAzNDg](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTAzNDg) or [http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commit;h=371de6e4e0042adf4f9b54c414154f57414ddd37](http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commit;h=371de6e4e0042adf4f9b54c414154f57414ddd37)

**i915.semaphores=1**
Is only enabled by default in the 3.2 release candidate kernels and only if VTd is disabled, but not in 3.1 or the final 3.2 release. Causes problems with VTd enabled.
See [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html) and [https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/23/247](https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/23/247) (semaphores disabled in final 3.2 release).

**i915.i915_enable_fbc=1**
Is enabled by default since kernel 3.1, according to [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html).

**i915.lvds_downclock=1**
Based on the quote above posted by nomego and the output of "xrandr", the Zenbook uses eDP (Embedded Display Port) instead of LVDS. So this option has no effect.

**intel_iommu=off**
Alternative to disabling VTd in the BIOS.

**acpi_oci="Linux"**
Only a cosmetical fix, because a line in dmesg (?) recommended it, but no positive or negative effect known.

So, the recommendation should probably be, if power usage is important:
- use kernel 3.1 or 3.2
- disable VTd in the BIOS
- use the kernel option "pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1" 
(The Elantech touchpad mouse buttons work under 3.1, that's one reason for the older kernel - and I think the only one.)

For stability, the default configuration of 3.1 or 3.2 kernel should be ok.

Regards
Aaron

Edit:
See also this blog post: [http://dodonov.net/blog/2011/12/20/holidays-news-from-intel-linux-graphics-land/](http://dodonov.net/blog/2011/12/20/holidays-news-from-intel-linux-graphics-land/)

---

> Hi,

Maybe a little off topic(Sorry for that). 

My computer wont start, when I turn it on by the power bottom the led in the power bottom and the caps lock start light up. But no screen light or picture.

I have just installed the v3.2-rc6 kernel from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com](http://kernel.ubuntu.com). And was happy to see that suspend was working, the day after I tried to start it again it won't start.

Hi Kim,

It is possible that is has locked up post suspend. You should be able to force it to fully power down by holding the power button for 5 or more seconds, I think. I haven't tried this myself but such behaviour is common.

### Post by Suor on 2011-12-22
If anyone interested I found a way to make WiFi fast:

```

echo "options ath9k nohwcrypt=1" >> /etc/modprobe.d/ath9k.conf

```
