### Post by ironmantis7x on 2011-12-22
> **aspettl said:**
> I just want to summarize the kernel options with this post, I tried to get an overview myself.

**pcie_aspm=force**
ASPM is not enabled by default, at least not yet. As far as I know this is ongoing work.
See also: [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=linux_aspm_solution&num=1](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=linux_aspm_solution&num=1)

**i915.modeset=1**
Is enabled by default, therefore unnecessary. It was only required for older 3.2-rc kernels (rc5 and below, because of a regression which is now fixed).

**i915.i915_enable_rc6=1**
Is enabled by default in the 3.2 kernels if VTd is disabled, but not in 3.1. Significant power usage improvements. Causes problems with VTd enabled.
See also: [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html)

**i915.semaphores=1**
Enabled by default in the 3.2 kernels (only if VTd is disabled). Not enabled in 3.1, causes problems together with VTd, see [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html).

**i915.i915_enable_fbc=1**
Is enabled by default since kernel 3.1, according to [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html).

**i915.lvds_downclock=1**
Based on the quote above posted by nomego and the output of "xrandr", the Zenbook uses eDP (Embedded Display Port) instead of LVDS. So this option has no effect.

**intel_iommu=off**
Alternative to disabling VTd in the BIOS.

**acpi_oci="Linux"**
Only a cosmetical fix, because a line in dmesg (?) recommended it, but no positive or negative effect known.

So, the recommendation should probably be:
- use kernel 3.2-rc6
- disable VTd in the BIOS
- use the kernel option "pcie_aspm=force"

The alternative:
- use kernel 3.1
- disable VTd in the BIOS
- use the kernel option "pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1" 
(The Elantech touchpad mouse buttons work under 3.1, that's one reason for the older kernel - and I think the only one.)

Regards
Aaron

Edit:
See also this blog post: [http://dodonov.net/blog/2011/12/20/holidays-news-from-intel-linux-graphics-land/](http://dodonov.net/blog/2011/12/20/holidays-news-from-intel-linux-graphics-land/)

### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-22
> **ironmantis7x said:**
> What battery life are you seeing with these options configured above?
Also which 3.1 kernel?  i am using 3.1.0-030100-generic...

ironmantis7x

I did some tests regarding power usage in idle mode, see [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11551282&postcount=331](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11551282&postcount=331). This was with 3.2-rc6 - but with ASPM, RC6 and semaphores, the power usage with 3.1.3 was the same, that is, the minimum was 4.6 W.

Because the battery has a capacity of 48 Wh, a mean power usage of e.g. 8W would correspond to 6 hours of battery lifetime. But I haven't done any tests to determine which workload / display brigtness etc. causes which (average) power usage.

Regards
Aaron

### Post by Aurélien Jacobs on 2011-12-23
First, thanks everybody for all the contributions ! It was very helpful to get my UX31 running nicely.

I wanted to give you back a little bit, so for those of you who make use of the USB Ethernet adapter, you will be pleased to learn that Linus git tree now has native support for it, thanks to a very simple patch of mine : [http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commitdiff;h=e8303a3b2196272c3eb994d0fd1a189a958a2bdd](http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commitdiff;h=e8303a3b2196272c3eb994d0fd1a189a958a2bdd)

Keep on sharing.
Aurel

### Post by pjstevns on 2011-12-24
> **mbernasocchi said:**
> I still can't use the pad, and gpointing-device-settings gives a segmentation fault.
any hints?
ciao

Does your ux31 carry the Elantech touchpad?
FYI:
I installed the 3.2-rc7 package.
Added the executable script in /etc/pm/sleep.d/ as documented in this thread.
Turned off VTd in the BIOS (206)
Added /etc/pm/config.d/unload_module as documented
Added pcie_aspm=force to the boot options, and reran update-grub

Now there's a touchpad tab in the system settings for mouse/touchpad, which allows me to select edge-scroll or two-finger scroll.
Suspend resume works great - at least while connected to main power.

Only thing missing is button-2 on the touchpad. Mmm.

Paul.

---

### Post by pjstevns on 2011-12-24
One other quirk is that drag-n-drop works weird. The left-button acts as a touchpad: only touch-drag on the button triggers a drag event. Not left-click and drag on the main touchpad area.

---

### Post by darfe on 2011-12-24
Hello.. I just got the two finger scroll working on my sentelic touchpad.

Thanks to [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic]("https://github.com/saaros/sentelic") and [https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic]("https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic")

Update:
I forgot to tell that these drivers does not work if you have disabled the "tap to click" option. 
[I] echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg 
 echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags[/I]

Update2:
Thanks to [https://github.com/wulczer/sentelic/tree/absolute-disable-on-pad-click-v2](https://github.com/wulczer/sentelic/tree/absolute-disable-on-pad-click-v2)
To disable tap to click with these drivers this can be used. 
echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags 

linus git 3.2-rc7 gnome 3 

Merry christmas!

---

### Post by seebach on 2011-12-24
Hi, I'm on 3.2 RC7 which absolutely has been the most usable version I've run.

But I don't have any drag and drop functions because the trackpad buttons don't work. The touchpad however still feels unresponsive and could be much more precise.

To sum up:

[LIST]
[*]No buttons
[*]Left and Right tab works out of the box
[*]2 finger scroll - 2 finger right tab
[*]Drag and drop enabled in gpointing-device-settings
[/LIST]

I am on a Kubuntu 11.10. It tells me that no touchpad is found on every reboot. In system settings->Input devices->Touchpad it says the same.

Thanks for all the good work done so far by a lot of people!
Best regards, 
Jeppe

I think the script to update the kernel is fine.

The Sentelic driver is C source code, not a script (technically these things are quite different). In order to use them, you must set up your machine to compile the whole linux kernel from source code (specifically Linux's GIT repository). Then copy the files to the source tree, then build a new kernel and install that kernel. This is trivial or non-trivial depending on who is doing it :)

---

### Post by darfe on 2011-12-25
Update on multitouch with sentelic pad.

Thanks to [https://github.com/wulczer/sentelic/tree/absolute-disable-on-pad-click-v2]("https://github.com/wulczer/sentelic/tree/absolute-disable-on-pad-click-v2")
To disable tap to click with these drivers this can be used.
echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags

### Post by nomego on 2011-12-26
Here's an alternate update kernel script that will figure out the latest version for current running kernel (3.1, 3.2) or the following (3.2, 3.3) for oneiric or precise on itself and download and install it for your architecture if it isn't installed already after you've confirmed that you want to install the release:

```


#!/bin/bash
BASE_URL="http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline"
KVC=`uname -r | head -c 3`
KVN=${KVC:0:2}$((${KVC:2:3}+1))
KTYPE=`dpkg --print-architecture`
LATEST_RELEASE=`wget -q -O - "${BASE_URL}/?C=M;O=A" | grep '\[DIR\]' | cut -f 7 -d '>' | cut -f 1 -d '/' | grep -E "v${KVC}|v${KVN}" | grep -E "oneiric|precise" | tail -n 1`
echo -n "Latest release is ${LATEST_RELEASE}, install? [y/N] "
read y
if [ x$y != "xy" ]; then
        echo "Exiting..."
        exit
fi
wget -q -O - "${BASE_URL}/${LATEST_RELEASE}" | grep tr | cut -f 7 -d '>' | grep ^linux | cut -f 1 -d '<' | grep -E "${KTYPE}|all" | while read deb; do
        RELVER=`echo $deb | cut -f 1 -d '_'`
        if [ `dpkg -s $RELVER | grep Status | grep "install ok" > /dev/null ; echo $?` -eq 0 ]; then
                echo "$RELVER is already installed"
        else
                echo "Installing $RELVER"
                wget "${BASE_URL}/${LATEST_RELEASE}/$deb" && sudo dpkg -i $deb
        fi
done


```

:)

### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-26
> **Suor said:**
> If anyone interested I found a way to make WiFi fast:

```

echo "options ath9k nohwcrypt=1" >> /etc/modprobe.d/ath9k.conf

```

Additionally to the nohwcrypt-option, I discovered that the background scanning for new wireless networks of network manager causes the network to stall frequently (for several seconds). This is very annoying, especially with SSH sessions.

Someone else had this problem, too, and just patched network manager:
[http://nilvec.com/disable-scanning-in-networkmanager-when-connected/](http://nilvec.com/disable-scanning-in-networkmanager-when-connected/)

I did the same and uploaded the package to a new PPA:
[https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook](https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook)
("sudo add-apt-repository ppa:aspettl/zenbook", if you want to use the patched version, too.)
This fixes the problem for me, at least now I can download big files with a constant speed. Furthermore, the disconnect/reconnect messages in dmesg and the router log file are gone.

The disadvantage is, of course, that switching between several wireless networks isn't that easy anymore, because no other networks will be visible if a connection is already established. But that's probably ok in most cases.

Regards
Aaron

This gets the source for the touchpad driver and saves it in a direcotry named sentelic.
```
git clone git://github.com/wulzer/sentelic.git
```
This gets the current linux kernel source and saves it in a directory called linux
```
git clone git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git
```
This changes to the directory where the source for the touchpad drivers is stored.
```
cd ~/linux/drivers/input/mouse
```
This overwrites the old source with the new one.
```
cp ~/sentelic/src/sentelic.c sentelic.c
```
```
cp ~/sentelic/src/sentelic.h sentelic.h
```
The rest is almost directly copied from [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild")
```
cd ~/linux
```
```
cp /boot/config-`uname -r` .config
```
```
yes '' | make oldconfig
```
```
sed -rie 's/echo "\+"/#echo "\+"/' scripts/setlocalversion
```
```
CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=`getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN` fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```
```
cd ..
```
Obviously you might want to change this if you have multiple .deb files in this directory
```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```
```
cd /lib/modules
```
```
ls
```
```
sudo update-initramfs -ck module-name-for-new-kernel
```
```
sudo update-grub
```
reboot

I tried to use the patches, but they didn't work well. Probably because they are designed for 3.2rc6.

As a note I noticed that i915_rc6 and i915.semaphores are disabled by default in 3.2rc7. If you want to take advantage of the powersaving features you must enable these features in /etc/default/grub.

### Post by darfe on 2011-12-27
> **shakabra said:**
> This gets the source for the touchpad driver and saves it in a direcotry named sentelic.
```
git clone git://github.com/wulzer/sentelic.git
```

I tried to use the patches, but they didn't work well. Probably because they are designed for 3.2rc6.

As a note I noticed that i915_rc6 and i915.semaphores are disabled by default in 3.2rc7. If you want to take advantage of the powersaving features you must enable these features in /etc/default/grub.

I use this branch with 3.2-rc7.
```
 https://github.com/wulczer/sentelic/tree/absolute-disable-on-pad-click-v2 
```

Remember to comment out the line thats disables absolute positioning in /etc/rc.local, /etc/pm/sleep.d/?
```

# Disable absolute positioning 
#echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg

# Disables tap to click
echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags 
```

And enable two finger scroll in the "new" pad tab in gnome mouse settings.

### Post by aspettl on 2011-12-29
RC6 will get disabled for 3.2, too, not only semaphores. See [http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commit;h=371de6e4e0042adf4f9b54c414154f57414ddd37](http://git.kernel.org/?p=linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git;a=commit;h=371de6e4e0042adf4f9b54c414154f57414ddd37) or [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTAzNDg](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTAzNDg).
So we're back to "pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1" again, at least if power usage is relevant. For stability, the decision of disabling RC6/semaphores is probably really the best one...
(I'll update my post about the kernel options on page 34.)

Just to note, I experienced one crash since I disabled VTd some weeks ago (and use RC6 and semaphores) - but it wasn't the sudden shutdown, the screen freezed and some seconds later the laptop powered off (or rebooted, I don't remember exactly). I was downloading something via WLAN and also copying some files to an USB drive, maybe it's related to that.

Edit: it was a ath9k issue, I have an error in the kernel log.

Regards
Aaron

[ 1798.261184] [Hardware Error]: Machine check events logged
```
mcelog (for analysing machine check events) shows messages like this:
```
CPU 1 THERMAL EVENT TSC 28ecf2e7c8a 
TIME 1325175754 Thu Dec 29 17:22:34 2011
Processor 1 below trip temperature. Throttling disabled
STATUS c000000088290c08 MCGSTATUS 0
MCGCAP c07 APICID 2 SOCKETID 0 
CPUID Vendor Intel Family 6 Model 42
Hardware event. This is not a software error.
```
But in my opinion, these are not critical - at least not in most cases, I get them often enough and I found similar reports with Google from other Sandy Bridge users - probably it has something to to with Turbo Boost.
See also [http://us.generation-nt.com/answer/mce-package-power-limit-notification-help-205585301.html](http://us.generation-nt.com/answer/mce-package-power-limit-notification-help-205585301.html).

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-12-29
> **jasric89 said:**
> Hi I have been trying to put a power script in from [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook) and it doesn't seem to work. I get no such file or directory found when trying to put a power script in etc/pm/power.d. I need a for dummies guide or something all Ive managed to do is update my kernel with step by step instructions. Is there any where I can go to get them? The wiki doesnt really help with novice users. I just want my zenbook to work. :(. 

Thank you in advance 

JC.

I wouldn't worry too much about the power script in /etc/pm/power.d if you are running 3.2rc7.

You should just ensure that /etc/default/grub has

```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash pcie_aspm=force"

```

in it, and then run

```
sudo update-grub
```

The wiki's script for /etc/pm/sleep.d might also be required - I've not removed mine since upgrading the kernel, and I'm not sure if it is required to make the sleep/resume work anymore.

Your zenbook should be working after these steps.. it is probably working now, so perhaps you could clarify what is not working?

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2011-12-29
> **jasric89 said:**
> Ok on version 3.2rc7 the resume doesn't seem to work. So If I press on suspend it just goes to a black screen with with a flashing cursor. I have to switch the laptop off, for it to work again. Understood. You will need to put the suspend-resume script into /etc/pm/sleep.d

My script is called 20_zenbook and contains:
```
#!/bin/sh

EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"

case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus | tee /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        done
        # Disable touch-to-tap on Zenbook touchpad
        echo -n 0x90 0x80 > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/setreg
        echo -n c > /sys/devices/platform/i8042/serio4/flags
        ;;
esac

```

This will fix your resume issue.

Battery life should be fixed if you turn off VTd in the BIOS, as mentioned on the wiki and in this thread.

#!/bin/sh

case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)

esac

```

This will fix your resume issue.

### Post by SirJuan on 2011-12-30
> **jasric89 said:**
> That's greats its just I'm totally new to Linux. I can get as far as nano etc/pm/sleep.d but when it trys to right it in there it says no file or directory and I dont know how to get past that bit. Also how do you get the script to run? 


Try sudo nano /etc/pm/sleep.d instead of nano etc/sleep.d. Sudo ensures that you have write permissions to a system directory and slash at the front helps you find the right directory even if you aren't located in the root (/) directory.

The script should be executed automatically on suspend. Mine has execute permissions so you may need to run:

```
sudo chmod 755 /etc/pm/sleep.d/20_zenbook
```


Perhaps also a couple of sanity checks.. can you execute

```
uname -a
```

and

```
ls -al /etc/pm/sleep.d
```

and post the results?

### Post by Aurélien Jacobs on 2011-12-31
For those of you with the Elantech touchpad using 3.2 kernel, there is a very simple solution to the very low sensitivity problem. You can just use synclient to setup the sensibility you want (and many other things).

Here is the script I personally use to setup the touchpad to my preference.

```

#!/bin/sh

# higher sensitivity
synclient FingerLow=9 FingerHigh=12

# faster movements
synclient MinSpeed=2.0 MaxSpeed=4.0 AccelFactor=0.1

# 2 fingers scroll
synclient VertTwoFingerScroll=1 HorizTwoFingerScroll=1

# faster coasting
synclient CoastingSpeed=10 CoastingFriction=25

# enable tap to click (2 fingers for middle click, 3 fingers for right click)
synclient TapButton1=1 TapButton2=3 TapButton3=2

# faster tap and double tap
synclient FastTaps=1 MaxDoubleTapTime=100

# continue dragging movement when reaching the edge of the touchpad
synclient EdgeMotionMinZ=30 EdgeMotionMaxZ=40 EdgeMotionMinSpeed=100 EdgeMotionMaxSpeed=400

```

Only the first line is required to increase sensitivity. Other lines make the touchpad much more useful to me, but may not fit your usage. I highly recommend to spend some time playing with synclient to find a setup that you really like (see 'man synaptics', there is a whole bunch of nice features).

The only problem left with the Elantech touchpad is the physical button handling. From what I understand, there is only one physical button, and the kernel driver properly report it. But the synaptics X.org input module doesn't support converting this in different button depending on the position detected on the tactile surface. It also doesn't support dragging with one finger while holding the left button because it the finger holding the button is also touching the surface, and thus, the finger dragging is considered as a second finger touching the surface. Moving 2 fingers on the surface doesn't allow dragging.

I don't know if it is worth trying to improve the X.org synaptics input module now that a real multitouch input module is part of the upcoming X.org 1.12. This sounds much more promising, and I can live with only tap to click for now.

Happy new year !

---

### Post by Zapiens on 2012-01-01
A full day of reading and surfing over wlan on the UX31, so far 9 hours and 5 minutes and still one hour left on the battery :-) Note flash plugins blocked in the browser.

I have made the following change to the power script:

```

# set all power control to auto
for i in $(find /sys/devices/pci0000\:00/ -name control); do
    echo auto > $i
done

## SATA link power management
for i in $(find /sys/devices/pci0000\:00/ -name link_power_management_policy); do
    echo min_power > $i
done

# Intel hda sound, go to power save after 10 sec idle
echo 10 > /sys/module/snd_hda_intel/parameters/power_save

```

Ubuntu as set the power_save of module snd_hda_intel to zero which inhibits power save.

I changed how thins were set as my bus numbering is not the same as in the original script. I also saw some devices which were on.

There are a couple of things left, when coming out of suspend the bind leaves the usb devices in on, i.e. no power saving.

When power on and on battery the cpu govenor is ondemand.

/Z

# if [ x$LOCK_SCREEN = xtrue ]; then
#       . /usr/share/acpi-support/screenblank
# fi
```

### Post by Aurélien Jacobs on 2012-01-01
> **jasric89 said:**
> Hi Aurélien Jacobs where about's should you put this script? For the novice people among us IE me :).

First you can save it in your home directory, let say, in a file called touchpad_settings.sh

Make sure this script is executable with : ```
chmod +x touchpad_settings.sh
```Then simply try to run the script with ```
./touchpad_settings.sh
```If this works fine, then you can try to make this permanent, but this may depend on your environment.
With gnome-shell, it needs to run after gnome did his own setup of the touchpad (including after waking-up from suspend). This can be done this way (replace "aurel" with your own login) :
```

gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.peripherals.input-devices hotplug-command /home/aurel/touchpad_settings.sh

```

For me, pm-suspend from command-line works out-of the box, wihout having to add a script in /etc/pm/sleep.d or variables in /etc/pm/config.d. 

However, I do have an issue with suspending when I press the  "Z2" (Fn+F1) hotkey. The behavior of the hotkey out-of-the-box is like descripbed in your wiki: I get a blank screen upon resume. However, adding the script in sleep.d does not change anything in my case. On the other hand, this bug can be fixed by commenting-out 3 lines in /etc/acpi/sleep.sh:
```
# if [ x$LOCK_SCREEN = xtrue ]; then
#       . /usr/share/acpi-support/screenblank
# fi
```

Which version of Debian? What value does DISPLAY_DPMS have (set in /etc/default/acpi-support)? Does it work if you instead of pressing the key just close the lid? Does it work while in a GUI (Gnome, KDE, ...)?

As I wrote above, suspend to RAM works for me with only a very simple tweak, and a kernel 3.2.0-rc7. Suspending from command line works out of the box. Suspending with the power button works if I comment out the part of  /etc/acpi/sleep.sh which blanks the screen prior to sleeping.

> **debianguy said:**
> Which version of Debian? What value does DISPLAY_DPMS have (set in /etc/default/acpi-support)? Does it work if you instead of pressing the key just close the lid? Does it work while in a GUI (Gnome, KDE, ...)?

I am using Debian testing (=Wheezy). this I wrote above.

My /etc/default/acpi has:```
DISPLAY_DPMS=xset
```When I close the lid, something happens, though I'm not sure this something is exactly suspend to ram... The screen goes blank and the power button is lit down (not blinking lke during suspend to ram, just lit down). Then, when I re-open the lid, screen goes back up almost instantly (a little too quick, therefore I suspect it'snot truely suspended). I get no warning or info message logged by the kernel.

When I'm logged into KDE, it works the same as far as I can say.

### Post by ideogon on 2012-01-04
Kudos to all the selfless people providing insight on how to fix the power, touchpad, and other issues. I now have a pleasantly usable machine, except that I have one last annoyance.

When I resume from suspend, the display never retains its brightness settings. I'm pretty sensitive to light, so it's annoying that the display goes back to very bright after I had set it to its lowest setting. Is there something I can add to one of the sleep.d scripts to adjust this?

Also, it would be nice if brightness had smaller increment levels and if I could make my display even a little bit dimmer. Any ideas?

---

### Post by mbernasocchi on 2012-01-04
> **ideogon said:**
> 
When I resume from suspend, the display never retains its brightness settings. I'm pretty sensitive to light, so it's annoying that the display goes back to very bright after I had set it to its lowest setting. Is there something I can add to one of the sleep.d scripts to adjust this?

From the power.d script:
```
# 50% screen brightness on battery
echo 5 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness
```
You should be able to integrate it somehow into sleep.d
ciao

---

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-05
Okay do this...

```
mkdir ~/kernel 
```

Here is the link is the link to the kernels I compiled for the UX31 with Sentelic touchpad...

[wurkwith.us/kernel]("wurkwith.us/kernel")

Download both of those files to the new directory ~/kernel. Then do this...

```
cd ~/kernel 
```
```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```
```
cd /lib/modules
```
```
ls
```
Look for the name of the new kernel.
```
sudo update-initramfs -ck name-of-new-kernel
```

I built this kernel using the guide found [here]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild"). I changed the /driver/input/mouse/sentelic.c and the /driver/input/mouse/sentelic.h files to match the work done [here]("https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic").

**None of this work is my own**. The only other thing I changed is the "compile kernel with debugging" in order to make a smaller kernel. 

With this kernel I just use; the RC6 boot parameters, the suspend script with only EHCI unloading (no XHCI or DPMS tricks) and the powersave script previously mentioned. I haven't crashed yet, but I'm pretty sure I will (Don't think RC6 is fixed). If you have problems then just follow the guide I linked, and clone the sentelic.c and sentelic.h files to proper place before compiling.

---

**None of this work is my own**. The only other thing I changed is the "compile kernel with debugging" in order to make a smaller kernel. 

With this kernel I just use; the RC6 boot parameters, the suspend script with only EHCI unloading (no XHCI or DPMS tricks) and the powersave script previously mentioned. I haven't crashed yet, but I'm pretty sure I will (Don't think RC6 is fixed). ### Post by maze2 on 2012-01-06
I could have mentioned that to you but the reason why it works in KDE is because I disactivated "Power Management" service in System settings --> Startup and Shutdown --> Service manager.

Since powerdevil pretends to handle userspace power mangement, it turns off acpi-support when it starts.

I hate that because power management is not the job of a DE, and you get to work two times at configuring it, and you never get consistent behavior between console and X. But when you disactivate powerdevil, it plays nice and you get acpi-support working when KDE is started.

Regarding the lid behavior I described, it was simply the result of a setting in /etc/default/acpi-support.

---

### Post by hrod on 2012-01-07
Hi,

First, I want to thank you for the help page and this very helpful and generous thread !

I follow the instructions on these pages, but I've some questions.

What I did : 1) I install the sleep script 2) the powersave script 3) disable VTd 4) add just pcie_aspm to the grub file 4) upgrade my kernel to the 3.1.7 version.
I didn't choose the 3.2, because of my elantech touchpad. I need to use the two fingers way to select text, and I prefer to clicking than tapping. I tried the Aurélien's script with the 3.2 kernel, which is really great (exactly what a touchpad should do), but I can't use anymore the two fingers click, etc.

My questions :

1) Is it possible to enable synclient, or to recompile the module in the 3.1.7 version, to use the touchpad with synaptics ? For example, to scroll up/down with two fingers, etc (like in Aurélien's script).

2) How can I disable the tapping method ? I prefer to click... 

3) I've more or less 4 hours of autonomy with my battery, and I want to extend it a little bit ? Can I do that with a 3.1.7 kernel ? I found a lot of informations, but I don't know which is the best way to do it. It would be perfect to have 5h-7h autonomy. 

4) Can I also increase the power of my wireless card ? It seems to be not really powerful.

5) The fan works all the time ! It's noisy, and not really normal. How can I fix that ? It didn't work all the time with windows, and I tried other distros like gentoo or crunchbang, and the fan worked quite normally. I install ubuntu several times and the problem is always the same.

6) I've the impression that I can see pixels on my screen... that the resolution isn't really smooth... Perhaps it's just an impression, or I should use a driver for the Intel card or configure the screen.

Thanks in advance !

### Post by MarcusMoeller on 2012-01-08
@Aurélien Jacobs Thanks, that really solved the tap-to-click problem.

I have just set:

synclient MinSpeed=1.5 MaxSpeed=3.0 AccelFactor=0.1

to make it a bit slower.

@hrod you should be able to set synclient TapButtonX=0 to disable tap-to-click where X is the number of fingers you tap with (1-3)

### Post by hrod on 2012-01-08

### Post by noreiller on 2012-01-09
Hi, thanks for all the work you have done.

I have an Elantech touchpad, BIOS version 207 and the modifications I made are : 
- use of the power.d and sleep.d scripts
- kernel [FONT=monospace][/FONT]version 3.2.0-030200rc7-generic_3.2.0-030200rc7.201112240135
- GRUB options : "i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force acpi_oci='Linux' quiet splash"

It made the multitouch working : 3 fingers for right click and 2 fingers scroll (vertically and horizontally). I have some reboots occuring, but I really can't anticipate them... 
My battery life is around 5 hours.

BUT, since I upgraded to BIOS v210 and after 1 or 2 minutes, when I use firefox, it freezes and the fans are used more and more... the computer becomes very hot and I'm obliged to power off by pushing several seconds the power button.
When booting on kernel 3.0.0-14, I have not this problem.

Does someone have the same problem and/or a fix ?

### Post by techfreak on 2012-01-09
GodzillaMonster:

When you flash the new BIOS it reset all the setting back to default and turns on the VT-d back to enabled. Go back into BIOS and disable VT-d all will be back to normal.

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-09
> **hrod said:**
> 

Don't you hear this little noise from the battery (without AC), like the light noise of a mosquito ? It changes with the brightness of the screen, and seems to disappear with the AC.
It's a little bit annoying...

It sounds like your power adapter may be shorting out. The noise is most likely the sound of an electrical arc. The screen is changing brightness because you are going from A/C to battery power rhythmically with the short. Maybe?

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-10
hrod,
If it is shorting out it's probably near one end of the chord. One way to see an intermittant short like this is to put the computer in a vary dark place and look at the connection points for any tiny sparks. 

You shouldn't be too worried. Asus will replace the adapter for you. However if you have to pay shipping it will probably be cheaper to buy another yourself.

### Post by techfreak on 2012-01-12
So far with the following setup on my UX31:

11.10 install 
3.2rc7 kernel updated (from ubuntu mainline)
running power savings script from the wiki
running sleep/suspend script from the wiki
added touchpad script (Elantech) to get sensitivity and two-finger scrolling to work [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11577569&postcount=428]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11577569&postcount=428")
added to /etc/default/grub -> GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force"
Changed the network manager to correct the repeated de-auths [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11581770&postcount=443]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11581770&postcount=443")

[LIST]
[*]now I get better battery 5+ hours
[*]No reboots or freezes
[*]Suspend works
[*]Heat from the ultrabook is moderate when under load
[*]Touchpad works for the most part (still issues with left/right buttons)
[*]Have yet to test the usb/ethernet or bluetooth (not deal breakers for me at this point)
[/LIST]

If anyone else has better functionality I would like to discuss any differences in setups to improve the OTB experience. For the most part I'm satisfied for now.

### Post by techfreak on 2012-01-13
> **GodzillaMonster said:**

So my latest changes to improve the UX31 under 11.10

[LIST]
[*]upgraded to kernel 3.2.1
[*]eliminated the touchpad script (tweaked the settings via gpointing-device-settings
[*]kept the same grub command line entries GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force"
[*]kept the default network-manager but added echo "options ath9k nohwcrypt=1" >> /etc/modprobe.d/ath9k.conf
[/LIST]

My results:

[LIST]
[*]Elantech touch pad works for the most part (no right click, ok two finger scrolling, ok sensitivity once tweaked.
[*]USB-Ethernet working ok
[*]Wifi stable ... so far
[*]Bluetooth not working - can't see devices and not visible to others :(
[*]suspend works with script (closing lid and Fn+f1)
[*]power savings is looking to be about 5+ hours (using script from wiki)
[*]temperature is on par 47.0C
[/LIST]

Thank you to all those that have put time and effort in finding the solutions to make this a better experience.

### Post by fast-rs on 2012-01-13
To all Sentelic touchpad users, here is a small workaround for the missing third mouse button. I often use the button for resizing windows or drop down marked text somewhere. So I really need it often. The workaround is to use the nearly needless menu button from the keyboard for it (between Alt Gr and Ctrl).

First you need to install xkbset for it:
```
sudo apt-get install xkbset
```The nice tool named xev (from the package x11-utils) told me, that the button has the keycode 135 for my DE keyboard. Test this out for your layout or for choosing a different key you do not need (pause for example).

Then create a script file which you make executable and put in the autostart. Fill it with this lines:
```
#!/bin/sh
xkbset m
xmodmap -e "keycode 135 = Pointer_Button2"
```Mouse button 2 is the middle one, button 3 the right one!

Hope this is usefull. Bye!

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-14
> **sircas said:**
> I obtained the same result and a further error:
 When I turn on the computer with external monitor plugged in to use extended desktop mode, the laptop screen goes black and I can not see the desktop. The mouse moves to the desktop with no problem and launch applications. When I check the usefulness of displays, shows that it is active in 1600x ... even turn it off and on without any improvement.
any ideas?

I attach the dmesg and Xorg log.

Sorry my english...

Hallo Sircas,

When you turn on the zenbook with the external monitor already plugged in, then the laptop display will be blank and the external monitor will display the BIOS splash-screen and become the main display... I think this is by design.

I don't think X11 can cope with this very well.

The current solution is to always plug the external monitor in AFTER your machine has started.

---

### Post by tjajab on 2012-01-14
A tip for those with an Elantech touchpad who are using the excellent script by Aurélien Jacobs. Besides adding it to "Startup applications" it could be nice to have it running on resume. A working solution to that it to use a script in /etc/pm/sleep.d. If you already have the USB loading/unloading script you can enter the following in "resume|thaw":
```

export DISPLAY=:0
su -c - <yourusername> <path/to/script>

```

Thanks to Kremlin in this thread: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10663041](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10663041)

Update: It could be necessary to have a short delay in the mouse-fixing-script to get it more robust, e.g.
```

sleep 1

```
in the beginning.

### Post by MilkeySUFC on 2012-01-14
Hi and a very big thank you to all who have helped us get our UX31's working better in Linux.

I have one annoyance though, whenever I restart the brightness is always 100% and I have to reduce it with <fn>-<F5>.

After restart System Settings shows display brightness at full, even if I set it to minimum.

Does anyone have an idea how to fix it?

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-14
> **MilkeySUFC said:**
> Hi and a very big thank you to all who have helped us get our UX31's working better in Linux.

I have one annoyance though, whenever I restart the brightness is always 100% and I have to reduce it with <fn>-<F5>.

After restart System Settings shows display brightness at full, even if I set it to minimum.

Does anyone have an idea how to fix it?

This is mentioned in this thread and on the wiki page for the Zenbook.

You can change the brightness to a particular level by putting something like the following in a startup or login type script:

```

# 50% screen brightness on battery
echo 5 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness
```

---

### Post by MilkeySUFC on 2012-01-14
> **GodzillaMonster said:**
> This is mentioned in this thread and on the wiki page for the Zenbook.

You can change the brightness to a particular level by putting something like the following in a startup or login type script:

```

# 50% screen brightness on battery
echo 5 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness
```

Hi GodzillaMonster,

Thanks for your help.

I already have the power saving script from the wiki with that line in it. Saved in /etc/pm/power.d/zenbook-powersave

Doesn't change my backlight on reboot, whatever value I put after the 'echo' (I've tried all values 1 to 9).

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-14
> **MilkeySUFC said:**
> Hi GodzillaMonster,

Thanks for your help.

I already have the power saving script from the wiki with that line in it. Saved in /etc/pm/power.d/zenbook-powersave

Doesn't change my backlight on reboot, whatever value I put after the 'echo' (I've tried all values 1 to 9).

No it won't.. the suspend script is only executed when starting and leaving suspend mode.

You will need to put it in a script that is run on startup or when you log in, such as /etc/rc.local

---

### Post by MilkeySUFC on 2012-01-14
> **GodzillaMonster said:**
> No it won't.. the suspend script is only executed when starting and leaving suspend mode.

You will need to put it in a script that is run on startup or when you log in, such as /etc/rc.local

It's not the suspend script, it's the power optimisation script, in /etc/pm/power.d not /etc/pm/sleep.d, I was under the impression that if it was in /etc/pm/power.d it would run at boot.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-14
> **MilkeySUFC said:**
> It's not the suspend script, it's the power optimisation script, in /etc/pm/power.d not /etc/pm/sleep.d, I was under the impression that if it was in /etc/pm/power.d it would run at boot.

Sorry.. I misread that.

Whereabouts in the script did you put it? 

Is it possible your power script has an error and therefore is not completing to the line you put the echo on? 

Anyhow, putting it in /etc/rc.local should still work.

### Post by techfreak on 2012-01-14
So with kernel 3.2.1 I'm getting hard shut downs. Nothing in the logs that gives me any hints. All on battery and the bottom of the notebook feels warm to touch.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-15
> **techfreak said:**
> So with kernel 3.2.1 I'm getting hard shut downs. Nothing in the logs that gives me any hints. All on battery and the bottom of the notebook feels warm to touch.

Hmm.. makes me a little nervous to unplug the AC..

I upgraded to 3.2.1 and noticed that with my original kernel parameters...
```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash pcie_aspm=force"
```
... the screen would no longer power off after 5 minutes of being idle. That is, the screen would go black, but the display was still obviously on.

I added the rc6 parameters as follows...
```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1"
```
...and the screen powers off properly. 

Okay.. now to unplug the AC and wait for a shutdown :S

---

And thanks GzM again for your help.

Milkey

### Post by maze2 on 2012-01-15
This version of /etc/acpi/asus-touchpad.sh works for me with an UX21E and a Sentelic touchpad:

```
#!/bin/sh

set -e

pff=/usr/share/acpi-support/power-funcs
[ -f $pff ] || exit 0

atp_error() {
        logger -t${0##*/} -perr -- $*
        exit 1
}

. $pff || atp_error "Sourcing $pff failed"

[ -x /usr/bin/xinput ] || atp_error "Please install package xinput to enable toggling of touchpad devices."

# if this is the right behavior, then this should be moved out of acpi-support
# to hal (or whatever is replacing hal for such events)
# getXconsole

# XXX:  according to man page 'list', 'list-props' and 'set-int-prop' are
#       options, not arguments.
XINPUTNUM=$(xinput --list | sed -rne 's#^.*Sentelic FingerSensingPad[[:blank:]]+id=([0-9]+)[[:blank:]]+.*$#\1#p')
[ "$XINPUTNUM" ] || atp_error "Invalid Synaptics TouchPad id '$XINPUTNUM'"

soff="Device Enabled"

# get the current state of the touchpad
TPSTATUS=`xinput --list-props $XINPUTNUM | awk "/$soff/"'{ print $NF }'`
case $TPSTATUS in
        [!01])
            #!/bin/sh

set -e

pff=/usr/share/acpi-support/power-funcs
[ -f $pff ] || exit 0

atp_error() {
        logger -t${0##*/} -perr -- $*
        exit 1
}

. $pff || atp_error "Sourcing $pff failed"

[ -x /usr/bin/xinput ] || atp_error "Please install package xinput to enable toggling of touchpad devices."

# if this is the right behavior, then this should be moved out of acpi-support
# to hal (or whatever is replacing hal for such events)
# getXconsole

# XXX:  according to man page 'list', 'list-props' and 'set-int-prop' are
#       options, not arguments.
XINPUTNUM=$(xinput --list | sed -rne 's#^.*Sentelic FingerSensingPad[[:blank:]]+id=([0-9]+)[[:blank:]]+.*$#\1#p')
[ "$XINPUTNUM" ] || atp_error "Invalid Synaptics TouchPad id '$XINPUTNUM'"

soff="Device Enabled"

# get the current state of the touchpad
TPSTATUS=`xinput --list-props $XINPUTNUM | awk "/$soff/"'{ print $NF }'`
case $TPSTATUS in
        [!01])
                atp_error "Invalid Synaptics TouchPad status '$TPSTATUS'"
                ;;
esac

# XXX: '--set-int-prop' deprecated
xcmd="xinput --set-prop $XINPUTNUM 126"
ledf=/sys/class/leds/asus::touchpad/brightness
if [ $TPSTATUS -eq 0 ]; then
        eval $xcmd 1 || atp_error "Command '$xcmd 1' failed"
        [ ! -w $ledf ] || echo 0 >$ledf ||
                atp_error "Writing 0 to $ledf failed"
else
        eval $xcmd 0 || atp_error "Command '$xcmd 0' failed"
        [ ! -w $ledf ] || echo 1 >$ledf ||
                atp_error "Writing 0 to $ledf failed"    atp_error "Invalid Synaptics TouchPad status '$TPSTATUS'"
                ;;
esac

# XXX: '--set-int-prop' deprecated
xcmd="xinput --set-prop $XINPUTNUM 126"
ledf=/sys/class/leds/asus::touchpad/brightness
if [ $TPSTATUS -eq 0 ]; then
        eval $xcmd 1 || atp_error "Command '$xcmd 1' failed"
        [ ! -w $ledf ] || echo 0 >$ledf ||
                atp_error "Writing 0 to $ledf failed"
else
        eval $xcmd 0 || atp_error "Command '$xcmd 0' failed"
        [ ! -w $ledf ] || echo 1 >$ledf ||
                atp_error "Writing 0 to $ledf failed"
fi
```This is really dirty, at the very least it should attempt to find the "126" programatically.

Then I simply had to add the following in my .xbindkeysrc:
```
# Toggle touchpad
"/etc/acpi/asus-touchpad.sh"
m:0x0 + c:199
```

### Post by hk68 on 2012-01-16

### Post by scarleo on 2012-01-16
Has anyone tested the Sentelic absolute positioning patch from here: [https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic](https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic) ?

If so, did it work ok? Could you or anyone else familiar with kernel module compiling please write a step by step instruction for how to make it work?

Thanks in advance, and also thanks to all contributing to the "debugging" of UX31.

---

### Post by wulczer on 2012-01-16
> **scarleo said:**
> Has anyone tested the Sentelic absolute positioning patch from here: [https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic](https://github.com/thaelim/sentelic) ?

If so, did it work ok? Could you or anyone else familiar with kernel module compiling please write a step by step instruction for how to make it work?

I've used it and even contributed a little fix which has been merged in. It works fine for me now.

I'm on Debian and running a kernel downloaded from kernel.org, but to try the multitouch Sentelic module I'd recommend following this guide: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile) (in point 5 choose B - download the source archive).

In point 6 take the sentelic.c and sentelic.h files from thaelim's git repository and copy them to drivers/input/mouse/ in the kernel source tree (you will overwrite the original files, so you might want to make a copy of them somewhere). Then follow the steps from the guide and you should end up with a working kernel that includes multitouch Sentelic support.

The only problem I still have with my UX31 is sudden shutdowns when using RC6 with i915. Perhaps someone should comment on this LKML thread [https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/3/9](https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/3/9) as it seems many people are experiencing this issue and we don't even know how to collect debugging information...

### Post by iron4o on 2012-01-16
> **hk68 said:**
> Greetings,
as my current Linux notebook rapidly nears its end-of-life (my 2 year old son extracted numerous keys from the keyboard...), I need a new notebook and am considering the ZenBook. I read through the wiki documentation and a large part of this thread. It seems to me that you were successful in aggregating many individual fixes and workarounds, but that even if the "canonical" set of workarounds is applied, issues such as spontaneous shutdowns remain. This would be not acceptable to me for a notebook used for serious work. What is your opinion, please? Given that you have the notebook and run Linux on it, was the work worth it, would you buy it again?

Thanks for your time and effort,
    Henrik

As mentioned the only real issue is the sudden shutdowns. And event that is optional. You will not experience any shutdowns with i915rc6 turned off which is the default for the 3.2 kernels. Of course your battery life will not be optimal and the laptop will be ~10C hotter than one with i915 turned on.

### Post by lemsto on 2012-01-16
About the shutdown issue (and rc6 issues in general), Intel devs are looking for people able to reproduce it.
Please see this post on Phoronix :
[http://phoronix.com/forums/showthread.php?68199-Intel-Wants-YOUR-Linux-Questions-Feedback&p=246785#post246785](http://phoronix.com/forums/showthread.php?68199-Intel-Wants-YOUR-Linux-Questions-Feedback&p=246785#post246785)

[I]"With 3.2-rc6, we tried to enable rc6 by default for the kernel, but it was quickly had to be reverted, as it turned out that in some cases we still have random crashes and hangs. The down side of this is that we don't know at the moment what is causing those issues, as only very few users has reported this problem, and, like always with rc6-related issues, none of Intel employees computers can reproduce said issues for now. So hopefully, if we discover what causes the problem there, we'll be able to workaround it just like we did with VTd, and enable it by default for Sandy Bridge on 3.3 kernel.

In general, if you do not experience random hangs and graphics corruptions when rc6 is enabled - I mean, issues which you haven't seen previously, you shouldn't have any issues with it at all. But if you do, we'll be very interested in hearing about it, because _we are on a quest to locate machines which can **reliably** reproduce rc6-related problems_. But so far, I'd say that around 99% of all machines should "just work" with it enable."[/I]


And here is the Howto report an Intel Graphics bug :
[http://intellinuxgraphics.org/how_to_report_bug.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/how_to_report_bug.html)


(I still don't have my UX31E, so won't be able to report anything :/ )

---

### Post by wulczer on 2012-01-17
> **tjajab said:**
> 
If I would get a crash, I very much would want to know how to report. Are there some debug parameters we could turn on or something else that would be useful for the kernel developers?


I'm running with drm.debug=0x06, as explained in [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/how_to_report_bug.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/how_to_report_bug.html) but as far as I see it doesn't really give any useful information, ie. there's nothing special in the kernel logs just before the crash.

Still, it's always better to give the upstream devs more info than less and I hope they'll be able to extract something useful from it.

If you get a crash you could perhaps comment on the bug report to confirm it, so that the developers get an idea of how many people are affected and maybe give us some more detailed instructions on which debugging options to use.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-01-22
> **techfreak said:**
> Sorry forgot 3.3 rc1 with "quiet i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 intel_iommu=off"

Enabling rc6 is directly related to the frequency of shutdowns.

If people are able to keep it enabled and try to determine a pattern for the shutdowns, that is really helpful.

Alternatively, for others that need more stability, I don't think it is wise to enable rc6 until this issue is resolved. You will lose a couple of hours of battery - so pack the extremely small adapter :)

---

### Post by lemsto on 2012-01-22
> **wulczer said:**
> Following the Intel dev's advice, I have opened an issue in the Intel Linux Graphics bug tracker: [https://bugs.freedesktop.org/show_bug.cgi?id=44867](https://bugs.freedesktop.org/show_bug.cgi?id=44867)

As netconsole isn't available by default, it seems to be possible to add few lines in networks drivers (asix or ath9k) to get it.
[http://lkml.indiana.edu/hypermail/linux/kernel/0607.2/0410.html](http://lkml.indiana.edu/hypermail/linux/kernel/0607.2/0410.html)

### Post by wulczer on 2012-01-22
> **lemsto said:**
> As netconsole isn't available by default, it seems to be possible to add few lines in networks drivers (asix or ath9k) to get it.
[http://lkml.indiana.edu/hypermail/linux/kernel/0607.2/0410.html](http://lkml.indiana.edu/hypermail/linux/kernel/0607.2/0410.html)
[http://old.nabble.com/-PATCH--net%3A-Add-netconsole-support-to-cs89x0-driver-p75495.html](http://old.nabble.com/-PATCH--net%3A-Add-netconsole-support-to-cs89x0-driver-p75495.html)

Both of these patches are for wired Ethernet devies and the UX31E only has a wireless card and a USB-to-ethernet adapter. I haven't found any examples of adding netpoll support to these kinds of devices (I don't know if it's even possible :( )

### Post by lemsto on 2012-01-22
> **wulczer said:**
> Both of these patches are for wired Ethernet devies and the UX31E only has a wireless card and a USB-to-ethernet adapter. I haven't found any examples of adding netpoll support to these kinds of devices (I don't know if it's even possible :( )
What I understood is wireless and USB cards have their modules loaded after netconsole. So boot phases can't be debugged using netconsole.
But once machine is started, netconsole can be started manually :
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/6090/netconsole-wont-start-says-wlan0-doesnt-exist-aborting](http://askubuntu.com/questions/6090/netconsole-wont-start-says-wlan0-doesnt-exist-aborting)
But Im not an expert in those aspects ...

Here are some other patch examples, but still for ethernet adapters :
[http://www.mail-archive.com/git-commits-head@vger.kernel.org/msg12710.html](http://www.mail-archive.com/git-commits-head@vger.kernel.org/msg12710.html)
or for the AT91
```
+#ifdef CONFIG_NET_POLL_CONTROLLER
+static void at91ether_poll_controller(struct net_device *dev)
+{
+	unsigned long flags;
+
+	local_irq_save(flags);
+	at91ether_interrupt(dev->irq, dev, NULL);
+	local_irq_restore(flags);
+}
+#endif

...

 	/* Install the interrupt handler */
@@ -979,6 +992,9 @@
 	dev->set_mac_address = set_mac_address;
 	dev->ethtool_ops = &at91ether_ethtool_ops;
 	dev->do_ioctl = at91ether_ioctl;
+#ifdef CONFIG_NET_POLL_CONTROLLER
+	dev->poll_controller = at91ether_poll_controller;
+#endif
```

Any chance this can be integrated in asix.c driver ?..

You have to unbind your ehci_hcd driver before shutting down and then to bind it again after resume. Please look at the script for /etc/pm/sleep.d earlier in this thread.

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-01-23
A big thanks to everyone in this thread.  Within 24 hours of purchasing this I went from thinking I'd have to run Linux as a VM under win7 for the forseeable future, to having what's starting to look like the best, most feature-complete native Linux laptop I've ever had.  For my purposes (mostly remote thin-client stuff) it's ready to rock. I'm about ready for the ceremonial peeling off of the Windows 7 sticker.

UX31E, 11.10 X86_64, mainline kernel 3.2.1 3.2.1-030201-generic, most of the tweaks from the wiki page.

I can live with the trackpad button issue, I usually use taps anyway.  Hint for selecting text: tap one end of the text, hold down shift and tap the other end. Or just get some practice at "tap,tap-and-drag", which works too.  

Runs perfectly on battery; but I have had one probably "enable_rc6"-related shutdown so far when editing a set of files over an nfs mount.  Unplugging from AC is no problem (so far).  Battery life seems phenomenal, and it runs cold with no fan activity at all for general web/text editing use.  Getting a consistent 24mbit/sec real throughput on wireless g, haven't needed the ath9k module option. 

I also recommend switching to Unity 2D, which eliminates the compiz cpu overhead, and for my money it actually looks a lot better.

Until there's a solution for the wired ethernet dongle I'll probably keep my Win7 partition, but already looking forward to wiping it out.

---

### Post by soso67 on 2012-01-23
> **Matthew Sainsbury said:**
> I have to fix the spotty Wifi on this, I don't want to go back to Windows at all!

A step by step would be awesome from anyone please.

I've followed most of the steps on this forum without any joy.

Any help would be brilliant.

Hello everyone,

I'm running 11.10 with the 3.3.rc1 mainline kernel from the mainline PPA.
Almost everything is working fine but the wireless for me. I've been looking for 2 weeks for a solution to stabilise my wifi.

Today, I found that if I disable IPv6, the wifi is more stable.  The signal is still weak, but at least it's stable.

I added ipv6.disable=1 in the GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT.

Cheers

---

Mouse settings, with the Sentelic, are also not available, but tap to click works without me making any changes.  Also, it seems as if it's disabling while typing--or perhaps I've just gotten in the habit by now of arching my fingers more than usual, but I don't think so.

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-01-23
> **EatMorePie said:**
> UX31E, 11.10 x86_64, mainline kernel 3.2.1 3.2.1-030201-generic, most of the tweaks from the wiki page.

> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Thanks for your post. One question:

The Wiki says for kernel 3.2rc7+ the USB ethernet dongle works. Is this incorrect?

Finally checked this. I can confirm that it works, though interestingly, I get 100Mbit/sec (real throughput 11.7MByte/sec) in the left, USB2 port, but a bit less than half that in the right, USB3 port: about 4.8MByte/sec actual.  

In use, dmesg shows quite a few of the following kinds of messages for both ports:

```
asix 2-1:1.0: eth0: asix_rx_fixup() Bad RX Length 1555
asix 2-1:1.0: eth0: asix_rx_fixup() Bad Header Length
```
*Edit: the above messages may be a red herring: I can only produce it when communicating with one particular host.  I suspect that host is the problem.*

(The 2-1:1.0 address part will differ depending on which port it is)

And a ton of this sort of error only on the USB3 port:

```
xhci_hcd 0000:03:00.0: WARN: transfer error on endpoint
```

That one seems more significant given that it's only present in the badly performng port.  

So yes, it's working but noisy, and only working well in the USB2 port.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-26
I think Pumo may be referring to posts in [this massive thread]("http://forum.notebookreview.com/asus/582672-asus-ux31-ultrabook.html") at notebookreview.com, or something similar.

Basically, the Zenbook is shipping with 2 different SSD brands (Adata and Sandisk). For most of the (230+ page) thread benchmarks and reviews were saying that the Adata SSD was much faster than the Sandisk SSD. By page 200, however, [new benchmarks were reporting the opposite]("http://forum.notebookreview.com/8223356-post1945.html").

I don't think that thread came to a clear winner. Anyway, either brand is supported by Ubuntu, though I don't personally know of any HD benchmark tools for Linux.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-26

xhci_hcd 0000:03:00.0: WARN: transfer error on endpoint

USB2 - Ping: 9ms, DL: 43Mbps, UL: 75 Mbps
USB3 - Ping: 8ms, DL: 43Mbps, UL: 76 Mbps

During the upload tests (in both ports) I get many (~30) errors like:
```
asix 3-1:1.0: eth0: asix_rx_fixup() Bad Header Length
```

Kernel is the 12.04 included kernel (3.2.0-10-generic)

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-01-26
Thanks. Yeah, I get the asix length calculation errors on both ports as well, thanks for confirming as I only saw it when connecting to one other machine, and I'd messed with that one a bit in the past trying to debug jumbo frames so I wasn't sure it was the Zenbook's fault.   The xhci_hcd error only occurs on USB3.  

Tested using dd to pull a file from another linux box on the same switch over nfs to local /dev/null.  My numbers are in MBytes/sec rather than bits.   You may not be able to detect the  performance problem over the internet, as your worst port speed may still be better than your WAN speed.  43Mbps is very, very slightly better than the bad USB3 speed so if you're not seeing the xhci_hcd errors at all, then perhaps your release doesn't have that problem.  Mine is vanilla 11.10 with a bleeding edge kernel.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-26
For people with Elantech touchpads, I've amended Aurélien Jacobs' synclient script (originally [[here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11577569&postcount=428")] now also in [Wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Touchpad")). I added the following 4 lines:

```
# Click with multiple buttons
synclient ClickFinger1=1 ClickFinger2=3 ClickFinger3=2

# Enable palm detection
synclient PalmDetect=1

# Enable tapping for right clicks
synclient RBCornerButton=3

# Enable edge scrolling
synclient VertEdgeScroll=1 HorizEdgeScroll=1
```

The first line allows you to use the hard-button with multiple fingers for right and middle clicks. The second line enables palm detection (turns off touchpad while typing), the last line enables right clicking with a single-finger tap in the lower-right corner, and the fourth line enables edge scrolling in addition to the two-finger scrolling already enabled in the previous script.


---

Does anybody have a solution to run the script on login, and after a resume event? I thought I saw this somewhere in this thread, but I can't find it now. 

Thanks.

### Post by alchemy42 on 2012-01-26
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> 

Does anybody have a solution to run the script on login, and after a resume event? I thought I saw this somewhere in this thread, but I can't find it now.



I believe what your looking for is this:


```
gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.peripherals.input-devices hotplug-command /path/to/script.sh
```

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-01-26
Check to see if you have a file /usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/50-synaptics.conf . If you do then chances are all your script is doing is undoing the settings there.  Try making your changes there using the xorg Option syntax. (and restart X obviously. Rebooting works, though you can also switch to a console with ctrl-alt-F1, killall lightdm as root, then switch back with ctrl-alt-F7)

A couple of behaviors are also overridden in the trackpad tab of the mouse/trackpad control panel, so be sure you have that set the way you like it as well. 

This is how it all works in my installation; ymmv.

Are there any comments from someone with more knowledge in this area?

Thanks for everything so far!

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-01-27
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> I don't see any settings in that file, though I may add them. How do I know which InputClass section is being activated for my touchpad? There are a bunch of other files with similar InputClass sections that sound possible, too. Thanks.

Here's mine. Your dist and kernel are different so I don't know that the detection will work for you.  Note that MatchDevicePath was initially commented out at the bottom, I inserted it uncommented at the top.

```
Section "InputClass"
        Identifier "Touchpad"
        Driver "synaptics"
        MatchIsTouchpad "on"
	MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/event*"
        Option          "MinSpeed"              "0.5"
        Option          "MaxSpeed"              "1.0"
	Option		"FingerLow"		"15"
	Option		"FingerHigh"		"20"
        Option          "AccelFactor"           "0.075"
        Option          "TapButton1"            "1"
        Option          "TapButton2"            "2"     # multitouch
        Option          "TapButton3"            "3"     # multitouch
        Option          "VertTwoFingerScroll"   "1"     # multitouch
        Option          "HorizTwoFingerScroll"  "1"     # multitouch
        Option          "VertEdgeScroll"        "1"
        Option          "CoastingSpeed"         "8"
	Option		"CoastingFriction"	"30"
        Option          "CornerCoasting"        "0"
        Option          "CircularScrolling"     "1"
        Option          "CircScrollTrigger"     "7"
        Option          "EdgeMotionUseAlways"   "1"
        Option          "LBCornerButton"        "8"     # browser "back" btn
        Option          "RBCornerButton"        "9"     # browser "forward" btn
# This option is recommend on all Linux systems using evdev, but cannot be
# enabled by default. See the following link for details:
# http://who-t.blogspot.com/2010/11/how-to-ignore-configuration-errors.html
#      MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/event*"
EndSection
```

And again, also check to see if there's a trackpad tab in your Unity mouse/trackpad settings because a few behaviors will be overridden there, since those settings are processed after X has started.

I had no problems formatting the partitions, but in the [Arch Linux Wiki]("https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Asus_Zenbook_UX31E") regarding the UX31 there is the following comment:
[INDENT]If you get an error trying to format partitions when installing Arch try adding this line to the kernel parameters: 
 [FONT=Courier New]libata.dma=0[/FONT] [/INDENT]Perhaps this helps also here for your Ubuntu installation?

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-28
> **aspettl said:**
> Additionally to the nohwcrypt-option, I discovered that the background scanning for new wireless networks of network manager causes the network to stall frequently (for several seconds). This is very annoying, especially with SSH sessions.

Someone else had this problem, too, and just patched network manager:
[http://nilvec.com/disable-scanning-in-networkmanager-when-connected/](http://nilvec.com/disable-scanning-in-networkmanager-when-connected/)

I did the same and uploaded the package to a new PPA:
[https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook](https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook)

("sudo add-apt-repository ppa:aspettl/zenbook", if you want to use the patched version, too.)
This fixes the problem for me, at least now I can download big files with a constant speed. Furthermore, the disconnect/reconnect messages in dmesg and the router log file are gone.

The disadvantage is, of course, that switching between several wireless networks isn't that easy anymore, because no other networks will be visible if a connection is already established. But that's probably ok in most cases.

Regards
Aaron


Aaron, I have the same problem. Should I install of the packages this method builds (all the libraries, etc.) or just network-manager....deb. My problem was not fixed by simply installing the newly built "network-manager_0.9.2.0+git201201101813.0b30200-0ubuntu2_amd64.deb" package.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-28
> **techfreak said:**
> I was getting random AP disconnects and I have found that by adding the BSSID in network-manager for my home AP the disconnects have stopped. I tried this temporary solution:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/373680](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/373680)

Using 12.04 daily
3.3 rc1 kernel mainline

Thank you. My takeaway from this (tl;dr for future readers) comes from the last post. That is, to set the BSSID for my home network so that Network-manager won't keep trying to find other access points. The un-stated down-side to the patch Aaron linked to is that it will disable roaming (moving from one AP to another in a large network). You get the BSSID from `iwconfig`.

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-28
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> This is great. Are you doing this with a script, or manually? If the former, would you please share? I'd like to build-in ath9k debugging. My wifi is still pretty terrible.

I'm building manually using git. Here's a good tutorial to get you started. [http://blog.avirtualhome.com/2012/01/13/compile-linux-kernel-3-2-for-ubuntu-11-10](http://blog.avirtualhome.com/2012/01/13/compile-linux-kernel-3-2-for-ubuntu-11-10) 

Just use ```
debian/rules editconfigs
``` to build in the debugging.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-29
> **EatMorePie said:**
> Here's mine. Your dist and kernel are different so I don't know that the detection will work for you.  Note that MatchDevicePath was initially commented out at the bottom, I inserted it uncommented at the top.

```
Section "InputClass"
        Identifier "Touchpad"
        Driver "synaptics"
        MatchIsTouchpad "on"
	MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/event*"
        Option          "MinSpeed"              "0.5"
        Option          "MaxSpeed"              "1.0"
	Option		"FingerLow"		"15"
	Option		"FingerHigh"		"20"
        Option          "AccelFactor"           "0.075"
        Option          "TapButton1"            "1"
        Option          "TapButton2"            "2"     # multitouch
        Option          "TapButton3"            "3"     # multitouch
        Option          "VertTwoFingerScroll"   "1"     # multitouch
        Option          "HorizTwoFingerScroll"  "1"     # multitouch
        Option          "VertEdgeScroll"        "1"
        Option          "CoastingSpeed"         "8"
	Option		"CoastingFriction"	"30"
        Option          "CornerCoasting"        "0"
        Option          "CircularScrolling"     "1"
        Option          "CircScrollTrigger"     "7"
        Option          "EdgeMotionUseAlways"   "1"
        Option          "LBCornerButton"        "8"     # browser "back" btn
        Option          "RBCornerButton"        "9"     # browser "forward" btn
# This option is recommend on all Linux systems using evdev, but cannot be
# enabled by default. See the following link for details:
# http://who-t.blogspot.com/2010/11/how-to-ignore-configuration-errors.html
#      MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/event*"
EndSection
```

And again, also check to see if there's a trackpad tab in your Unity mouse/trackpad settings because a few behaviors will be overridden there, since those settings are processed after X has started.

Thank you! Did you have to do anything else to enable mouse buttons 8 and 9? **EDIT** No, I had a bug in my script below.

By the way, for anybody interested, you can apply (preview) these settings without restarting X by running this command:

```
cat /usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/50-synaptics.conf | grep Option | grep -v "^\#" | head -n -1 | awk '{print "synclient " $2 "=" $3}' | sed 's/\"//g' | bash
```

(remove "| bash" from the end to see what commands are being executed)

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-29
> **pumo said:**
> any ideas how to get dragging with left button on with elantech?
like windows resize or bookmark drag to specific folder

I have this working just fine. What is your desired functionality? Tapping, or click and drag? 

Here's a pastebin [link]("http://pastebin.com/sJEN5uaX") for my `/usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/50-synaptics.conf`. I edited [the one from EatMorePie]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11645192&postcount=633").

It supports both tap-tap-and-hold to drag, and also click and press to drag. **note:** be sure to restart X to active this. Also, be sure to check out the settings in Settings Manager, because they work out of the box pretty well, and will override any settings in that xorg.conf file.

well tap and moving finger isnt workin with me (12.04 kubuntu with 3.2.2 mainline kernel) not that two finger tap wont work at right click without script
i am using this script to get all work.
```

#!/bin/sh

# higher sensitivity
synclient FingerLow=9 FingerHigh=12

# faster movements
synclient MinSpeed=2.0 MaxSpeed=3.5 AccelFactor=0.1

# 2 fingers scroll
synclient VertTwoFingerScroll=1 HorizTwoFingerScroll=1

# enable tap to click (2 fingers for middle click, 3 fingers for right click)
synclient TapButton1=1 TapButton2=3 TapButton3=2

# continue dragging movement when reaching the edge of the touchpad
synclient EdgeMotionMinZ=30 EdgeMotionMaxZ=40 EdgeMotionMinSpeed=100 EdgeMotionMaxSpeed=400

# Click with multiple buttons
synclient ClickFinger1=1 ClickFinger2=3 ClickFinger3=2

# Enable palm detection
synclient PalmDetect=1

# Enable tapping for right clicks
synclient RBCornerButton=3


```

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-29
I have an update regarding poor WiFi performance.

If you are getting frequent disconnect-reconnect messages in your dmesg (or kernel.log) you may try simply running:

```
sudo killall wpa_supplicant
```

This forces wpa_supplicant to restart, and for me, fixes the problem. I got the idea from [this post]("http://azitech.wordpress.com/2010/02/22/deauthenticating-reason3"), and others. I do not understand why this works, and I don't yet know how to automate this. If I learn, I'll edit this post.

Previously mentioned fixes (like patching network-manager, and setting nohwcrypt in `/etc/modprobe.d/ath9k.conf`) did not work for me.

**Edit** I updated the Wiki with this information (and also a note about the MCE errors I, and others are seeing in the kernel log.)

---

Another problem which hasn't been mentioned yet, as far as I know:
With the 3.0 kernel of Ubuntu 11.10 and the 3.1 kernel surround audio via HDMI works (just choose the "Digital Surround 5.1 (HDMI)" profile). ### Post by philipMac on 2012-01-30
> **philipMac said:**
> tried another thumb drive (although it's also Sandisk), didn't work.




Solved!

So... what I had to do was rather than go to the boot tab of the BIOS and select the USB drive from here, I had to go to the last Save And Exit tab, and from *there* I could select the USB drive, and everything worked perfectly.

There were two options, one UEFI USB_disk_name, and one simply called USB_disk_name.

I selected the non UEFI one, which worked.  (I didn't test the other.)

Also, I used Unetbootin to create the USB drive.  The first screen I got was a Unetbootin screen (I am not precisely sure why, presumably there's some wrapper there).  I selected boot linux, everything worked perfectly.

---

I selected the non UEFI one, which worked.  (I didn't test the other.)

Also, I used Unetbootin to create the USB drive.  The first screen I got was a Unetbootin screen (I am not precisely sure why, presumably there's some wrapper there).  I selected boot linux, everything worked perfectly.

Interesting... could you let us know your kernel boot options? ie: this line in /etc/default/grub:

```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash pcie_aspm=force i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1"

```

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-01-30
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> I have an update regarding poor WiFi performance.

If you are getting frequent disconnect-reconnect messages in your dmesg (or kernel.log) you may try simply running:

```
sudo killall wpa_supplicant
```

This forces wpa_supplicant to restart, and for me, fixes the problem. I got the idea from [this post]("http://azitech.wordpress.com/2010/02/22/deauthenticating-reason3"), and others. I do not understand why this works, and I don't yet know how to automate this. If I learn, I'll edit this post.

Previously mentioned fixes (like patching network-manager, and setting nohwcrypt in `/etc/modprobe.d/ath9k.conf`) did not work for me.

**Edit** I updated the Wiki with this information (and also a note about the MCE errors I, and others are seeing in the kernel log.)

I'm still having network disconnect problems, so I removed `network-manager` and installed `wicd`. My connection seems much more stable now. I'll update this report tomorrow.

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-01-31
> **GodzillaMonster said:**
> Interesting... could you let us know your kernel boot options?

Nothing unusual in my boot options, I just added rootfstype=ext4 because of some warnings I got after using my kernel and left out the pcie_aspm option, as it's not supported:

```
rootfstype=ext4 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 elevator=noop
```

The elevator option is for using a non prioritized IO scheduler, since the SSD needs no command reordering.

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-31
> **benesm1 said:**

I'm pretty sure I know how to compile the extra firmware into the kernel but what do I need to do with the file from the BIOS? Do I even need it if I'm already using BIOS v210?

---

### Post by benesm1 on 2012-01-31
> **shakabra said:**
> I'm pretty sure I know how to compile the extra firmware into the kernel but what do I need to do with the file from the BIOS? Do I even need it if I'm already using BIOS v210?

The DSDT table is part of the BIOS firmware, the files posted on my webpage are extracted from version 210, of course the errors are corrected. You need to disable CONFIG_STANDALONE in Generic Driver Options and set the path to the hex file in CONFIG_ACPI_CUSTOM_DSDT_FILE in ACPI Support.

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-01-31
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Aaron, sadly I don't have any 5.1 setups in my home. I *may* be able to test this weekend though. What's the best way to test? I'll try `speaker-test -c 5 -t wav` unless you have another recommendation. Do we need to specify a device, or anything? Any tips will save me time, and will help ensure I test it right while I'm at my friend's.

I'll report back in a week on this.
Thanks! I just choose the profile "Digital Surround 5.1 (HDMI) Output + Analog Stereo Input" in the sound module of the control center. There is also a button "Test speakers" (or similar, not sure about the translation) where you can test each channel separately. ("speaker-test -c6 -twav" produces the same results with this configuration).

Another alternative:
Just use the default profile "Analog Stereo Duplex" and try the following commands to explicitly test the HDMI output:
"speaker-test -Dplughw:CARD=PCH,DEV=3 -c2" should output pink noise on the front channels, the AV receiver should recognize a stereo signal. This works for me.
"speaker-test -Dplughw:CARD=PCH,DEV=3 -c6" should (in theory) output pink noise on all channels. But I hear it only on the front channels, although the AV receiver recognizes a surround signal.

(I'll be able to test again in about two weeks, unfortunately not before.)

> **EatMorePie said:**
> Various places on ye intarwebs also suggest it can be done with grub2 without recompiling, for example: 

[http://blog.michael.kuron-germany.de/2011/03/patching-dsdt-in-recent-linux-kernels-without-recompiling/](http://blog.michael.kuron-germany.de/2011/03/patching-dsdt-in-recent-linux-kernels-without-recompiling/)

Mind you, this just an example I've found on the web in the last few minutes; not something I can vouch for or something I've tried, and I won't be in a position to try today.
Glad to see that, looks promising!

Regards
Aaron

### Post by shakabra on 2012-01-31
Here's one I built.
[COLOR=Red][B]THIS WILL NOT WORK WITH ANYTHING BUT UX31.
THIS KERNEL CONTAINS A MODIFIED DSDT FIGURE OUT WHAT THAT IS BEFORE YOU BREAK YOUR MACHINE PLEASE.
 
UX21 USERS WILL NEED TO EXTRACT THERE DSDT AND PATCH IT THEMSELVES.

DID I MENTION THIS ONLY WORKS WITH ASUS ZENBOOK UX31?

[COLOR=Black]SOURCES:
[/COLOR][/B][/COLOR][git://kernel.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ubuntu-precise.git](git://kernel.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ubuntu-precise.git)
[http://files.benesovi.eu/ux31e/](http://files.benesovi.eu/ux31e/)
[https://github.com/saaros/sentelic](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3)[COLOR=Red][B] 

[COLOR=Black]FILES:
[/COLOR][/B][/COLOR][wurkwithus/kernel]("http://ubuntuforums.org/wurkwithus/kernel")

[B]INSTRUCTIONS:

1. Install the debs
2. Run [/B]```
sudo update-initramfs -ck 3.2.2-ux31+
```

**3. Run **```
sudo update-grub
```

[B]4. Cross fingers. Reboot.

[/B]I also took the liberty of updating the Sentelic touchpad drivers. They were very slow. The touchpad is much nicer to use now. 

I had trouble building with the modified DSDT. I had to use the old make-kpkg method. Hence there are only 2 debs and an extra step on your part. So be it. 

I'm getting really good battery life using the powersave script from the wiki and the kernel parameters mentioned here [http://files.benesovi.eu/ux31e/](http://files.benesovi.eu/ux31e/). 

I haven't had a shutdown *yet*.

**If anyone wants to modify the wiki and add the link to this build that's fine, but add a link to this post as well.**


[COLOR=Red][/COLOR]

> **Suor said:**

I would hightly recommend it. Yes. Again I'm no expert so step in if I'm wrong. 
It's really easy to update. Just grab the driver from [http://www.asus.com/Notebooks/Superior_Mobility/ASUS_ZENBOOK_UX31E/#download](http://www.asus.com/Notebooks/Superior_Mobility/ASUS_ZENBOOK_UX31E/#download) extract it on a thumb drive.
