### Post by benesm1 on 2012-01-31
Hi,
the DSDT table is extracted from BIOS 210, so yes, you need to upgrade to 210 to use the fixed DSDT. Another thing is, that rc6 is unstable with the IOMMU, so you need to disable IOMMU in thr kernel and VT-d in the BIOS, this is a general issue. My kernel line is posted on my webpage, as for the UX21 users - cat /sys/firmware/acpi/tables/DSDT to some file, and post it here, I will attempt to fix it.
As to to the battery life - today I enjoyed watching movies the whole trip from Prague to JFK on my UX31e :-)
I will later post my complete kernel config on my webpage.

---

Most probably not related to any of the problems, but I'm curious if anyone else noticed that? Or is it just the 210 BIOS update (I updated BIOS and moved to the fixed DSDT at roughly the same time)?

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-01
> **sircas said:**
> I have a noob question, sorry :(
Why do you use a amd64 kernel instead of i386? its not for amd?


The amd64 architecture was originally created by AMD, but almost all current Intel processors use it as well. Intel's own 64-bit architecture is called Itanium, but never got really popular (and certainly not among desktop computers).

See [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X86-64](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X86-64)

Hi,
what model of laptop are you using? I have hands on experience only with UX31E. I'm using xfce4 as my DE, unfortunately its display control widget is quite simple, it can do only cloning. In this app is the laptop screen seen as "COR 13"" I'm therefore using ARandR, a GTK interface to xrandr. It shows the output names rather than display names. Until now I just tried the external VGA display as an extended desktop without any trouble. I need to test the HDMI connection as well.

I have no issues with wifi either, aside from the LED on the F2 key sometines behaves funny.

It's also possible, that the Oneiric userspace is just too old...

### Post by shakabra on 2012-02-02
Okay guys sorry for the mix up. I'm kind of new at git and kernel building. I was having trouble building with the modified DSDT because I didn't give an absolute path in the config. For a while I didn't even realize that I didn't build the kernel with the fixed DSDT. I got it now though and learned a few things in the process. I think I will write a little tutorial on it when I get a minute. I will link to it on the wiki. 

Thanks a bunch to **benesm1**. I haven't gotten to do much testing, but everything seems to work right now. 

I have taken everything out of the kernel that deals with virtualization and IOMMU. So if you were planning on using your laptop as a VPS then you're out of luck. I also updated the Sentelic touchpad drivers using the awesome work done by **saaros** , **wulczer **and others [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic). Bluetooth works due to the work of **erandom** here, [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3) . 

If you run this kernel you can ```
dmesg |grep DSDT
``` and you should see some cool messages about dangerous kernel tainting. **Thanks for tainting my kernel benesm1!**

I think all of the work is amazing and it's why I choose Linux. With open source software we can work together to fix our problems without waiting for some corporation who doesn't give a crap to fix it for us. I have learned so much since purchasing this machine. I can't wait to learn some more.

Here's the new builds. [http://wurkwith.us/kernel](http://wurkwith.us/kernel). Just install and reboot. If you're coming from a kernel newer than 3.2.0-12 then you will need to select this kernel at the grub menu when you boot. **Make sure you update your BIOS to 210. [COLOR=Red]This kernel will only work properly on a UX31. [/COLOR]**

Aloha, mahaloa nui loa

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-02
> **shakabra said:**
> Okay guys sorry for the mix up. I'm kind of new at git and kernel building. I was having trouble building with the modified DSDT because I didn't give an absolute path in the config. For a while I didn't even realize that I didn't build the kernel with the fixed DSDT. I got it now though and learned a few things in the process. I think I will write a little tutorial on it when I get a minute. I will link to it on the wiki. 

Thanks a bunch to **benesm1**. I haven't gotten to do much testing, but everything seems to work right now. 

I have taken everything out of the kernel that deals with virtualization and IOMMU. So if you were planning on using your laptop as a VSP then you're out of luck. I also updated the Sentelic touchpad drivers using the awesome work done by **saaros** , **wulczer **and others [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic). Bluetooth works due to the work of **erandom** here, [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3) . 

If you run this kernel you can ```
dmesg |grep DSDT
``` and you should see some cool messages about dangerous kernel tainting. **Thanks for tainting my kernel benesm1!**

I think all of the work is amazing and it's why I choose Linux. With open source software we can work together to fix our problems without waiting for some corporation who doesn't give a crap to fix it for us. I have learned so much since purchasing this machine. I can't wait to learn some more.

snip..

Aloha, mahaloa nui loa

Hey Shakabra,

I agree with your sentiments, too.

### Post by benesm1 on 2012-02-02
[COLOR="Red"]**CLARIFICATION:**[/COLOR]
IOMMU only influences VT-d, this is not vital to virtualization. You should definitely disable VT-d in the BIOS as well.
I am running smoothly both linux and windows xp guests under KVM on my UX31E. Especially the SPICE protocol and QXL drivers are impressive. No problems with virtio as well.

[COLOR="red"]**HOW-TO check, whether the kernel uses patched DSDT:**[/COLOR] dmesg should emit a warning concerning tainted kernel, otherwise you are using stock ASUS DSDT. You can compare dmesg output from patched and unpatched kernel, I also fixed a bug concerning OS detection.

My dmesg:

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-02
I built my own **UX21** kernel (based off of Ubuntu 3.2.0-12) with the following modifications
[LIST]
[*]benesm1's modified ux21 DSTD table (with Bios 209)
[*]Bluetooth support added
[*]Disabled IOMMU (I couldn't find an option for DMAR)
[*]Some kernel options for non-existent hardware disabled
[/LIST]

My idle power usage (with Wifi, Bluetooth, USB Ethernet) is around 8W.

I can confirm that VGA does work, and that I can still boot up a WinXP guest OS (using VirtualBox).

[INDENT]
* A thing I only had once with the UX21, but more often with the eeepc 1001PQ was, when I turned the laptop off and then plugged in the power cord, the laptop would occasionally turn itself on. *Is this a misconfiguration or just a glitch?*

* PowerTOP can't read wakeups/second, GPU ops/second and VFS ops/second any more (it says -nan). Also the frequency tab's kind of messed up, it shows only Idle for all cores and at 0.0%. *I must have removed some functionality from the kernel that PowerTOP needs, can anyone tell me which that is?*

* The last two or three days, when updating aptitude (although I don't think it has to do directly with aptitude, that's just when I saw it), I got some disturbing kernel messages. It says, that an exception occurred with the ata1 link, resetting link and something about ACPI commands. I attached the dmesg output. Would be nice if someone had a look.

* This has nothing to do with the kernel, but I'm writing this post, so I might as well include it: I configured Debian to shut down when I press the power button, but the laptop goes to standby, interrupting the shutdown. [/INDENT]

Thanks for your help,
Peter

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-02
> **darukka said:**
> @SorryGoFish could you post a howto for us beginners? would love to build my own kernel!

I could make a how-to for generic Debian systems, not Ubuntu especially, if you are interested (I'm running Debian testing)?

I'm using the latest mainline (I think that's it) kernel, which is 3.2.2 at the moment.
I kicked almost all drivers that I was sure I wouldn't need and built in all those that are loaded with the standard kernel. The startup time improved a little, although I didn't take any measurements, maybe I just imagined it :o
The modules directory is down to 29MB vs. 105MB with the stock kernel, the kernel image itself is 4MB vs. 2.7MB, but without initrd (which gives one or two seconds).

But I have to say I'm by no means a professional, I can just write down everything I did to make a kernel.
edit: Also I don't how much difference there is between Debian and Ubuntu regarding kernels.
edit2: It's the stable kernel I'm using, not mainline...

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-02
Just as a hint for Ubuntu users, if you want to compile your own kernel:
[http://blog.avirtualhome.com/2012/01/13/compile-linux-kernel-3-2-for-ubuntu-11-10/](http://blog.avirtualhome.com/2012/01/13/compile-linux-kernel-3-2-for-ubuntu-11-10/)
(not sure if that link was mentioned before or if I found it with Google... ;-))

I used that tutorial to compile a 3.2 kernel including Ubuntu patches (it is based on vanilla 3.2.2). I didn't touch the config, i.e., I took the default config from the oneiric 3.0 kernel and chose the default setting for new options in 3.2. Only exception, of course, is setting the path to the modified DSDT.

In addition to the packages mentioned in the blog posting, I also had to install the packages "libelf-dev", "asciidoc" and "binutils-dev" to compile the kernel.

Edit: too slow ;-), but I hope my comments regarding config and packages are useful.

Regards
Aaron

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-02
> **benesm1 said:**
> What about VT-d and IOMMU? Both deactivated? Maybe it's worth trying vanilla kernel.

VT-d disabled in BIOS, IOMMU disabled in the kernel config (CONFIG_IOMMU_SUPPORT not set).

Using the following kernel command line:
```
root=UUID=9c9dfe9d-32b3-4bac-aff6-d0ca77337a47 ro quiet i915.powersave=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 snd_hda_intel.power_save=10 ipv6.disable=1
```By vanilla kernel do you refer to the kernel provided by the distro, or not overriding the DSDT? I was getting shutdowns with the distro kernel (both with and without changing DSDT), even with intel_iommu=off in the command line.

The feeling so far was that your DSDT made the laptop last longer, but after ~10 hours of usage (working long hours :) ) it inevitably dies.

---

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-02
> **benesm1 said:**
> By vanilla kernel I meant kernel downloaded from kernel.org. I am using the DSDT modification for over a week and never experienced a shutdown. And I certainly gave my laptop a hard time. During that week I had to reboot only once, because I screwed something up in the X server and had no sshd installed.

I'm running 3.2.1 from a kernel.org tarball, with patches for the Sentelic touchpad and Bluetooth. Do you regularly suspend the laptop, or do you sometimes use it in streaks? Like 10+ hours in a row? I have a vague feeling that the shutdown happens if you enter and exit the RC6 state too many times and that a suspend/resume cycle resets it somehow. It's complete guesswork on my part, though.

I tried to keep the configuration I used the last time as close as possible to yours, but enabled loadable module support, compiled most of the stuff as modules and added some I use. But the important parts were identical.

Maybe there's some other factor, like different usage patterns - I for instance am on a flaky WiFi network that disconnects me a lot and sometimes have periods of inactivity followed by lots of activity (reading docs -> coding -> reading logs -> coding -> writing on a piece of paper -> coding...).

Maybe I'll try 3.2.1 with your exact config, just to see if there's something there that does make a difference... but I can't see what would it be :(

[LIST]
[*]xf86-video-intel version: 2.17.0
[*]xserver version: 2:1.11.2.902-1
[*]mesa version: 7.11.2
[*]libdrm version: 2.4.29
[/LIST]
I understand you're now a vanilla 3.2.1 kernel with the .config and boot line from your page and the custom DSDT, right?

I am using gentoo-patched kernel, which is essentialy vanilla with some minor fixes. Please note, that I was suffering from the sudden death syndrome even with the Gentoo kernel, which prodded me to do the DSDT and dmesg bugfixing.
As for the other software I am using:
[LIST]
[*]x11-drivers/xf86-video-intel-2.17.0-r3 with sna enabled (sandybridge new acceleration)
[*]x11-base/xorg-server-1.11.2-r2
[*]media-libs/mesa-7.11.2 with egl ,gallium, llvm, nptl and shared-glapi USE flags
[*]x11-libs/libdrm-2.4.27
[/LIST]
So it seems that you are using newer xorg-server, newer libdrm, identical mesa and maybe identical xf86-video-intel (don't know about the SNA).
As a policy, I am trying to have as few keyworded (unstable) packages as possible and of course I am excercising full control over my packages build options and dependencies, which is exactly why I left Debian in the first place...

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-02-03

### Post by debianguy on 2012-02-03
> **benesm1 said:**
> [COLOR="red"]**HOW-TO check, whether the kernel uses patched DSDT:**[/COLOR] dmesg should emit a warning concerning tainted kernel, otherwise you are using stock ASUS DSDT. 

I don't think this is necessarily true. When using grub to load the new dsdt the kernel should not notice that the dsdt is not the original version and this will not call itself tainted. At least that's how I understand it. 

But you can see if the the dsdt was loaded by grub because it probably has a different compiler version and different addresses listed.

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-05
To everyone reporting sudden shutdowns:
Do you always check the log files? Just to make sure that the sudden shutdowns are really "sudden" in the sense of "nothing is in the log files". An error in the asix ethernet module has been fixed (in 3.2.1) that caused crashes, but the ath9k module still seems to be problematic. At least I had several ath9k crashes so far and they're also reported regularly by other users.

I think there are several types of crashes and we should try to tell them apart. Even those crashes where nothing appears in the log could have different causes, and the fixed DSDT may fix only one of them. And maybe not everyone has all types of crashes (e.g. different usage pattern, slightly different hardware or kernel options, connected devices like external monitor)... That would explain the many different reports ranging from "crashing several times a day" over "only two times a week" to "none".

So, my request is, if you report a crash, please supply the following information (even if you mentioned it some posts before, it's hard to keep track ;-)):
[LIST][*]kernel (vanilla / Ubuntu patches / ..., exact version number)
[*]kernel options
[*]do you use the fixed DSDT?
[*]is there something in the logs?
[*]ath9k error in the logs: anything special like many connections, download running, weak signal, ...?
[*]no error in the logs: external monitor connected (VGA or HDMI)?
[*]running on battery or not?
[*]and if possible: how often do you get a crash with this configuration? (makes only sense if you're using the laptop several hours a day...)
[/LIST]

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-05
Maybe it would be a good idea to open another thread only for crash reports, where all reports get collected.
It would be easier to keep track this way...

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-05
> **aspettl said:**
> To everyone reporting sudden shutdowns:
Do you always check the log files? Just to make sure that the sudden shutdowns are really "sudden" in the sense of "nothing is in the log files". An error in the asix ethernet module has been fixed (in 3.2.1) that caused crashes, but the ath9k module still seems to be problematic. At least I had several ath9k crashes so far and they're also reported regularly by other users.

I think there are several types of crashes and we should try to tell them apart. Even those crashes where nothing appears in the log could have different causes, and the fixed DSDT may fix only one of them. And maybe not everyone has all types of crashes (e.g. different usage pattern, slightly different hardware or kernel options, connected devices like external monitor)... That would explain the many different reports ranging from "crashing several times a day" over "only two times a week" to "none".

So, my request is, if you report a crash, please supply the following information (even if you mentioned it some posts before, it's hard to keep track ;-)):
[LIST][*]kernel (vanilla / Ubuntu patches / ..., exact version number)
[*]kernel options
[*]do you use the fixed DSDT?
[*]is there something in the logs?
[*]ath9k error in the logs: anything special like many connections, download running, weak signal, ...?
[*]no error in the logs: external monitor connected (VGA or HDMI)?
[*]running on battery or not?
[*]and if possible: how often do you get a crash with this configuration? (makes only sense if you're using the laptop several hours a day...)
[/LIST]

Regards
Aaron

These are all good points.

From my own experience, it is pretty simple to tell the difference between the two issues.

The atk9k problem products a kernel panic and the machine reverts to console mode and prints out the kernel panic.

The shutdown problem abruptly powers down the machine.

A quick summary of my understanding of where we stand is that:
[LIST]
[*] shutdowns only occur when using the i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 kernel flags
[*] every mainline kernel version that has been mentioned here, and shakabra's DSDT-patched kernel, have produced at least one shutdown
[*] the ath9k kernel panic occurs more often in kernels above 3.2.1
[/LIST]

I think it would be good to see if someone could contradict this summary - in that they have a kernel with the rc6 flags set and have never had a shutdown as described above, even if they have had a kernel-panic style halt.

But yes, it would certainly be good to get some more context to build up a better picture.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-05
> **debianguy said:**
> Well, it doesn't for me, if this is the same problem. I get a panic report saying "BUG: soft lockup ... [kworker...]" in syslog, but the system never reverts to console but instead hangs in X where only the mouse movement still works.Everything else does not. The backtrace shows some ath9k routines but I wonder if  this is the same ath9k problem you see? 

I also noted that with Debian kernel 3.2.2 it is way worse than with 3.2.1.

Understood. Though I still propose we have two classes of issues.

A) An involuntary shutdown that only occurs when rc6 is enabled and involves the machine's power (ie: the power light) going off within a few seconds without user intervention. There is nothing in the logs to indicate what happened.

B) Machine locks up or becomes unresponsive and has to be forced to turn off by holding the power button down. This one manifests visually by either reverting to console mode (from the graphical UI) and showing debug info. It usually leaves something in the logs related to issues with ath9k (the wifi device).

---

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-06
> **fenski said:**
> I wonder if it was only mine laptop or is it reproductible. I had to download something and was told by friend to use JDownloader for this.

After installation of this software I had sudden shutdowns max 10 minutes after launching it.

Maybe someone could try to install JDownloader and confirm that it increases number of shutdowns drastically?

I installed it and used it to download an empty 200MB file using four connections over and over. After 20 minutes, no shutdown (3.2.1 with modified DSDT).

Just a guess, but maybe what you're getting is related to the wireless driver? Then using a program that opens lots of connections could explain why it's more probable for the problem to occur.

When launching JDownloader and after the laptop shuts down, do you see anything suspicious in /var/log/kern.log, /var/log/messages or /var/log/dmesg.0? Maybe you could try running JDownloader with the USB-Ethernet dongle and see if it changes anything?

I'm now running a script that uses intel_reg_dumper to take a snapshot of the RC6-related registers every second, I'll look at it when (if?) a shutdown happens.

I have had no ath9k panics crashes but I have super poor wifi signal strength with this driver. I can be sitting next to the AP and signal strength drops off relatively soon after connecting. I have tried setting BSSID and even switching to Wicd at one point.

### Post by Thijs22 on 2012-02-07
The aspm setting is crucial in 3.2 kernels to get to the long battery life. In 3.3, this famous regression has been fixed, and apparently this is backported to 3.2.5 right now. So time to remove aspm=force from everybody's kernel options :)

Disabling ipv6 may or may not have given me a bit more stability in wireless, i'm not sure. It does not do anything to the power consumption, unless through increase stability of the connenction, and therefore less scanning (or such). 

My wifi signal is quite stable right now, although it definitely falls of in strength faster than my girlfriends macbook.

---

### Post by sircas on 2012-02-07
> **shakabra said:**
> Okay guys sorry for the mix up. I'm kind of new at git and kernel building. I was having trouble building with the modified DSDT because I didn't give an absolute path in the config. For a while I didn't even realize that I didn't build the kernel with the fixed DSDT. I got it now though and learned a few things in the process. I think I will write a little tutorial on it when I get a minute. I will link to it on the wiki. 

Thanks a bunch to **benesm1**. I haven't gotten to do much testing, but everything seems to work right now. 

I have taken everything out of the kernel that deals with virtualization and IOMMU. So if you were planning on using your laptop as a VPS then you're out of luck. I also updated the Sentelic touchpad drivers using the awesome work done by **saaros** , **wulczer **and others [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic). Bluetooth works due to the work of **erandom** here, [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1867447&highlight=zenbook&page=3) . 

If you run this kernel you can ```
dmesg |grep DSDT
``` and you should see some cool messages about dangerous kernel tainting. **Thanks for tainting my kernel benesm1!**

I think all of the work is amazing and it's why I choose Linux. With open source software we can work together to fix our problems without waiting for some corporation who doesn't give a crap to fix it for us. I have learned so much since purchasing this machine. I can't wait to learn some more.

Here's the new builds. [http://wurkwith.us/kernel](http://wurkwith.us/kernel). Just install and reboot. If you're coming from a kernel newer than 3.2.0-12 then you will need to select this kernel at the grub menu when you boot. **Make sure you update your BIOS to 210. [COLOR=Red]This kernel will only work properly on a UX31. [/COLOR]**

Aloha, mahaloa nui loa

   Sorry I took so long, I've been on exams and I could not responter before.

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force quiet splash"

### Post by nixpro on 2012-02-07
Hello all. I have been following this very informative thread since it was four pages long. Bravo for all your hard work and friendly cooperation!

I have an UX31E i5 variant with BIOS 210 and Fedora 16, and I am getting 10 hours on idle (WiFi on, auto network scan, minimum bright, light web browsing) with kernel 3.1.2 (elevator=noop i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force), benesm1 patched DSDT loaded from GRUB and all the powertop recommendations in the wiki plus two more it gave me in fedora (Fresco 1009 not included, of course).

No sudden (rc6) shutdowns by the moment (and I previously had them even less than five minutes since power on), but I have not used the laptop for more than two hours in a stretch yet. I plan to have it running unattended all night long and see what happens at the morning.

As for battery duration, with any kernel newer than 3.1.2 (Fedora skipped 3.1.3) all the way to 3.2.2, power consumption has been always higher. Interestingly, leaving the kernel option pcie_aspm=force out increases the power consumption. After what Thijs22 said, I think I will try 3.2.5 when it hits the Fedora repositories.

This fedora 3.1.2 kernel has the Elantech patch included, happily for me as this is the model of touchpad in my Zenbook. They also ship it with the ath9k driver from wireless-compat (but I have had a pair of ath9k shutdowns... and, of course, the weak signal syndrome) but not with the rts5139 card reader driver. As for this, I recommend loading it in a per-use basis, as I can confirm what benesm1 said about the card reader drawing a noticeable amount of power even when not using it. I think it may be related to the polling frequency.

A funny thing I have noticed is that the system is having a hard time in reporting a power consumtion lower than 10% of the battery charge until it is below about 41W. So, it reports 10 hours (maximum, it varies according to the current task) on idle for a while.

On Fedora (maybe it is my particular setup) I have noticed that some power optimizations did not survive suspending the computer, so I had to make some modifications to the scripts. In particular, I was very upset to find that the cpufreq policy was not respected.

Sorry for the long post. I hope someone finds it useful :)

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-07
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> Now that you say setting the BSSID helped, I finally have the incentive I needed to patch NetworkManager :-)

Setting the BSSID is a way to *avoid* patching NM. You can set the BSSID using the system-tray icon / GUI.

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-07
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Setting the BSSID is a way to *avoid* patching NM. You can set the BSSID using the system-tray icon / GUI.

Yes, but I use more than one wireless network. I'd have to change the BSSID every time I wanted to use another network, or am I incorrect?

By patching NetworkManager I have the benefits of both sides, if I understand it correctly. Besides, patching NM is a thing of 20 minutes or so, I already did it :-)

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-07
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> Yes, but I use more than one wireless network. I'd have to change the BSSID every time I wanted to use another network, or am I incorrect?
You can set the BSSID for every single wireless network. Just open "nm-connection-editor", switch to the tab with the wireless networks, there you can edit all configured networks.

I removed my patched version of network manager, setting the BSSID solved the problem completely. Even though it's simple, a custom network manager has the disadvantage that you have to compile again if a new version is available (which is probably a security update).

Edit:
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> And just to clarify: should one (with kernels prior to 3.3 or 3.2.5) use the pcie_aspm=force option even if dmesg says "ACPI FADT declares the system doesn't support PCIe ASPM, so disable it." ?
Yes, before 3.2.5 or 3.3, use "pcie_aspm=force".

Regards
Aaron

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-07
> **aspettl said:**
> You can set the BSSID for every single wireless network. Just open "nm-connection-editor", switch to the tab with the wireless networks, there you can edit all configured networks.

Pardon my confusion, I was under the impression BSSID is the SSID, but that's not the case. 
As I understand it, the BSSID can be used when connecting to a network that has only one access point and is the MAC address of this access point.
So I could basically use it only for my home network, since the other networks I'm connecting to are spread across multiple access points.

Are there any negative effects of patching NM besides having to do the patch for each new version?

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-07
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> Are there any negative effects of patching NM besides having to do the patch for each new version?

You won't be able to "roam." This means when you're on a network with multiple access points (all with the same SSID), you'll have to disconnect, and re-connect to associated with a new access point.

More detail [here]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/373680").

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-07
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> You won't be able to "roam." This means when you're on a network with multiple access points (all with the same SSID), you'll have to disconnect, and re-connect to associated with a new access point.

Thanks, I guess that means I'm installing the unpatched version again :-\"

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-07
Does anybody know abut the warning on the Wiki in regards to sleeping the USB controllers? I've copied it below:
> If this workaround not used, memory controller setting will be screwed. After not correct suspend, many memory blocks starting with ~1G will be corrupt. Good way to see it is to use "memtest86+". Only way to fix controller setting is to open the case and plug off battery. If you start Windows or Linux with corrupted controller, you will get system crashes or/and damaged file system.

Since suspend and hibernate worked out of the box for me (with 12.04) I never setup any sleep.d script. I just ran memtest and it passed, but this message on the Wiki is scary.

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-08
I now switched from my custom kernel with fixed DSDT to the current Precise kernel, with DSDT loaded via Grub 2.
It just seems to work, the DSDT message from the kernel at least says so ;-)

You just have to save the "dsdt.aml" (generated by "iasl -tc dsdt.dsl") to /boot/dsdt.aml and put a new script in /etc/grub.d/, which includes the necessary commands into the Grub config file (don't forget to make the file executable). See [http://blog.michael.kuron-germany.de/2011/03/patching-dsdt-in-recent-linux-kernels-without-recompiling/](http://blog.michael.kuron-germany.de/2011/03/patching-dsdt-in-recent-linux-kernels-without-recompiling/).

Because I want to avoid unknown side effects of using the fixed DSDT with Windows, and I also want to be able to boot without fixed DSDT, I replaced the 01_acpi.txt in /etc/grub.d/ (see blog posting above) by a modified 10_linux file, which I saved as 09_linux_dsdt, and included only some additional lines for the DSDT:
[http://paste.ubuntuusers.de/405522/](http://paste.ubuntuusers.de/405522/) (unified diff to 10_linux included in Ubuntu)
This script just creates additional boot menu entries with "fixed DSDT" suffix.

I think for a productive system with regular kernel (security) updates, the Grub 2 solution is much better - it just works. But of course with this approach, we have to get all other fixes/improvements included in the mainline Linux kernel :-)

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-08
> **cromat said:**
> I understand it is just a matter of creating a kernel module and then adding it to the modules loaded on boot?
In principle, yes. Only the linux header files are required for the compilation. A convenient method would be to use dkms - once configured, dkms recompiles the module automatically when a new kernel gets installed.
See [https://help.ubuntu.com//community/DKMS](https://help.ubuntu.com//community/DKMS)

> **cromat said:**
> @aspettl I'm curious why compiling now is not the better option. Thinking about this more, if I want to add all my additional patches for hardware it is easier to compile into the kernel, also if staying up to date on kernel patches for security is your #1 concern, why not keep up with the latest stable (which btw is working great for me right now) and manage it yourself. Until the touchpad drivers and other fixes are included I would think it would be easier to manage this yourself, I could be wrong.
Sure, for integrating patches a custom kernel is necessary! But I hope we can get all important patches integrated upstream so that everything works out of the box (and for me, it's already ok because I don't need bluetooth and I have the Elantech touchpad). The fixed DSDT will always be an exception, therefore a simple solution that just works is preferable - and where we can use the default distribution kernels, which are frequently updated with bugfixes and security patches.
A custom kernel is nice, but (in my opinion) after some time annoying. Some years ago I built kernels regularly with custom voltage tables for my laptop (undervolting, which reduced fan noise to zero for normal work). That's ok for a transitional period, but not permanently. Most users don't want to download/install or compile a new kernel manually, and even "power users" will have different interests after a while.
(Of course you could say that some or even most kernel security issues aren't important for normal users, especially for single user systems, so a regular kernel upgrade wouldn't be necessary. But I just don't like that thought... ;-))

So, we should aim to get the patches upstream as fast as possible.

Does anyone know the status of the bluetooth patch ([http://thread.gmane.org/gmane.linux.bluez.kernel/18801](http://thread.gmane.org/gmane.linux.bluez.kernel/18801))?

Can someone comment on the status of the Sentelic touchpad driver? Bugs, stability, code quality?

Regards
Aaron

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-09
Sentelic touchpad:
According to [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic/issues/3](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic/issues/3) there is a complete rewrite of the driver (or at least in progress) and sending the patch upstream is planned.
(For building as a module, the Makefile is currently missing: [https://github.com/saaros/sentelic/issues/2](https://github.com/saaros/sentelic/issues/2))

Elantech touchpad:
Are there also patches? The script with custom settings applied via synclient is a good solution, I think. Probably everyone likes different settings.

Bluetooth: the patch with new device ids shouldn't be a problem to get upstream... there's no danger of regressions.

Sleep issues: To avoid the current script, we'd have to develop a real patch against the USB driver (ehci_hcd), that of course hasn't any negative effect on other systems. I don't know whether that's possible. But the current solution (unbind/bind via script) works and is very easy, so it's not top-priority.

This warning on the wiki page:
> If this workaround not used, memory controller setting will be screwed. After not correct suspend, many memory blocks starting with ~1G will be corrupt. Good way to see it is to use "memtest86+". Only way to fix controller setting is to open the case and plug off battery. If you start Windows or Linux with corrupted controller, you will get system crashes or/and damaged file system.
It has been asked before, but I don't remember an answer: Does anyone have more information?
I tried to suspend my system at the beginning without the workaround, and it caused no problems (other than that it didn't work, of course).

Regards
Aaron

### Post by fast-rs on 2012-02-10
I searched for a changelog of the bios version 211. The "VGA Bios Updated" part sounds interesting...
[http://www.hardwareluxx.de/community/f248/ux31-neues-bios-version-211-a-871964.html#post18372910](http://www.hardwareluxx.de/community/f248/ux31-neues-bios-version-211-a-871964.html#post18372910)

By this way I found a (often repeated) note for updates, which I can not find anywhere here in the thread: You can brick your zenbook by upgrading the bios from a USB media formated with NTFS! I think this is important for people with multi os booting.
[http://forum.notebookreview.com/asus/174395-bios-update-guide-asus-notebooks.html](http://forum.notebookreview.com/asus/174395-bios-update-guide-asus-notebooks.html)

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-12
I haven't had any shutdowns since three days, whereas previously I was getting them at least once per day.

My current configuration is:

[LIST]
[*]  UX31E
[*]BIOS 210
[*]VT-d disabled in BIOS
[*]  self-compiled 3.2.5 kernel from a kernel.org tarball
[*]a very slightly modified version of benesm1's kernel config (attached)
[*]fixed DSDT from [here]("http://files.benesovi.eu/ux31e/ux31e_dsdt.hex")
[*]sentelic.c/sentelic.h in drivers/input/mouse replaced with ones from [here]("https://github.com/saaros/linux/blob/sentelic/drivers/input/mouse/sentelic.c") and [here]("https://github.com/saaros/linux/blob/sentelic/drivers/input/mouse/sentelic.h")
[*]a patch to get bluetooth recognized (attached)
[*]rts5139 blacklisted (create a file in /etc/modprobe.d with the line "blacklist rts5139")
[*]Xserver and libraries from Debian testing
[*]the following kernel command line:
[/LIST]
 ```
BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-3.2.5-wulczer+dsdt root=/dev/sda1 ro quiet elevator=noop rootfstype=ext4 i915.powersave=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 snd_hda_intel.power_save=10 ipv6.disable=1

```For power saving I'm only using laptop-mode, where I disabled SATA power management (change CONTROL_INTEL_SATA_POWER to "0" in /etc/laptop-mode/conf.d/intel-sata-powermgmt.conf) and added usbhid to AUTOSUSPEND_USBTYPE_BLACKLIST in /etc/laptop-mode/conf.d/usb-autosuspend.conf (without this on battery power my USB mouse was not moving until I clicked one of the buttons).

I'm not using any power management apart from laptop-mode (no battery management daemon running; I run Openbox so there's no Gnome power management tools present).

For suspending the laptop I've modified /etc/acpi/actions/lm_lid.sh to the attached (without power management daemons the laptop did not suspend when closing the lid). I've also put an executable script in /etc/pm/sleep.d/ called zenbook (attached) that unbinds EHCI buses.

With this configuration, I get good battery life, the fan is almost always off, the sudden shutdowns don't happen anymore and suspend/resume works.

I feel that the solution for the shutdowns was changing the kernel config to be more like benesm1's one. I previously compiled a kernel using a config based on his, but still had shutdowns. This time the **only** things I changed were compiling in Sentelic vs Elantech, support for loadable modules (but all modules except rts5139 are compiled in, not loaded!), adding support for Bluetooth the SD card driver and RFCOMM and getting rid of KVM support.

Not sure if unloading the SD card driver has anything to do, I just want to be on the safe side and can always load it manually when I want to mount an SD card.

If I were to guess, I'd say that somehow compiling this minimal kernel was the tipping point. I previously ran with the fixed DSDT and still had shutdowns. This time all modules are built in, there's no initrd support and a whole lot of other things are left out, and I get no shutdowns. The power management tweaks might get you more battery life, but my real problem were the shutdowns.

I'm not very inclined to keep playing with the config, updating the BIOS, etc... I like the "if it ain't broke, don't fix it" philosophy :) I guess if I'll ever want to mount an EXT3 partition, or suspend to disk, or use IPSEC I'll just recompile the kernel with the necessary modules, see if it still has no shutdowns and if so, keep running with it. Little by little!

PS: For some reason the forum won't let me upload a kernel config file, so I rolled all four of the files in a tarball and attached that.

---

### Post by lemsto on 2012-02-12
[I]New RC6 patch from Intel :
[http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1NzA](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1NzA)[/I]


From the Intel mailing list on Saturday:

    so far, apparently all the RC6-related issues on Sandy Bridge seem to be gone when we enable RC6 but do not enable deep RC6. This apparently also cures the symptoms we were seeing with RC6 when VTd was active, so in theory we won't need the intel_iommu duct-taping anymore.

    Somehow, we haven't attempted to flip the default RC6 status once again this year (yet), so this seems to be a good timing to get this into -next and ask the ones affected by RC6 bugs with 3.2 kernel to test. If this indeed would fix the problems with rc6, I guess we can slip this into -fixes; but with all the RC6 default status flip-flopping of the past months I prefer to be sure that it won't get reverted once again.

    Note that this patch also gives a way to get into deepest rc6 - but it really never should be enabled by default. It is provided merely as a way to easy the debugging of those RC6 issues in the future.

---

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-12
> **fenski said:**
> Hmm... UUID based fstab doesn't work with this kernel config.
In fact all I get is kernel panic right know... let's see how to change it to be able to use it without /etc/* modifications.

Yeah, apparently to get UUID-based fstab support you need to include more CRC functions in the kernel... I just set GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true in /etc/default/grub.

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-13
> **maccam94 said:**
> Remove the RC6 option. Battery life will worsen, but RC6 isn't stable yet.

Yep.. I think this is worth reinforcing. 

You will not get a "sudden power-off shutdown" if you do not specify any rc6 options. 

You may get a kernel panic from some new kernels, but this is (probably) due to the wireless driver.

As an example, when I am on vanilla 3.2.1 with no rc6 related options I have no issues whatsoever - and that is with 10+ hours of use per day.

### Post by wulczer on 2012-02-13
> **A33 said:**
> Has anyone gotten rid of the shutdowns?

As other people suggested, the easiest way to avoid sudden shutdowns is to not enable RC6 support in the i915 module.

It seems that benesm1 has succeeded in getting rid of the shutdowns: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1865577](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1865577)

I'm currently in my fourth day of usage, with an uptime of almost 45 hours and I haven't had a shutdown yet, my config is detailed in [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1865577](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1865577)

Note that benesm1 is using Gentoo and I'm on Debian testing with a rather uncommon configuration. In both cases getting rid of the shutdowns required some heavy tinkering that's easy to get wrong. I hope that someone with a more standard installation will manage to isolate a minimum number of steps necessary to have RC6 without shutdowns.

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-13
I compiled a new kernel with Dodonov's patch, based on the Ubuntu 3.2 kernel (which is based on 3.2.5). Fixed DSDT is loaded via Grub 2, ASPM and semaphores is active. The power usage with i915_enable_rc6=0 is approx. 12.6W for me (wireless connection established, lowest screen brightness setting, idle), with i915_enable_rc6=1 (the new default) it is 5.7W. I also tried i915_enable_rc6=3 just to see the power usage, which was the same for me (5.7W). And to note: currently I have the same power usage with the stock 3.2 kernel of Ubuntu and also with the vanilla 3.3-rc3kernel (of course both with RC6 enabled).

I can't say anything about stability yet. I haven't even got a "typical" sudden shutdown since I installed the fixed DSDT. I had a crash without error in the log, but it wasn't really sudden as before, there was a second of screen corruption prior to the shutdown...

Edit - to the questions above:
The kernel from [http://people.canonical.com/~ogasawara/eugeni/rc6/amd64/](http://people.canonical.com/~ogasawara/eugeni/rc6/amd64/) with Dodonov's patch should work, but I think it's not yet clear whether it should be used without VT-d and/or with fixed DSDT. And of course it doesn't include the bluetooth patch or Sentelic touchpad driver.
I'd suggest to let VT-d be disabled, use the fixed DSDT and use Dodonov's RC6 patch, which avoids the "deep RC6" state. In this configuration, the laptop should be stable - given that the RC6 patch solves our problem. I think it's possible that especially the broken DSDT causes other similar crashes (and only for the Zenbooks), which shouldn't prevent the patch from getting mainline.

Regards
Aaron

PS, regarding power usage: Some time ago I got down to 4.6W (with a 3.2-rc6 kernel), not sure why not today. I'll have to look into that another time.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-13
I am a little confused why we are rolling kernels with both fixed DSDT patches and the new intel rc6 work.

People have experienced the sudden-power-off-shutdowns with fixed DSDT kernels (I have), although some have not. Many of those who have not had shutdowns also have drastically modified kernels, so it is hard to pinpoint the effectiveness of modified DSDT alone. It is even possible the DSDT is not an issue at all.

Would it not be more scientific to only roll kernels with one particular fix attempt at a time?

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-14
> **fenski said:**
> I wonder what was the default without this patch?
What rc6=1 meant before splitting it out to three stages?
According to [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/intel-gfx/2012-February/015076.html](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/intel-gfx/2012-February/015076.html), it was "RC6 and deep RC6" (new rc6=3).

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-14
> **thcourbon said:**
> I have a UX31e with an Elantech touchpad.

Have you guys managed to make the pseudo-button on the bottom of the touch area working ? Also is it possible to drag by clicking with the physical button while moving with another finger on the touchpad ?

I have not, and I don't believe there is driver support for either of these options. There are workarounds for right-clicking, and dragging. These can be found on the [Wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Touchpad").

> **thcourbon said:**
> Also I was used to the gesture under windows (under firefox mainly), is it possible to get those back under Linux ?
You can set corners of your touchpad to act as mouse buttons 8 and 9 (browser back and forward). The options are `LTCornerButton=8`(Left Top corner = back) and `RTCornerButton=9` (Right Top = forward).  What are the gestures like in Windows?


> **brainsqueezer said:**
> Anybody could tell me what is your idle temperature?
My idle temp is 47C. Compiling a kernel gets it to 90C. (These are the Core 0 and Core 1 temps reported by sensors. [modprobe coretemp])

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-14
> **ericus said:**
> Based on just reading the latest pages it seems to be a lot of issues with this ultrabook (although someone linked the wiki and said almost everything works now).

Can someone please give me a quick resume?
And, would you recommend me to buy this computer, or should I choose another one (for use with ubuntu only)?

I love my Zenbook running Ubuntu (12.04). My current bug list:
[LIST]
[*]WiFi card has trouble staying connected on enterprise networks (with many access-points)
[*]Touchpad marked buttons do not work (but easy workarounds exist)
[*]Multi-jack does not support microphone
[*]Limited battery life (5 hours under light load)
[*]Some assembly required (tweaks, custom kernel) to achieve optimal stability and usability
[/LIST]

The reason I decided to buy mine, despite the list of troubles, is partly due to the active problem solving going on in this forum. Most issues have been addressed, and the above list is, to my mind, totally tolerable.

Un-related to Ubuntu: in my opinion, the keyboard is a piece of junk. It seems to me that it's getting better (breaking in), but you should try one out, in person, if you can.

---

### Post by techfreak on 2012-02-14
> **cromat said:**
> Are you running the BIOS 211?
Yes running bios 211 with dsdt.aml loaded by grub.

Just had my first hard shutdown on this patched kernel using **i915.i915_enable_rc6=2**

I will try "3" later this evening


Custom Kernel [http://people.canonical.com/~ogasawara/eugeni/rc6/](http://people.canonical.com/~ogasawara/eugeni/rc6/)
 - i915_enable_rc6=1 (it should enable only RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=2 (it should enable only deep RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=3 (it should enable RC6 and deep RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=4 (it should enable deepest RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=5 (it should enable RC6 and deepest RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=6 (it should enable deep and deepest RC6)
 - i915_enable_rc6=7 (it should enable all possible RC6 states)

### Post by shakabra on 2012-02-14
> **cromat said:**
> Mind posting a quick how-to on how you did the patch? 

Sorry, meaning did you use the patched .deb files to install the kernel or some other means?
Just copy the patch from the mailing list starting with the signed off by line. Save it as rc6.patch or something. then from the top of your kernel tree run.
```
patch -p1 < /path/to/patch/file.patch
```
That's how I did it. I am getting the best battery life so far. Still using the same config I posted earlier. I haven't had a shutdown yet.

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-02-14
I have the 3.3-rc3 vanilla kernel with Sentelic and RC6 patch applied, custom config (builds in 10min :D), no fixed DSDT, VT-d enabled in kernel and BIOS, KVM enabled in kernel config. Command line is:
```
i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.powersave=1 elevator=noop fbcon=scrollback:128k
```

I now have an uptime of about 2.5 hours, mostly on AC but also battery.
My battery life was a little under 5h (about 6-7W) in idle.

### Post by cromat on 2012-02-15
> **ericus said:**
> Again, will I be disappointed if I buy this device? Do you think the problem will be solnedgången in the nearest future? Especially the battery life...

I don't think so again, if you are expecting multi-touch to work, then I would suggest you read up on the differences between the Elantech and Sentelic touchpads.  As far as the machine goes I think it works will on linux, a few quirks and other than the rc6 stability, I'm seeing around 5 -6  hours of battery, and in Windows I see probably 6 - 7 at the most. The touch pad in windows is horrible...the default driver in linux that doesn't have multitouch I think works better than the one for windows.  Standby and resume are faster in windows (really is 2 seconds, 3-4 on linux at the most), but boot in linux is faster.

Its a matter of what you need the laptop for, if you are willing to put some extra time and get the machine working to your preferences go for it, Otherwise find a machine with a more proven reliability.  

With everyone working on solutions (ubuntu, fedora, gentoo, mint people) there is alot of working knowledge and it has happened quickly.

setting 2 shutdown after 20 min of use
setting 3 shutdown approx 1 hr into use
4,5,6,7 shutdown after few hours into each trial.

I am curious if anyone else had similar experience.

This is consistent with expectations - only r6=1 was applicable to our shutdown issue according to the documentation. 

Thank you for performing an exhaustive test though!

---

### Post by Danners on 2012-02-18
Been running heavy usage on RC6=1 for a few days now with no issues, decided to switch it up to RC6=3 this morning and had a power down within 30 mins :(

Using: Linux Xenbook 3.2.5-tunerc6v1-generic #tunerc6v1 SMP Thu Feb 9 04:56:10 UTC 2012 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-18
> **Danners said:**
> Been running heavy usage on RC6=1 for a few days now with no issues, decided to switch it up to RC6=3 this morning and had a power down within 30 mins :(

Using: Linux Xenbook 3.2.5-tunerc6v1-generic #tunerc6v1 SMP Thu Feb 9 04:56:10 UTC 2012 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

This is totally as expected. 

rc6=3 is **exactly** the same as rc6=1 used to be.

Therefore this is exactly the same as reverting to any pre-patched kernel and re-confirming the shutdown problem that everybody was having with that pre-patched kernel is still there.

---

### Post by Danners on 2012-02-18
> **GodzillaMonster said:**
> This is totally as expected. 

rc6=3 is **exactly** the same as rc6=1 used to be.

Therefore this is exactly the same as reverting to any pre-patched kernel and re-confirming the shutdown problem that everybody was having with that pre-patched kernel is still there.

I see, thanks for explaining. Is there any other difference between pre-patch RC6=0 and post-patch RC6=1? Reason I ask is I'm getting significantly better battery performance since updating the kernel and using RC6=1 (7-10W vs 14-17W).

What is the general consensus on deeper and deepest idle states? Can it theoretically make a noticeable difference to battery life?

Cheers!

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-02-18
> **Danners said:**
> I see, thanks for explaining. Is there any other difference between pre-patch RC6=0 and post-patch RC6=1? Reason I ask is I'm getting significantly better battery performance since updating the kernel and using RC6=1 (7-10W vs 14-17W).

What is the general consensus on deeper and deepest idle states? Can it theoretically make a noticeable difference to battery life?

Cheers!

The consensus is that the only safe power-saving state for our architecture is standard RC6 power saving (RC6=1). Deeper (2) and deepest (4) will lead to sudden shutdowns and should be avoided. Past commenters here have even reported that these states did not improve battery life anyway.

---

### Post by Danners on 2012-02-18
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> The consensus is that the only safe power-saving state for our architecture is standard RC6 power saving (RC6=1). Deeper (2) and deepest (4) will lead to sudden shutdowns and should be avoided. Past commenters here have even reported that these states did not improve battery life anyway.

Cheers! I'm quite happy with the battery life using RC6=1, am getting about 5 hours out of each charge which is more than I was ever expecting.

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-18
It looks as if the important things will just work in Ubuntu 12.04 :-):

According to the changelog ([http://changelogs.ubuntu.com/changelogs/pool/main/l/linux/linux_3.2.0-17.26/changelog](http://changelogs.ubuntu.com/changelogs/pool/main/l/linux/linux_3.2.0-17.26/changelog)), the newest kernel (3.2.0-17.26, based on 3.2.6) enables RC6 by default and disables deep RC6 for Sandy Bridge.
I booted the kernel without custom DSDT and without any (custom) kernel parameters. Temperatures / fan behaviour confirms that RC6 is enabled.

Bluetooth works, too. At least scanning for devices works. There's an open bug report: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/916319](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/916319)
Can someone confirm that bluetooth works with the current precise kernel?

For 3.2.0-16.25, there's also "x86, mce, therm_throt: Don't report power limit and package level thermal throttle events in mcelog". So those messages should be gone.

The ASPM fix is already included a while, almost since the beginning of the Ubuntu 3.2 kernel series.

Now the suspend script and the touchpad (especially Sentelic) seem to be the only remaining problems. Are the ath9k crashes gone?

I think a major rework of the Wiki page will be necessary soon ;-)

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-18
> **ericus said:**
> Do you think this ultrabook ever will be fully supported with Ubuntu? Without a lot of tweaks etc.

Good question - this thread is huge so it can be hard to figure out the current status. However, reading through a few recent posts it does looks like fixes for the biggest issues are close to being submitted upstream.

It also looks like there is no need to worry about patching the DSDT. It even appears that the suspend related scripts aren't needed with the 3.3 kernel.

Therefore, it would seem that an Elantech UX31 will pretty much work out-of-the-box with kernel 3.3.

Those with Sentelic touch pads unwilling to build their own kernels will have to wait for the driver to be included in the kernel.. I'm not aware of any ETA for this.

(remember that rc6 support is only about battery life - and if the rc6 related patch gets put into the 3.3 or 3.4 kernel, we will have better battery life with a simple tweak (addition of a boot parameter))

Better summaries of the current situation would be greatly appreciated! (EDIT: like Aaron's, above!)

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-20
> **pumo said:**
> is there need to add "i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force" anymore?
Semaphores is disabled by default, I just checked the source of Ubuntu-3.2.0-17.26 and 3.3-rc4. If you want to activate it, you should disable VTd, see [http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html](http://intellinuxgraphics.org/2011Q4.html). But RC6 is a lot more important.
The ASPM fix included in Ubuntu-3.2 and vanilla 3.3 series shouldn't require pcie_aspm=force anymore.

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by pumo on 2012-02-20
ok thanks,VTd have been disabled since I installed kubuntu.
I havent quite understand where do I might need VTd=enabled?
if I install virtualbox should be enabled ?

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-02-20
> **pumo said:**
> I havent quite understand where do I might need VTd=enabled?
if I install virtualbox should be enabled ?
No, VT-d is only required to pass through physical devices to a virtual machine.

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by scottro on 2012-02-20
One thing I'm finding--it's been mentioned in passing, but for me, it turns out to have a major effect---is that dimming the screen, with Fn+F5 will add hours to battery life.  I haven't left the machine running to test, but, going by the acpi command in Ubuntu, Fedora, and Arch--and I can be in text mode, not X and still have the same result--dimming the screen can change ACPI reading from about 3.5 hours (when fully charged) to 5-7 hours.

### Post by benesm1 on 2012-02-20
[COLOR="Red"]**i915.rc6 testing update:**[/COLOR]

Hi guys,
during last two weeks I was testing extensively several kernels from the 3.3rc line and I came to these conclusions:
[LIST=1]
[*]The in-kernel ASIX USB ethernet driver sucks loud, it causes lockups even under slightest load. Interestingly enough, a Skype call in the background kept running even with after such crash.
[*]I began using Bluetooth. It seems, that it has no effect on stability whatsoever.
[*]And now the best one: I found out, how to produce the sudden shutdown "on demand" :-).
[/LIST]
How I did it:
The main thing is, that I am using XFCE4 (with compositing enabled) as my desktop environment. I wanted to give a try to Gnome3 and this is where I hit the wall. While I had no shutdown in all the time with patched DSDT (BIOS version 210 and 211) when I was using XFCE4, the moment I ran Gnome3 and tried to recompile some packages, my system just dropped dead. The usage scenario was as follows: Running Gnome Shell, compiling in the background and watching a movie in VLC. I got a shutdown in ten minutes. I was able to reproduce this behavior after reboot. [COLOR="Red"]Later I applied this patch [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/intel-gfx/2012-February/015131.html]("http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/intel-gfx/2012-February/015131.html"), but I got a shutdown as well, albeit this time it took like an hour and a half to poweroff.[/COLOR]

The question is: [COLOR="Red"]can anybody running Gnome3 or Unity switch to XFCE4 and give it a try?[/COLOR] Leave new DSDT in place, because without it I was getting shutdowns in XFCE and even in console.

---

From the WIKI:
"Works out of the box from kernel 3.2-rc7 and later thanks to Aurélien Jacobs and commit "

This has the rc6 fix in it.

---

### Post by cromat on 2012-02-22
> **Vetteklr said:**
> OK, I have them downloaded and on my thumbdrive. 

Now what? Is there a certain order I have to install them in? They open up in application manager. 

I feel like such a new guy with this. :(:confused:

If you want to get the battery saving of RC6 I suggest:
```
sudo sed -ie 's/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=.*/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="i915.powersave=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1"/g' /etc/default/grub
```

If you are doing the above make sure you do so before installing the kernel otherwise an extra command is required.

Assuming your in the directory where they exist:
```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```

Reboot and grub should show the new kernel.

---

With ubuntu 11.10 what king of problems will I face? I read hibernate/sleep/multitouch ...etc tens of problems. I'm quite confused and disappointed.

Any advice is appreciated.

Here is a quick digest but I suggest taking a look at the wiki:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook)

Which touchpad do you have Sentelic or Elantech?

I having the Sentelic touchpad, I compile in the driver from github into my kernel along with the RC6 patch.  Everything else works with a simple patch or configuration.

### Post by cromat on 2012-02-22
> **pumo said:**
> does eugenis rc6 patched kernel need anything to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT line?

His original patch only enabled the choosing of the RC6 states so yes. I'm using:

```
i915.powersave=1 i915.semaphores=1 i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 elevator=noop quiet splash
```

### Post by cromat on 2012-02-22
> **ericus said:**
> Thanks. By the patch, you mean the patch in the first link? 
What does DSDT do?

And sorry for being stupid, but how do you install that patch?
After compliling the kernel? But how to patch it?

Yes there is both .deb files and the .patch file available the .patch one is there if you want to compile from source.

The Differentiated System Description Table is the main table in the ACPI part of a computer's BIOS.
The Advanced Configuration and Power Interface (ACPI) defines a large number of tables that provide the interface between an ACPI-compliant operating system and system firmware. These allow description of system hardware in a platform-independent manner in ACPI Machine Language (AML).

There is an error in the table noticed by @benesovi a gentoo user.  After the RC6 fix, I have not seen a need to use this fix on my machine, though I might try it out in the future, others are not using the fix as well.

A compiled kernel cannot be patched as the patch updates source code, the patch would be applied as follows the a downloaded kernel source that you would intend to update
```
patch -p1 < pathFileHere 
```

The link to eugeni patch the .deb files already contain the patch, compiling is only required for a Sentelic touchpad right now.

### Post by hansdezwart on 2012-02-25
I have just bought a UX31E (with the i7 processor and an Elantech touchpad, it is just out of the factory I think as it came with BIOS version 211) on the basis of the Wiki page ([https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook)) telling me that with a bit of work everything should pretty much be fine.

One instant problem that I have run into is that there doesn't seem to be a way to get wifi working out of the box using an Ubuntu 11.10 Live USB stick (it does work when I try with Fedora 16 and with OpenSuse 12.1).

I've tried most of the sensible advice from here: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/WirelessTroubleShootingGuide](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/WirelessTroubleShootingGuide), but that doesn't help me closer to a solution. See the output from some relevant commands:

**nm-tool**

```
NetworkManager Tool

State: disconnected

- Device: wlan0 ----------------------------------------------------------------
  Type:              802.11 WiFi
  Driver:            ath9k
  State:             unavailable
  Default:           no
  HW Address:        01:08:CA:87:2F:D5

  Capabilities:

  Wireless Properties
    WEP Encryption:  yes
    WPA Encryption:  yes
    WPA2 Encryption: yes

  Wireless Access Points
```

**lshw -C network**

```
  *-network DISABLED
       description: Wireless interface
       product: AR9485 Wireless Network Adapter
       vendor: Atheros Communications Inc.
       physical id: 0
       bus info: pci@0000:02:00.0
       logical name: wlan0
       version: 01
       serial: 01:08:ca:87:2f:d5
       width: 64 bits
       clock: 33MHz
       capabilities: pm msi pciexpress bus_master cap_list rom ethernet physical wireless
       configuration: broadcast=yes driver=ath9k driverversion=3.0.0-12-generic firmware=N/A latency=0 link=no multicast=yes wireless=IEEE 802.11bgn
       resources: irq:17 memory:dea00000-dea7ffff memory:dea80000-dea8ffff
```

**iwconfig**

```
wlan0     IEEE 802.11bgn  ESSID:off/any  
          Mode:Managed  Access Point: Not-Associated   Tx-Power=0 dBm   
          Retry  long limit:7   RTS thr:off   Fragment thr:off
          Power Management:off
```

**rfkill list**

```
0: hci0: Bluetooth
	Soft blocked: no
	Hard blocked: no
1: asus-wlan: Wireless LAN
	Soft blocked: no
	Hard blocked: no
2: asus-bluetooth: Bluetooth
	Soft blocked: no
	Hard blocked: no
3: phy0: Wireless LAN
	Soft blocked: no
	Hard blocked: no
```

I have a sneaky suspicion that this problem will solve itself once I get a slightly newer kernel things will change, but as I don't have an Internet connection on the laptop things will be hard.

The work that has gone into the wiki page is great and I really appreciate it. The approach per type of functionality is clear and quite useful. It doesn't help me with a fresh machine though, because it doesn't tell me the steps that I should take one by one to get a working Ubuntu laptop (wifi working, sound/mic working, decent battery life (doesn't have to be the best it can be), no random shutdowns.

I would gladly help with making such a guide, so I will document the steps that I will take to get it there, but I might need some help here and there as I am certainly no true expert. I have started my journey by asking on AskUbuntu how to get the Wifi going (see here: [http://askubuntu.com/questions/107598/how-do-i-get-wireless-to-work-on-a-asus-zenbook-ux31e](http://askubuntu.com/questions/107598/how-do-i-get-wireless-to-work-on-a-asus-zenbook-ux31e)), unfortunately the tag "zenbook" doesn't seem to exist yet and I don't have enough reputation to create it. Hopefully somebody from this forum can help out with that.

### Post by xheyther on 2012-02-26
The linux kernel 3.3-rc5 was released yesterday night/this morning/this nightdepending on your timezone.

It contains the first patch from Eugeni Dodonov but not the one that fix operator precedence and actually correct the rc6 activation on our beloved Zenbooks.

So for all of you who run custom kernel don't forget to fetch and apply it (from here for example: [https://bugs.freedesktop.org/show_bug.cgi?id=44867#c45](https://bugs.freedesktop.org/show_bug.cgi?id=44867#c45)).

I expect that the rc6 problem will be gone in 3.3 and maybe in the 12.04 kernel.

As of for now and for me the things that don't work out of the box are the bluetooth and the external screen that you have to plug after the X start (which is something I always forget and really bother me).

### Post by xheyther on 2012-02-27
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> So basically with the new 3.3-rc5 kernel, the very first patch which enabled i915_enable_rc6 values of 0-7 is obsolete now?
We are back to either enable or disable, but it works, am I right?

And to save everyone a little guesswork: with the 3.3-rc5 kernel, the first patch mentioned in the linked post above is already applied, the second one (operator precedence) is not.

The patch that allowed to choose the RC6 activation level was never intended to be ported on 3.2 nor 3.3. It leaves in drm-next and will certainly be merged for 3.4.

Still with 3.3-rc5, Eugeni Dodonov did send a patch that, when the kernel is booted with i915.rc6_enable_rc6=1, will only enable shallow rc6 so the sandy bridge owner won't suffer hard shutdown.

But this patch (which is included in 3.3-rc5) was messed up (an operator precedence issue was causing rc6p, the level that make snb machine to crash, to be allways activated). So you still need the last patch posted by Eugeni which will basicaly fix the fix. 

I personally (I obviously am not Linux Torvalds) expect the final fix for 3.3 to be merged for the next RC.

All of that is if you roll your own kernel right from the source. The Ubuntu-kernel team has already picked all the necessary patch (and I believe that rc6 is also enabled by default) so if you use a kernel from them, you should have nothing to do to enjoy 6 hours long trip without charge with your Zenbook.

Hope it was clear this time.

### Post by candelierj on 2012-02-27
Hi everyone, 

I'm following this thread since its beginning and it makes me learn very interesting things !

I can reproduce easily a shutdown (kernel 3.3rc4 + rc6 patch, same since 3.2) : 
- on AC or on battery, doesn't matter
- high activity on wifi (like dl a distribution (...) with transmission at ~1.2Mb/s) -- it doesn't occurs on usb/ethernet
- as soon as my screen fade out to black, either a freeze or a kernel panic occurs.

I thus suspect a problem with ath9k suspend or else...

Julien

(sorry for my english)

edit: Vt-d enable, rc6=1, all scripts (in sleep.d and power.d) prescribed in wiki

### Post by baustromverteiler on 2012-02-27

any thougts?

I am using Fedora 16 and the original kernel rpms, so I install these modules in the updates directory. VT-d and intel_iommu settings make no difference.

Does anyone that have compiled his/her own kernel and get ridden for good of this RC6 bug have a .config that would like to share? I need KVM, LVM, crypto-loop, UUID-partitions and several other things, but I don't believe these can be related to this bug.

Thank you for your help!

### Post by darukka on 2012-02-29
I haven't had a shutdown since I used the 3.2.5-tunerc6v1-generic kernel. Altough I get a lot of freezes! I am pretty sure this has nothing to do with the kernel directly, as I get the same freezes in kernel 3.3rc4.

Anyhow, I found a way to get around the freeze by going to the console with Ctrl+Alt+F1 and killing the compiz task with sudo kill -9 <compizid>. The task will not kill without the -9! When I switch back to X with Ctrl+Alt+F7 compiz restarts automatically and everything works again.
It is quite funny, that all the freezes I had appeared while using Google Chrome. I am still able to move the mouse, but everything else is "frozen". When compiz is restarted the previously selected tab is closed, but i still have all the other tabs.
I would say (but I am not really sure about it, it could be random) that the freezes usually only appear when I do traffic intense surfing like Youtube or downloading.

Does someone have a similar behavior?

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-02-29
> **darukka said:**
> I haven't had a shutdown since I used the 3.2.5-tunerc6v1-generic kernel. Altough I get a lot of freezes! I am pretty sure this has nothing to do with the kernel directly, as I get the same freezes in kernel 3.3rc4.

Anyhow, I found a way to get around the freeze by going to the console with Ctrl+Alt+F1 and killing the compiz task with sudo kill -9 <compizid>. The task will not kill without the -9! When I switch back to X with Ctrl+Alt+F7 compiz restarts automatically and everything works again.
It is quite funny, that all the freezes I had appeared while using Google Chrome. I am still able to move the mouse, but everything else is "frozen". When compiz is restarted the previously selected tab is closed, but i still have all the other tabs.
I would say (but I am not really sure about it, it could be random) that the freezes usually only appear when I do traffic intense surfing like Youtube or downloading.

Does someone have a similar behavior?

This would appear to be a bug with compiz or the video driver.

It might be advisable to not wrap this one within the term "freeze", as that is currently being used to talk about lockups (ie: the ath9k problem). If anybody else is getting this and calling it a "freeze" then please let everybody else know.

Our current informal terminology is:

[LIST]
[*]Sudden Shutdown - power goes off within a second or two, all on its own.
[*]Freeze/Lock Up/Panic - the machine halts, often showing a text kernel panic issue about the ath9k driver, and cannot be recovered unless you hold down the power button
[/LIST]

If I was to add your issue to the list I would call it a "Unresponsive GUI" issue.

### Post by nixpro on 2012-03-01
> **cowai said:**
> In cinnamon I have a bluetooth icon on the panel. But lspci doesn't say anything about bluetooth

Just for the record, as I don't remember if this has been said here before: the bluetooth radio is on the same card than the WiFi one, but it's connected through an USB interface. So, one can see it connected using lsusb instead of lspci.

### Post by jmlynn on 2012-03-01
I used Acronis True Image installed on a bootable USB drive to boot up and backup the Original C: drive (the Win7 Home), the empty D: drive and the "hidden" Asus Recovery partition onto a 1 TB USB Drive.

Then I shrink the D: drive to give me 80 GB of storage as free space and installed Ubunto on it.  Once Ubutu is up, I noticed that the installation had installed GNU boot manager (I believe), which identify both the Ubunto 11.10, the Windows 7 and the Asus Recovery partition.  

I haven't tried it yet, but I assume I can boot up the Recovery partition to restore the machine back to original distribution state.  But well, who would? 

My next task is to take a backup of every partitions not that I got what I like onto this wonderful machine.

Jeff

### Post by jmlynn on 2012-03-01
Just in case someone is either still wondering if dual-booting Windows 7 and Ubuntu is a workable solution or still wondering the problems facing geting Ubuntu to run in the Asus Zenbook UX31 machine.  Here were my detailed "adventures" to get a fully functional dual booting machine, (except suspense/resume is still not working yet):

[LIST]
[*]I used an Acronis True Image on USB drive to boot up and save the complete hard drive into a 1TB USB drive
[*]I then shrunk the D: (data drive) to give me 80 GB free space
[*]Download Ubuntu onto another USB drive as bootable USB drive following the direction under "Show me" in Ubuntu web site
[*] Install Ubuntu to the free space as dual boot with Winows 7
[*]upon rebooting Ubuntu after installation, I noticed that I have no network support even though the UX31 came with Wifi built-in and a USB-to-ethernet adapter, none were supported in the current ubuntu 11.10 distribution
[*]borrowed a DLink N300 USB wireless adapter, Ubuntu sees it immediately and I invoke the "Update Manager" to update any updates.
[*]Once update was completed and after reboot, I noticed that there are now two Wifi adapters activated.
[*]Plugged-in the Asus Usb-to-ethernet adapter and the ethernet cable from my router, I also got wired connection as well.
[/LIST]
So now, I have an almost fully functioning dual-booting UX31, that is how I like (love) it.  Sad that I cannot do away with Windows, as I still need for task such as update my maps with my GPS 4 times a year.
I am sure a few more adventures will get my suspense//resume working, sooooon!  But even than, I can live without resume, as the machine cold start into Ubuntu under 8 seconds loaded with Apache server and Mysql server.  What is the different betwen 8 sec vs 3 sec when I used to have the most powerful luggable notebook from Dell and Toshiba that cold start at about 2 minutes.:p



So happy computing with your new Asus UX-31 machine,


Jeff

### Post by diegocarrera on 2012-03-02
The bluetooth patch  was submitted upstream, but it has not been accepted as of yet: [https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/4/69.]("https://lkml.org/lkml/2011/12/4/69")

You can report it at [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/916319](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/916319)

or

you can recompile your own kernel, and include @fenski bluetooth patch
[http://fenski.pl/zenbook/bluetooth.patch](http://fenski.pl/zenbook/bluetooth.patch)

### Post by Mike Lovell on 2012-03-04
> **SorryGoFish said:**

No I did not, that was a bit silly of me.  I thought it meant to run that if the device wasn't recognized as a touchpad (like it wasn't back in the older kernels).

Thanks, it's all working!  Yay! :D

---

### Post by Mike Lovell on 2012-03-04
> **Mike Lovell said:**
> No I did not, that was a bit silly of me.  I thought it meant to run that if the device wasn't recognized as a touchpad (like it wasn't back in the older kernels).

Bad assumption on my part.

Thanks, it's all working!  Yay! :D

BTW, that was ([source]("http://www.theorangenotebook.com/2012/02/call-for-testing-clickpad.html")):


```
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:chasedouglas/clickpad
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
```

Logout, login again:

```
xinput | grep Elantech
```

Note down the ID, then:


```
wget http://people.canonical.com/~cndougla/enable-clickpad.sh
chmod a+x enable-clickpad.sh
wget http://people.canonical.com/~cndougla/enable-rightbutton.sh
chmod a+x enable-rightbutton.sh
```

Then:

```
./enable-clickpad.sh <id>
./enable-rightbutton.sh <id>
```

And it should all work.

---

I don't think this is actually basic.. earlier in this thread we used a script in /etc/pm/sleep.d/ to do things when sleeping and resuming.. I think that sort of thing is the right place do to this.

Some like:

```

#!/bin/sh

case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Your magic goes here
        ;;
esac 

```

would appear correct.. chmod the script to execute too.

### Post by essen on 2012-03-06
Hello, I've been lurking on this thread for a while and the last fixes solved my unexpected shutdown issues with rc6. However there is still one issue that remains which may or may not be related but started happening at around the same time, which is an unexpected shutdown happening when unplugging the power cord.

It doesn't seem to happen all the time, especially when unplugging from the other end of the cord. It doesn't happen while in Grub, or the BIOS, but it does happen after Linux boots, even before I log in. I suspect the issue to be related to laptop-mode or other settings that get enabled when Linux is starting up, but can't seem to find the right one.

Amongst the people here who experienced this issue, did anyone find any hint of what could cause this?

Thanks for the answers.

Thanks for the answers.

To track this down, I think anybody experiencing this should state what their status is with regard to all power-saving settings.

I don't know much about this, but I'm sure this includes anything in **/etc/pm/**, whether you have **laptop-mode** enabled, what your current fix for **rc6** is (and any grub boot flags).
