### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-07
Just to note, here is a discussion about the clickpad support in precise:
[http://ubuntu.5.n6.nabble.com/ClickPads-and-Click-Actions-td4532895.html](http://ubuntu.5.n6.nabble.com/ClickPads-and-Click-Actions-td4532895.html)

We know that we have to plug in the external monitor after X startup (or I think even after booting is sufficient), otherwise the internal monitor stays black. Windows can reactivate it and I'd like that feature in Ubuntu, too.
I noticed that the kernel outputs the following message if the external monitor has been plugged in too early:
```
[drm:intel_dp_complete_link_train] *ERROR* failed to train DP, aborting
```
This seems to be a DisplayPort problem. I found reports with other Sandy Bridge notebooks and e.g. docking stations, where external monitors connected via DisplayPort had a similar problem - but it isn't exactly the same. For us, the BIOS seems to deactivate the DisplayPort link (at least partially).
Did anyone already investigate this issue?

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-07
> **aspettl said:**
> We know that we have to plug in the external monitor after X startup (or I think even after booting is sufficient), otherwise the internal monitor stays black. Windows can reactivate it and I'd like that feature in Ubuntu, too.
I noticed that the kernel outputs the following message if the external monitor has been plugged in too early:
```
[drm:intel_dp_complete_link_train] *ERROR* failed to train DP, aborting
```
This seems to be a DisplayPort problem. I found reports with other Sandy Bridge notebooks and e.g. docking stations, where external monitors connected via DisplayPort had a similar problem - but it isn't exactly the same. For us, the BIOS seems to deactivate the DisplayPort link (at least partially).
Did anyone already investigate this issue?
Update:
I found a simple workaround: just press Fn+F8 while the BIOS is still working (before loading Grub, e.g. when it asks for the BIOS password, if set). Then it will switch from external to internal display and everything later works as usual with both internal and external display :-)
So it's not necessary to unplug the VGA/HDMI cable every time, which really isn't good for the connector if done often. It's a really simple and straightforward solution, but somehow I didn't think of it before (and didn't read of that in this thread so far).

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by ericus on 2012-03-08
I've been testing a lot of kernels and reinstalled my system for serveral times now, and I think I've found the best option for me.

Ubuntu 12.04 (with gnome-session-fallback), kernel 3.3rc6, boot option "i915.i915_enable_rc6=1 i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force", the powersaving script, [the clickpad script from douglas]("http://www.theorangenotebook.com/2012/02/call-for-testing-clickpad.html"). 

With the screen brightness set to 0-20% (which is okay for almost every enviroment, except for direct sunlight), and cpufreq-indicator (which allows me to set CPU clock on all kernels at once) @800MHz or ondemand, I'm seeing about up to 8.5 hours (!) of battery life, and a discharge rate of ~5.5W.

No sudden shutdowns, and the wifi works perfect. I've had one disconnection so far, but that might as well be my servers fault.

Bluetooth does not seem to work though. But, I'm not going to use it anyway.

So far, so good!:)

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-03-11
> **EatMorePie said:**
> As far as I can tell from some light experimentation, even if you create a bootable SD card, the UX31 will not offer to boot from it.  Is this correct or have I missed a step?   

It'd be nice to have a removable alternate boot volume that didn't occupy/stick out of a USB slot.

If it's possible, you may have to enable booting from it in the BIOS. I think most BIOS do not support this though.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-03-11
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> If it's possible, you may have to enable booting from it in the BIOS. I think most BIOS do not support this though.

Holding ESC down whilst starting the machine up will give you a list of bootable devices, including USB ones. If the card slot doesn't show up in that list, then it won't boot from it.

### Post by sekhar.rahul on 2012-03-14

### Post by brainsqueezer on 2012-03-15
Yesterday I updated to Precise and Kernel 3.3. Wanted to test the new Synaptics module for Xorg. I was developing some code to detect gestures with 2 and 3 fingers on the touchpad. And it started working :) Began switching windows with two fingers.

Sadly I got some panics and partition went readonly. After some investigation messages told something about ext4 journaling. Found there is a bug going on with the data=ordered option for ext4.

After one of those panics my computer doesn't boot. When I power on the led turns on, I hear the fan and the caps lock key light shows for a second. Nothing more happens, the screen doesn't show anything and nothing works in the keyboard. I don't know if I got memory controller corruption or what. I'm thinking about leaving the computer on to waste the battery to check if that cleans the memory controller. Not sure about that.

Any ideas?

I had purchased the Zenbook UX31-DH72 recently.  I had installed Ubuntu 11.10 using USB thumb drive, the latest stable release and the latest release of VirtualBox on it with Windows XP.  There are a few things that kind of behave strangely, otherwise, both OSes work great:

[LIST]
[*]Need to do the fn+F8 button prior to boot menu to use LCD screen + external monitor
[*]every now and then, even after fresh reboot, opening Firefox will forced log off
[*]sleep/hibernate/resume didn't works for ubuntu
[/LIST]
Other than that, it works fine.  I didn't see most of the other problems folks in this forum reported.  However, I did need to borrow a "name brand" wifi USB adapter to get both the buildt-in wifi adapter and the wired ethernet adapter to work.  This was done after the USB ubunto installation is complete, but unable to install updates due to missing drivers for both adapter.  Hence need a name brand one to get installation completed.  Once latest updates are installed, everything works fine.


Same as you, I installed Windows XP so I can update my TomTom GPS, my Scanner, and use MS Office to collaborate documents with others.  I even use the Ubuntu One to syn document between Ubuntu and Windows so I don't need to tx file using the USB Drive.  Other than that, I "live" mostly in Ubuntu.



As you indicated, you are a dot net developer, you will have to live with the 4GB RAMs, you can use a USB hub to get more USB ports and a large Western Digital Passport compact TB drive.  With the fast and light weight, you will love this machine.



Good luck and have fun!


Jeff

### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-16
> **quesst said:**
> That's right, but as far as I have exprienced that this does not work, when I want to use the internal display at the same time.
Quesst
Works for me, at least with HDMI, didn't test VGA. But you have to press Fn+F8 before Grub loads.

**ASPM issue**
I think I found the reason why power usage is higher since 3.2 kernels: ASPM is not enabled, even with "pcie_aspm=force".
```
sudo lspci -vv | grep ASPM
```
Output with old 3.1.10 kernel:
```
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed unknown, Width x0, ASPM unknown, Latency L0 <64ns, L1 <1us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #1, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <1us, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #2, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #4, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 2.5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <4us, L1 <64us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 unlimited, L1 unlimited
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
```
Output with current 3.3-rc7 kernel (or 3.2.11, or 3.2-Ubuntu, or 3.1.10 without pcie_aspm=force):
```
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed unknown, Width x0, ASPM unknown, Latency L0 <64ns, L1 <1us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #1, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <1us, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #2, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #4, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 2.5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <4us, L1 <64us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 unlimited, L1 unlimited
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
```

I have a power usage of approx. 4.5W on the old 3.1.9 kernel, compared to >5.5W with 3.3-rc7 (or 3.2 vanilla, or 3.2 Ubuntu). 3.2.5 (and higher) or 3.3 kernels contain the "ASPM fix" (see e.g. [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1MzY](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1MzY)), so this seems to be a problem for us.

Here is a good page about ASPM: [http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM](http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM)
I'm not keen on trying setpci directly, but maybe I'll try the enable-aspm script... that should be safe, as we know which ports support L0/L1 by looking at the output of lspci -vv using an older kernel.

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-16
Update:
The enable-aspm script seems to work :-)
I have to call it two times, once for root complex 00:1c.3 (PCI Express Root Port 4), endpoint 03:00.0 (USB 3.0 controller), and once for root complex 00:1c.1 (PCI Express Root Port 2), endpoint 02:00.0 (Atheros wireless).

Probably these values are the same for other UX31 users, but maybe not, so make sure they are. (I'm using BIOS version 210.)
This drops power usage by about 1W for me (on idle).

Regards
Aaron

### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-17
My approach was simple:
I booted an old kernel (Ubuntu 11.10 3.0 kernel should do, but also 3.1 series or 3.2 series until 3.2.4) and wrote the current configuration to a file:
```
sudo lspci -vv > lspci_vv_old
```

Then, I rebooted with a recent kernel and did the same again:
```
sudo lspci -vv > lspci_vv_new
```

Now you can compare the two files. There are four entries in the lspci_vv_old file with "ASPM L0s L1 Enabled", where the lspci_vv_new file contains "ASPM Disabled". Those four entries correspond to two devices (USB 3.0 and wireless) and the two corresponding root complex devices (USB 3.0 / wireless are connected to those "PCI Express Root Ports").
For me, these are:
```
00:1c.1 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family PCI Express Root Port 2 (rev b5)
00:1c.3 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family PCI Express Root Port 4 (rev b5)
02:00.0 Network controller: Atheros Communications Inc. AR9485 Wireless Network Adapter (rev 01)
03:00.0 USB Controller: Fresco Logic Device 1009 (rev 02)
```

You have to determine which device is connected to which root complex, this can be done with
```
lspci -t
```
The root complex ids are on the second level (below "[0000:00]") and the square brackets behind them tell which bus address belongs to the root complex. The bus address is the first part of the device id, before the colon.
Example, for my UX31:
```
-[0000:00]-+-00.0
           +-02.0
           +-16.0
           +-1b.0
           +-1c.0-[01]--
           +-1c.1-[02]----00.0
           +-1c.3-[03]----00.0
           +-1d.0
           +-1f.0
           +-1f.2
           \-1f.3
```
Therefore, 02:00.0 is below 00:1c.1, and 03:00.0 below 00:1c.3.

You have to insert the endpoint and root complex device id in the enable-aspm script:
[http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM#Enabling_ASPM_with_enable_aspm](http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM#Enabling_ASPM_with_enable_aspm)
I created two copies of enable-aspm and set ROOT_COMPLEX and ENDPOINT in both, i.e.,
```
ROOT_COMPLEX="00:1c.1"
ENDPOINT="02:00.0"
```
and
```
ROOT_COMPLEX="00:1c.3"
ENDPOINT="03:00.0"
```

Regards
Aaron

### Post by aspettl on 2012-03-18
It doesn't really matter. They just have to be executable and get called with root privileges. First test whether they work as desired by executing them with sudo (and checking the ASPM state again with lspci). If you want to make that permanent, then the place to put the commands would probably be /etc/rc.local, e.g., put the two enable-aspm scripts in /usr/local/sbin, make root the owner (to avoid the risk that a non-root user can modify a file that will be executed with root privileges), and call the scripts from /etc/rc.local by inserting their full path before the "exit 0".

Regards
Aaron

### Post by matt2053 on 2012-03-19

sudo update-grub

Ha! I think I figured it out. It's actually there, in the Grub menu under "Previous Linux Versions." Grub thinks 3.3 is older than 3.3-rc7, probably just because of the file names.

Output with old 3.1.10 kernel:
```
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed unknown, Width x0, ASPM unknown, Latency L0 <64ns, L1 <1us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #1, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <1us, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #2, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #4, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 2.5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <4us, L1 <64us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 unlimited, L1 unlimited
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
```
Output with current 3.3-rc7 kernel (or 3.2.11, or 3.2-Ubuntu, or 3.1.10 without pcie_aspm=force):
```
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed unknown, Width x0, ASPM unknown, Latency L0 <64ns, L1 <1us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #1, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <1us, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #2, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #4, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 2.5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <4us, L1 <64us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 unlimited, L1 unlimited
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
```

I have a power usage of approx. 4.5W on the old 3.1.9 kernel, compared to >5.5W with 3.3-rc7 (or 3.2 vanilla, or 3.2 Ubuntu). 3.2.5 (and higher) or 3.3 kernels contain the "ASPM fix" (see e.g. [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1MzY](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTA1MzY)), so this seems to be a problem for us.

Here is a good page about ASPM: [http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM](http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Documentation/ASPM)
I'm not keen on trying setpci directly, but maybe I'll try the enable-aspm script... that should be safe, as we know which ports support L0/L1 by looking at the output of lspci -vv using an older kernel.

Regards
Aaron
Thanks Aaron, I'll look into this. Does this look OK? (3.3rc6):
```
sudo lspci -vv | grep ASPM
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed unknown, Width x0, ASPM unknown, Latency L0 <64ns, L1 <1us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #1, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <1us, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM L0s L1 Enabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk-
		LnkCap:	Port #2, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #4, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <512ns, L1 <16us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 2.5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 <4us, L1 <64us
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
		LnkCap:	Port #0, Speed 5GT/s, Width x1, ASPM L0s L1, Latency L0 unlimited, L1 unlimited
		LnkCtl:	ASPM Disabled; RCB 64 bytes Disabled- Retrain- CommClk+
```

### Post by mrv on 2012-03-22
FYI, I updated the [wiki page](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook) with ALPM information (saves 1W), and referring here for the ASPM information. I also made separation between Ubuntu 12.04 LTS and 11.10, plus made the 12.04 now "default". I marked power management to be only with remarks anymore since RC6 is now enabled by default.

I also added a couple of optional kernel parameters I'm using, and took a little head start by marking Bluetooth as fixed as it'll be now included in the next 12.04 kernel update!

With WLAN connected, display on with minimum brightness, Bluetooth on and Unity running I have now ca. 5.0W idle power consumption on my UX31. ALPM was probably the biggest win (from 5.9W -> 5.0W), although some of the others may have helped as well.

(note that I'm also running Unity from PPA - 5.8 which is to-be-released-very-soon-now should have a fix that reduced compiz wakeups from 50-60 to ~10)

---

### Post by EatMorePie on 2012-03-24
> **MilkeySUFC said:**
> 
I have the same outputs as you but my noob-ishness means I don't know where to put the scripts. 


Just to clean it up a bit, I replaced the variable settings in the enable_aspm script with this:
```

[[ "2" == "$#" ]] || {
  echo $0 takes 2 arguments
  exit 1
}

ROOT_COMPLEX=$1
ENDPOINT=$2

```

Made it executable and moved it into /usr/local/sbin.   Then changed /etc/rc.local to
```

/usr/local/sbin/enable_aspm "00:1c.1" "02:00.0"
/usr/local/sbin/enable_aspm "00:1c.3" "03:00.0"

exit 0

```

(I probably don't need to add that **my values may not be yours** and you **must** do the [lspci checks in aspetti's comment]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11772555&postcount=1299") to get the correct ones for your machine. Do I?)

Anyway, works great, along with all the other tweaks I've learned about here, I'm getting about 4.2W idle and barely 5W at the moment, with 8 firefox tabs open but not much else going on.  Unity 2D makes a difference as well.

Hi all,

### Post by mrv on 2012-03-27
> **ericus said:**
> The ALPM ([https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/PowerManagementALPM](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/PowerManagementALPM)) did not decrease my power usage, it increased it! What?!
From 6.06W > 6.08W with screen brightness set to minimum and no programs running on both tests.

The power usage varies all the time a bit. I'd recommend not only not running anything else than Unity, but also checking with powertop that there isn't anything causing any abnormal level of wakeups ("normal" currently seems to be around 80-110 with WLAN connected and screen on). For example, ca. minute after power on and logging in there happens a couple of processes at least.

I tested with the powerstat from [this PPA](https://launchpad.net/~colin-king/+archive/powermanagement) that by default settings uses a 3 minutes cooldown time and 5 minutes measuring time (you can see the measurements every 10 seconds). Please also take care that screen doesn't power off during the test, so that the result is consistent.

Unity 5.8 + compiz brought the compiz wakeup fixup which lessened powertop wakeups. In addition to that, I'm using the two extra kernel parameters in wiki which may affect something (at least the drm vblank setting helps with i915 wakeups now that the fixed compiz is in 12.04).

ALPM did drop the power usage with around 0.9W for me, seemingly, but of course there is also a possibility of an error. I believe the the rate reported by battery may not be really accurate anyway, and of course since I've been testing with WLAN enabled, the WLAN driver might select a higher transmit power among else. For example I did at some point get a reported 4.53W, but now I'm getting 5.2W without any apparent difference.

With regards to ASPM, if you are not in a hurry, pcie_aspm=force kernel parameter should start to work again and force the ASPM on Zenbook in the coming weeks in Ubuntu. After that you won't need the manual enable_aspm scripts, but you will need the pcie_aspm=force parameter since Zenbook BIOS is doing the wrong thing.

Note: I'm also using the stock 12.04 kernel, not any 3.3 rc kernel et cetera.

---

```
mikelovell@laptop:~$ ~/Scripts/enable-clickpad.sh 12
mikelovell@laptop:~$ ~/Scripts/enable-rightbutton.sh 12
X Error of failed request:  BadAtom (invalid Atom parameter)
  Major opcode of failed request:  17 (X_GetAtomName)
  Atom id in failed request:  0xb0002
  Serial number of failed request:  23
  Current serial number in output stream:  23
(standard_in) 1: syntax error
(standard_in) 1: syntax error
(standard_in) 1: syntax error

X Error of failed request:  BadMatch (invalid parameter attributes)
  Major opcode of failed request:  141 (XInputExtension)
  Minor opcode of failed request:  57 ()
  Serial number of failed request:  20
  Current serial number in output stream:  21

```

The source of the problem appears to be "xinput list 12" failing now:

```
mikelovell@laptop:~$ xinput list 12
ETPS/2 Elantech Touchpad                	id=12	[slave  pointer  (2)]
	Reporting 10 classes:
		Class originated from: 12. Type: XIButtonClass
		Buttons supported: 12
X Error of failed request:  BadAtom (invalid Atom parameter)

  Current serial number in output stream:  23
		Button labels: "Button Middle" "Button Right" "Button Wheel Up" "Button Wheel Down" "Button Horiz Wheel Left" "Button Horiz Wheel Right" None None None None None

```

Could someone perhaps give me the values of:

$left
$right
$top
$bottom

From *"enable-rightbutton.sh"*.  Maybe I can hard-code them and get right click back.

Are you running the above versions?  Also, is your right click now working?  In which case, if you still use the script, could you give me the output of those variables (in my first post).

I have the same problem.

If somebody with an Elantech touchpad can paste the output of:
```
xinput list 12
```
We would be very grateful.

**EDIT** Got it.
```
xinput set-prop 12 "Synaptics Soft Button Areas" 1638 3145 1470 3441 0 0 0 0
```

I used `xinput list-props` instead. Here's code to replace from `i=0` to `done < .....`
```
read minx maxx miny maxy <  <(xinput list-props "$device" | grep "Synaptics Edges" | sed -e "s/[^:]*[^0-9]*//" -e "s/,//g")
```

**EDIT 2**:
By the way, the last 4 zeros are for middle click. I just wrote some code to use the middle also. The resulting command is:
```
xinput set-prop 12 "Synaptics Soft Button Areas" 1939 3145 1470 3441 1336 1938 1470 3441
```

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-04-02
I just spent some time trying to figure out why the TRRS Combo-jack (audio + microphone) is not working.

I found that by editing [FONT="Courier New"]/etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf[/FONT] and adding the following line I am able to use my cell-phone headset as both headphones and a microphone automatically. 

```
options snd-hda-intel model=laptop-dmic
```

This may break HDMI audio out (I think, **Edit** Maybe not. Can somebody with HDMI check, please?). 

[The full list of HD audio drivers is here]("http://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/sound/alsa/HD-Audio-Models.txt"). Maybe somebody can help me find the right choice to get everything working?

---

### Post by tombh on 2012-04-03
Just to say I've had 10 solid days with my new UX31E running Kubuntu 12.04LTS and I'm extremely happy with it. It's a beautiful laptop and I'm very impressed with its Linux compatibility.

The only minor niggles I've had have been the screen brightness randomly forcing itself to full and 1 (yes just 1) irrecoverable freeze after installing Skype. WiFi reception seems to always report lower than other laptops and smartphones, but is always stable and fast so no complaints.

Battery life is 4-5 hours, doing the usual browsing, coding, etc.

9 out of 10 :)

### Post by mrv on 2012-04-04
Yep, 3.2.0-21 has a suspend problem :( It's probably related to the Bluetooth even though not even unloading the ath3k/btusb modules seem to help.

I added my 20_zenbook experiment to the [bug report](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/969883), but indeed it wasn't enough. I hope that someone can figure out a workaround for the problem, since otherwise the Bluetooth needs to be disabled again.

In other news, 3.2.0-22 seemingly got uploaded now with the pcie_aspm=force fix, which means that enable_aspm scripts will not be needed anymore.

Update: Good news! The suspend problem was not apparently Bluetooth related at all, since with 3.2.0-22 both Bluetooth and suspend work!

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-04-04
> **mrv said:**
> Update: Good news! The suspend problem was not apparently Bluetooth related at all, since with 3.2.0-22 both Bluetooth and suspend work!

Do you still need the suspend scripts to make it work? 

I had a problem with my laptop not going to a "real" suspend mode with 11.10 or 12.04 beta 1. What I mean is that the led on the right side of the machine (power indicator?) was constantly on even after closing the lid (even with the suspend scripts) so been waiting for an "out-of-the-box" fix for the problem before installing again.

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-04-04
> **DeusExMachinae said:**
> Do you still need the suspend scripts to make it work? 

I had a problem with my laptop not going to a "real" suspend mode with 11.10 or 12.04 beta 1. What I mean is that the led on the right side of the machine (power indicator?) was constantly on even after closing the lid (even with the suspend scripts) so been waiting for an "out-of-the-box" fix for the problem before installing again.

It's my understanding that the script is only there to protect memory from damage (which has been reported to happen in a few rare cases). You can suspend and resume without any sleep.d script (on -20 and -22, among other kernels). [Check the Wiki on this]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Suspend").

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-04-04
> **mrv said:**
> Since so many problems start to be solved, I'm trying out enabling VT-d together with i915.semaphores :) I want to try if the wiki information about disabling it is still needed. I work with Zenbook every day so I guess I should hit the random shutdowns eventually.

I believe disabling VT-d isn't needed anymore since deep and deeper RC6 have been deactivated in the kernel.
I haven't had any shutdowns whatsoever since probably kernel 3.3-rc2 or so, when the patch for the different RC6 levels was first released.

Correct me if I'm wrong though, I don't know which patches are integrated into the earlier kernels or Ubuntu kernels.

### Post by Aurélien Jacobs on 2012-04-05
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> After rebooting I just noticed that scrolling on the touchpad is EXTREMELY slow (like one line for a swipe across the whole pad).

I think this is because of an update of xserver-xorg-input-synaptics from (Debian) version 1.5.0-2 to 1.5.99.901-1 (the changelog says something about ClickPad).
Does anyone else have this problem as well, or an idea of how to fix it?

Yes, I had the same problem. Upgrading to xserver-xorg-input-synaptics 1.5.99.902-1 improves the situation, but it still requires you to reduce the value of VertScrollDelta and HorizScrollDelt
For exmple:
```

synclient VertScrollDelta=20 HorizScrollDelta=20

```

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-04-05
> **Aurélien Jacobs said:**

```

synclient VertScrollDelta=20 HorizScrollDelta=20

```

I should mention that I use the Saaros patch for the driver.

### Post by jonjondwillis on 2012-04-05
I've been plagued by sporadic I/O errors that cause my zenbook to freeze, sometimes irrecoverably. It happens almost(?) exclusively when I'm on battery power.

I've enabled ASPM and and ALPM by following the guides on the Wiki and this thread. It seems that my SSD isn't getting enough power when I have SATA Link Power Management set to min_power, so I've disabled it for now. My battery is draining maybe 1-2W faster (~7W currently) than it was without this. I'll report back if stability improves.

Anyone else plagued by disk lockups?

Also, powertop reports some strange top power consumers:

  3.89 W    100.0%                      Device         Radio device: asus-nb-w
  2.60 W    100.0%                      Device         Display backlight
  2.03 W      0.0%                      Device         Display backlight

---

### Post by jonjondwillis on 2012-04-05
I found some useful info on the [ ArchLinux wiki]("https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Asus_Zenbook_UX31E#Additional_powersavings") that recommends:
> 
Do not add the following option to the kernel line
pcie_aspm=force 
if
dmesg | grep -i "acpi fadt"
outputs
ACPI FADT declares the system doesn't support PCIe ASPM, so disable it.

I ran 
dmesg | grep -i "acpi fadt"

and it output 

ACPI FADT declares the system doesn't support PCIe ASPM, so disable it.


Disabling ASPM for now. I'm on kernel 3.4-rc1.

Edit) No crashes so far, and I've almost gone through a full charge cycle.

---

I am having a similar problem but I am using 11.10 with kernel 3.3.1. I can get my machine to suspend and wake again, but when I suspend it (i.e., shut the lid), the power indicator does not start to blink and the cooling fan remains running.

Any suggestions?

Cheers
Darcy

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-04-07
> **webberdar said:**
> Hey there,

I am having a similar problem but I am using 11.10 with kernel 3.3.1. I can get my machine to suspend and wake again, but when I suspend it (i.e., shut the lid), the power indicator does not start to blink and the cooling fan remains running.

Any suggestions?

Cheers
Darcy
I actually just now found out what my problem was. 

I had another script in the in /etc/pm/sleep.d/ folder with some lines to enable 2-finger scrolling on my touchpad when the computer wakes up from suspend. Somehow that broke suspend altogether. 

I also tried writing the touchpad script into the same file where the suspend script is in, but that didn't work either. Most propably there was something wrong with the tpuchpad script. But no matter. I'm happy as long as suspend works properly.

Maybe you have a similar situation going on?

---

### Post by webberdar on 2012-04-07
Hey DeusExMachinae

I think I got it sorted too! I didnt find a script in /etc/pm/sleep.d that has anything to do with two-finger scrolling, but I did find an error in my 20_zenbook script and deleted another called "novatel_3g_suspend" and now it works. Excellent, cheers! 

Now I just need to sort out my touchpad (I cant seem to right click) and my wireless as I get very poor performance and cant get wifi more than a meter away from my router. ### Post by matt2053 on 2012-04-07
> **DeusExMachinae said:**
> I actually just now found out what my problem was. 

I had another script in the in /etc/pm/sleep.d/ folder with some lines to enable 2-finger scrolling on my touchpad when the computer wakes up from suspend. Somehow that broke suspend altogether. 

I also tried writing the touchpad script into the same file where the suspend script is in, but that didn't work either. Most propably there was something wrong with the tpuchpad script. But no matter. I'm happy as long as suspend works properly.

Maybe you have a similar situation going on?

I don't have problems with my touchpad settings coming out of suspend. Try putting them in /etc/X11/xorg.conf.d

For example, I have a file /etc/X11/xorg.conf.d/20_synaptics that contains this:

```
# Example xorg.conf.d snippet that assigns the touchpad driver
# to all touchpads. See xorg.conf.d(5) for more information on
# InputClass.
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE, your distribution will likely overwrite
# it when updating. Copy (and rename) this file into
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf.d first.
# Additional options may be added in the form of
#   Option "OptionName" "value"
#
Section "InputClass"
        Identifier "touchpad catchall"
        Driver "synaptics"
        MatchIsTouchpad "on"
# This option is recommend on all Linux systems using evdev, but cannot be
# enabled by default. See the following link for details:
# http://who-t.blogspot.com/2010/11/how-to-ignore-configuration-errors.html
      MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/event*"
    Option "FingerLow" "5"
    Option "FingerHigh" "15"
    Option "CoastingSpeed" "10"
    Option "CoastingFriction" "25"
    Option "EdgeMotionMinZ" "30"
    Option "EdgeMotionMaxZ" "40"
    Option "EdgeMotionMinSpeed" "100"
    Option "EdgeMotionMaxSpeed" "400"
    Option "ClickPad" "1"
    Option "Palm Detect" "1"
EndSection

Section "InputClass"
        Identifier "touchpad ignore duplicates"
        MatchIsTouchpad "on"
        MatchOS "Linux"
        MatchDevicePath "/dev/input/mouse*"
        Option "Ignore" "on"
EndSection
```Maybe try that. You can tweak the setting values to your liking. Notice I don't have an entry for two-finger scrolling. I simply enabled that in the regular touchpad settings, here, and it works fine (with kernel 3.3):

[IMG]http://i.imgur.com/nuFZt.png[/IMG]

### Post by jonjondwillis on 2012-04-10
> **DeusExMachinae said:**
> My wireless just keeps breaking connection. It's like a bouncer who let's people into the bar and kicks them out right when they've bought their first beer. Annoying as hell. Can't do any internet stuff on the computer, really. Not even download any updates.

How would one go about installing 3.3? Or is there even a remote possibility that it would help?

[http://www.upubuntu.com/2012/03/how-to-install-linux-33-kernel-in.html](http://www.upubuntu.com/2012/03/how-to-install-linux-33-kernel-in.html)

Like so?

Download the headers and image file for the kernel version you want for your CPU architecture from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/)

cd to the directory you downloaded them to. Make sure there's no other .deb files in it. Then:

sudo dpkg -i *.deb

sudo update-grub

reboot


If you've got a sentelic touchpad you probably will need to roll your own kernels. I'm currently on 3.4-rc2 on Linux Mint 12 and I'm getting acceptable WiFi performance. 3.4-rc1 actually had better connectivity, but I had to apply [this patch]("http://marc.info/?l=linux-wireless&m=133282325727326&w=2") or I would get kernel panics when connecting to networks with multiple access points.

### Post by kiirokurisu on 2012-04-10
For all you guys running unstable or custom kernels, have you tried just running the current kernel in 12.04 (3.2.0-22)? On my UX31E it runs perfectly, everything works. My only customisations are kernel boot parameters:
```
pcie_aspm=force elevator=noop drm.vblankoffdelay=1 i915.semaphores=1
```

and a suspend/resume script as per the Ubuntu Wiki entry for the Zenbook. I get extremely low power consumption (<6 watts with 40% screen brightness at idle), wireless performs great, bluetooth works spot-on, and the system is rock-solid stable. I highly recommend this configuration for anyone with a Zenbook. I even have multi-touch working on the touchpad using a DKMS driver for the Sentelic touchpad. If anyone is interested in further info please send me a PM (given the rate of posts in this thread I would probably miss replies easily).

---

### Post by kiirokurisu on 2012-04-11

For those who suffer of unexpected shutdowns...could you please confirm this procedure?

After the bootloader, so in the Ubuntu/Windows login screen:
1. Unplug the asus powercord from the socket
2. Wait until the green led is completely off (we need 2-3 minutes)
3. Plug the asus powercord to the notebook
4. Plug the powercord to the socket

Tested 3 times yesterday/today with no shutdowns.

Please let me know.

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-04-15

What I'm seeing is a signal with a strength of 2/4 bars. It's not the strongest but it works fine. I'm able to download stuff and browse the internet jsut fine. Everything seems ok with no error messages.

[IMG]http://i.imgur.com/4cOhd.png[/IMG]

### Post by housseinireza on 2012-04-16
Hello everybody,
is there a kernel version which solves the suspend problem?
With kernel version 3.2 the suspend functionality is not working and also there are some problems with external monitors. I tried the kernels 3.3, 3.3-rc6 and 3.4-rc2 but the suspend was not working and the bluetooth was not working anymore (which was fine in 3.2).

Someone experienced the same issues and why is there a remark in the wiki that suspend is working?

Cheers,
Reza

---

### Post by jonjondwillis on 2012-04-16
> **housseinireza said:**
> Hello everybody,
is there a kernel version which solves the suspend problem?
With kernel version 3.2 the suspend functionality is not working and also there are some problems with external monitors. I tried the kernels 3.3, 3.3-rc6 and 3.4-rc2 but the suspend was not working and the bluetooth was not working anymore (which was fine in 3.2).

Someone experienced the same issues and why is there a remark in the wiki that suspend is working?

Cheers,
Reza

You may need..

Make executable: /etc/pm/config.d/unload_modules
```

SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci_hcd ehci_hcd uhci_hcd"
```

Make executable: /etc/pm/sleep.d/zenbook_suspend
```

EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1a.0 0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
case "${1}" in
    hibernate|suspend)
        # Switch USB buses off
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/unbind
        done
        #for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
        #    echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/unbind
        #done
        ;;
    resume|thaw)
        # Switch USB buses back on
        for bus in $EHCI_BUSES; do
            echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/ehci_hcd/bind
        done
        #for bus in $XHCI_BUSES; do
        #    echo -n $bus > /sys/bus/pci/drivers/xhci_hcd/bind
        #done
	;;
esac
```

### Post by mrv on 2012-04-18
> **daulpavis said:**
> 
I'm on kernel 3.2.0-23-generic and am looking to compile a new kernel with the Sentelic touchpad drivers. I was wondering, is this kernel name an alias for one of the 3.3 kernels?


Ubuntu's kernel is 3.2 like the name says, although it has many patches that the official 3.2 or 3.3 do not have (for example, RC6 is enabled by default and Zenbook's Bluetooth patch is in - both only in upstream 3.4).

So it's also recommended as the base to build a custom kernel if you need to.

For anyone used to trying out latest mainstream kernels, I'd really recommend to use the official Ubuntu kernel. Also regarding wiki, it should match with what comes out-of-the-box in Ubuntu, not what's the situation after installing custom kernels.

Regarding the WLAN, here's my two cents from the "works just fine" group: for me it also shows just 2/4 or sometimes even 1/4 signal strength, but in reality the speed is just great - basically the full 24Mbps my broadband happens to allow, and more in the internal network. I'm situated something like 5m/15feet away from the router, and the router is inside a metal box and behind a wall. WLAN has also worked for me everywhere I've tried it, but it's true that I've probably not tried multi-router networks.

The WLAN experience is just fine for me - on par with Intel WLAN:s, which I didn't actually expect from Atheros. But apparently ath9k then has still some multi-accesspoint problems according to this thread, and it'd be worth mentioning especially if everyone agrees the problem exists clearly in the multi AP networks but not in single AP network.

(FWIW, I do have two separate WLAN networks, but with different ESSIDs)

### Post by pipposcollo on 2012-04-20
> **jonjondwillis said:**
> You may need..

Make executable: /etc/pm/config.d/unload_modules
```

SUSPEND_MODULES="xhci_hcd ehci_hcd uhci_hcd"
```Make executable: /etc/pm/sleep.d/zenbook_suspend
```

EHCI_BUSES="0000:00:1a.0 0000:00:1d.0"
XHCI_BUSES="0000:03:00.0"
...

```
The rest is codewise the same as recommended in the Wiki page (the additional code lines here relate to XHCI but are commented out). However, there are differences, since the recommended workaround:

[LIST=1]
[*]has "20_zenbook" rather than"zenbook_suspend" as filename;
[*]does not mention the executable "unload_modules" file.
[/LIST]
My first question is whether these differences are of any significance and, if so, which of the two is the effective workaround?

Second question: I've just installed the latest 12.04 Beta 2 (64bit, with 3.2.0-23 kernel) on a brand new ux21, and almost everything works fine. However, while suspend seems to work flawlessly, there's no "hibernate" entry in the topright dropdown menu, nor is this an option upon shutdown selection therein. I wonder whether this is intended (maybe it would be due to some of the recommended optimizations, since saving to RAM rather than SSD would make the latter live longer... does this make sense?), or is it a fault instead?

```

shutin@skyline-gtr:~$ iwconfig
lo        no wireless extensions.

wlan0     IEEE 802.11bgn  ESSID:"Warecorp"  
          Mode:Managed  Frequency:2.462 GHz  Access Point: 58:6D:8F:98:01:F3   
          Bit Rate=52 Mb/s   Tx-Power=16 dBm   
          Retry  long limit:7   RTS thr:off   Fragment thr:off
          Power Management:on
          Link Quality=30/70  Signal level=-80 dBm  
          Rx invalid nwid:0  Rx invalid crypt:0  Rx invalid frag:0
          Tx excessive retries:1  Invalid misc:57   Missed beacon:0

```
The same AC has "perfect" connection status in Win7, but has only 2\4 in Ubuntu 12.04 

Kernels is 3.2.0-23

Try disabling the power management for wlan0 :

sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off

and if a single AP then set the BSSID through editing the network settings for that specific AP. I got better performance on my system that way but your mileage may vary. 

I also experimented with settings in my AP and set encryption to WPA AES and set the channel to a specific channel that had less noise from the neighbours AP.

Running ping to my router would give me initially a very high latency and slight packet loss then it would "calm" down to a low latency with no packet loss. I agree with SorryGofish that the icon on top panel is not the most effective way to judge wifi strength I would rely more on the output from iwconfig

---

### Post by scottro on 2012-04-26
I've just put in the latest Precise on this.  Upon installation, Sentelic touchpad wasn't seen by synclient (nor did I have various options in system configuration menu to disable while typing and so on.)  However, I updated immediately after install, and upon reboot, it's seen by synclient.  

Wireless is working for me--I'm not very far from the router, but it seems fine, so far. 

Power, at default settings, is varying between 4-5 hours.  

So, for me at least, some of the problems have been solved.

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-04-27
Hey dudes/dudettes and everything between. Just took the dive and removed Windows 7 completely from my Zenbook. Ubuntu only now.

So I installed the now released 12.04 LTS from an USB-drive, made the minor changes to fix suspend mode, right clicking and... 

...what the hell...? This thing works! I mean REALLY works!

No problems with the WiFi as of yet. That was a very big problem in beta 2. I don't know what's changed. Maybe the new kernel?

I think that considering the hardware everything works. I don't know about Bluetooth or HDMI output or some other stuff, cause I don't really have any use for that.

Every piece of software in this thing is f*n awesome. Nothing that comes with the installation is pointless. The problem with every Windows machine I've bought is the bloatware, but with an Ubuntu installation you get what you want, and more. And if you need anything else, just pop open your software center and there you go. I believe every one of you had a look of disbelieve on your face when you first started your UX31 and saw all the sh*t that Asus had preinstalled on your precious laptop. Got to love Canonical and the Linux community for being... awesome.

Now I truly feel the ZEN in the Zenbook. Thank you. That is all.

---

### Post by kiirokurisu on 2012-04-28
The latest kernel in Precise (3.2.0-24) has brought with it a backported multitouch driver for the Sentelic touchpads, but the behaviour is somewhat erratic. I have opened a bug ([990260]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/990260")) for this issue; anyone else experiencing erratic touchpad behaviour should check the bug and add any extra info you think might be useful.

### Post by esodin on 2012-05-01
> **djve said:**
> I'm a newbie to Ubuntu. I used to go for Gentoo but work supplied me with an MacBook for the last 4 years so I'm a little out of what's been happening in linux.

I have Zenbook and it's running the 12.04 Beta 2 but, like most people, I want a higher resolution than the default 96dpi. I know it can do 132dpi.

$ xdpyinfo |grep resolution
resolution:    132x132 dots per inch

xrandr doesn't update the resolution.  I tried `xrandr --output eDP1 --dpi 132/eDP1` and got no errors but nothing happened. Trying `xrandr --screen 0:0 --dpi 132/eDP1` gave the same results. The command `xrandr -display eDP1 --dpi 132/eDP1` gives "Can't open display eDP1" and I expected this as there is no Xorg.conf file in /etc/X11.

Can someone hit me with a clue stick as to what I'm missing? `xrdb -query` stays at "Xft.dpi: 96" no matter what I try.

Thanks

D.

The 132 screen resolution works for me. See below. 

```
#######:~$ xrandr --output eDP1 --dpi 132/eDP1
#######:~$ xdpyinfo |grep resolution
  resolution:    132x132 dots per inch
#######:~$ xdpyinfo |grep resolution
  resolution:    132x132 dots per inch
#######:~$ uname -r
3.2.0-24-generic
#######:~$ 

```

Sorry I can not tell you why it works but hope it offers you some hope. ;)

New topics (moving down the priority list)

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-05-04

I actually have had no disconnects in a couple of days either. *knock on wood*

Sometimes the connection slows down considerably, though. It's pretty erratic.

---

### Post by rodpott on 2012-05-05
> **DeusExMachinae said:**
> I actually have had no disconnects in a couple of days either. *knock on wood*

Sometimes the connection slows down considerably, though. It's pretty erratic.

I had the same problem. After i changed some setting on my AP the connection-problems are gone.

I setup my AP with a different channel and set the channel width to 40MHz (before 20Mhz)

This settings speed up the connection up to 7MB/sec when you are in the same room.

My problem is that the reach of the zenbook is much worse than my dell xps14z. At the same place i have about 1,5MB/sec compared with 500KB/sec with the zenbook.

---

### Post by wildmttrout on 2012-05-06
Considering either the UX31 or UX 21. Thoughts? 

If I understand correctly the UX31 has the I5-2557M where the 21 has the I5-2467M. The 2557 processor seems to be a little stronger and a little more stable. This makes me lean 31.

Now, I run 10.10. I really like the interface and I am not really excited about moving to Unity. However, I noticed a few posts about 12.04 running GNOME3 with the classic interface which looks almost exactly like the 10.10 GUI. First off, is this correct? If so, does the UX31/21 run this smoothly?

Next, what kernel works the best? I have saw a few posts outlining different kernels, however it seems confusing which one to use. 3.2.14? or 3.3rc5? Bluetooth is not a deal-breaker for me. Touchpad, WIFI, and battery life are much more important. 

BTW, what kind of battery life is everyone getting? I've seen 5-6 hrs. Is this the norm?

Next, I have read over the Wiki a little, however is there anything (besides the hibernate/resume script) I should be familiarizing myself with? 

Note, I am not a noob, however I am not an expert with Linux coding either. I code python, R, and TCL/TK mainly so it is slightly foreign to me. However, I am very willing to learn and very willing to do the work to make the machine the best it can be. I rather enjoy learning to fix issues, however I often need guidance.

Thanks!!!

### Post by arune on 2012-05-07
Hello
I got myself UX31 a few weeks ago, now running 12.04 and it works great. To get Wifi stop dropping out I could disable powersaving but another way was to add the BSSID to network configuration AND [disable IPv6]("http://www.upubuntu.com/2011/05/how-to-disable-ipv6-under-ubuntu.html"). No more dropouts since then BUT the range is not satisfying.

> **SorryGoFish said:**
> 
New topics (moving down the priority list) 

How long does your battery last when it's suspended? Mine drains much faster than advertised, I'd say it looses an hour of battery for every 8 hours it's asleep.

Yes, the battery drains fast in suspend! I have not measured but it drains much faster than my (7 years) old laptop.

> **PeterFeicht said:**

### Post by rodpott on 2012-05-09

Damyan
 
Hi damyan, i had a similiar issue on my Dell XPS 14z. Maybe this will help you to solve it for the moment or to let you search a solution. Pleas notice that the steps are for ubuntu:
```
 
I also had the following problem: "Computer hibernates when unplugged from powersource"
 
Here are the steps to handle this for the XPS 14z:
 
1. sudo apt-get install dconf-tools
2. dconf-editor
3. Navigate to the 'org.gnome.settings-daemon.plugins.power' section
4. Uncheck 'use-time-for-policy'

```

### Post by marcio_mi on 2012-05-09
> **gnome2379 said:**
> Help please!

I have UX31 with Precise Pangolion. I had no issues with it until yesterday. I never used the hibernate or suspend mode, I used to power down when it is not in use. Yesterday the system prompted me to update patches (around 26 of them). Post the upgrade, I restarted my laptop and post the Grub selection and around the time the desktop loads the system blanked out for long time (it normally loads in couple of seconds).

I forced a shutdown and tried to restart but this time my laptop refused to even load the ASUS flash screen. I could only see a blank screen with power and caps light on!!

Is there a way I can restore my system back? Any help is appreciated.

Regards

I just had this issue two days ago, even though it happened in a different way. For me it was that the computer was kept in suspend mode for too long (maybe putting the battery in a very low charge state). after that the screen was completely black and only the power light and the caps lock light were on (the laptop was warming up, hence something else must have been active too)

My solution was: I left the laptop in this state until the battery was completely drained out (it might take several hours), then plugged in the charger, and magically the computer restarted. I don't know whether it would work also in your case, and you decide whether it is something you also want to try.

For me, it avoided a talk with ASUS support, which they say it's not the best around...

### Post by gnome2379 on 2012-05-09
Thanks for the replies.

I had called up the ASUS support and they want to take the laptop back to check. As long as they can get the laptop back in a working condition I will be glad.

I have been using Ubuntu for long now (even though am just a novice) I have had problems with losing grub or screwing up my partition but as long as the system started up I was able to use a spare PC and get to the forum to make it work.. but this one is dead as dead.

Any reason what could have caused this - was it the battery, bios not being up to date or ...

Some info on my installation:

1) I had a dual-boot with win 7 and did not remove the asus back up/recovery partition.

2) I have not updated the BIOS because when i tried to locate the files on the usb drive, system reported those files were older than what was installed (the files were named with some numbers like 201 or 209.. instead of dates!)

3) I did not use the script modification advised on the ubuntu site for asus to help from suspend/hibernation. I tend to power down the ux31 when it is not in use.

4) I used the scripts to make the touchpad respond to right clicks and use it as a clickpad (using xinput to find the id for the elantech touchpad thingy and use it as input parameter for the scripts i downloaded)

all of this was working fine until yesterday.. I did not make any other changes except agreeing to install the patches and the laptop was running on battery that time (I did not notice it to be that low it was pretty well charged)

Is there any way I can boot in to a USB (live-usb) and run some commands and post the output here to see what could have possibly gone wrong? (before I could pack this off to the support) may be it would give the developers some info on what caused the crash?

### Post by vivace on 2012-05-11
Hi

Since i upgraded to 12.04 my touchpad script in rc.local is not working. I always get: 

```
X Error of failed request:  BadValue (integer parameter out of range for operation)
```

not sure what could be the problem?

```
#!/bin/sh -e
#
# rc.local
#
# This script is executed at the end of each multiuser runlevel.
# Make sure that the script will "exit 0" on success or any other
# value on error.
#
# In order to enable or disable this script just change the execution
# bits.
#
# By default this script does nothing.

# higher sensitivity
synclient FingerLow=9 FingerHigh=12

# faster movements
synclient MinSpeed=1.5 MaxSpeed=3.5 AccelFactor=0.1

```

### Post by wildmttrout on 2012-05-11
I simply cannot get my touchpad the way I want it. The palm detect is giving me fits. Every time to try to type, my cursor jumps around horribly. Also, it seems to be much less sensitive than I would like. Any ideas? 
BTW, this is the Elantech touchpad.

```
#!/bin/sh

# higher sensitivity
synclient FingerLow=2 FingerHigh=5

# faster movements
synclient MinSpeed=0.25 MaxSpeed=4.0 AccelFactor=0.15

# 2 fingers scroll
synclient VertTwoFingerScroll=1 HorizTwoFingerScroll=1

# faster coasting
synclient CoastingSpeed=10 CoastingFriction=25

# enable tap to click (2 fingers for middle click, 3 fingers for right click)
synclient TapButton1=1 TapButton2=3 TapButton3=2

# faster tap and double tap
synclient FastTaps=1 MaxDoubleTapTime=100

# continue dragging movement when reaching the edge of the touchpad
synclient EdgeMotionMinZ=30 EdgeMotionMaxZ=40 EdgeMotionMinSpeed=100 EdgeMotionMaxSpeed=400

# Click with multiple buttons
synclient ClickFinger1=1 ClickFinger2=3 ClickFinger3=2

# Enable palm detection
synclient PalmDetect=1
synclient PalmMinWidth=3 
synclient PalmMinZ=80
```

Also, according to conky, my processor is not using turbo boost. Any ideas here?

analyzing CPU 0:
  driver: acpi-cpufreq

  hardware limits: 800 MHz - 1.60 GHz

```

Ok, so according to turbostat, the CPU is capable to turbo boost however I am not convinced it is actually using this technology in Ubuntu.
```
GenuineIntel 13 CPUID levels; family:model:stepping 0x6:2a:7 (6:42:7)
8 * 100 = 800 MHz max efficiency
16 * 100 = 1600 MHz TSC frequency
20 * 100 = 2000 MHz max turbo 4 active cores
20 * 100 = 2000 MHz max turbo 3 active cores
20 * 100 = 2000 MHz max turbo 2 active cores
23 * 100 = 2300 MHz max turbo 1 active cores

```

Could it be possible that Conky or Cpufreq-info is not registering the turbo boost frequency but the cpu is actually boosting to these speeds (2300 MHz)?

---

### Post by aspettl on 2012-05-13
> **wildmttrout said:**
> It does seem the processor is not using turbo-boost. Below is the output from cpufreq-info

```
cpufrequtils 007: cpufreq-info (C) Dominik Brodowski 2004-2009
Report errors and bugs to cpufreq@vger.kernel.org, please.
analyzing CPU 0:
  driver: acpi-cpufreq
  CPUs which run at the same hardware frequency: 0 1 2 3
  CPUs which need to have their frequency coordinated by software: 0
  maximum transition latency: 10.0 us.
  hardware limits: 800 MHz - 1.60 GHz
  available frequency steps: 1.60 GHz, 1.60 GHz, 1.50 GHz, 1.40 GHz, 1.30 GHz, 1.20 GHz, 1.10 GHz, 1000 MHz, 900 MHz, 800 MHz
  available cpufreq governors: conservative, ondemand, userspace, powersave, performance

```
There are two "1.60 GHz" steps, one is 1.6 GHz + 1 Mhz, which afaik means the CPU uses Turbo Boost.

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2012-05-13
Hey dudes. I managed to resolve my WiFi problems permanently. Haven't had any lagging or disconnects in a cuople of days or so. 

What I did was use one of the possible workarounds that are described in the "Appendix: Older information" section of the wiki. 

I had to remove network-manager and install [wicd]("http://wicd.sourceforge.net/moinmoin/Wicd%20on%20Ubuntu"). I also had to manually enable the tray icons in Unity ([see here]("http://askubuntu.com/questions/69005/wicd-tray-icon-doesnt-show")). This worked for me, maybe it'll work for you too.

### Post by aspettl on 2012-05-15
> **vivace said:**
> Is there something that happened so that syncclient wont work from /etc/rc.local anymore?
X has to be running for synclient to work - maybe rc.local is executed too early? Anyway, I think it is more appropriate to set these things on a per-user basis, i.e., by calling the script without superuser rights using gnome-session-properties or the gsettings input-devices "hotplug-command" key [1], which was suggested earlier in this thread.

[1] ```
gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.peripherals.input-devices hotplug-command /home/...
```

### Post by MikeBraxner on 2012-05-22
Has anyone checked if TRIM is *actually working* on the UX31's SanDisk's SSD U100?

For a clean installation of Kubuntu 12.04, I followed the [[COLOR="DarkOrange"]general suggestions on using SSD-drives[/COLOR]]("https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Solid_State_Drives") under linux, i.e. UUID/GUID partitioning via gdisk with proper alignment, leading 2MB Bios-boot part, proper fstab-flags (noatime, nodiratime, discard), scheduler to noop, etc. pp.

[[COLOR="DarkOrange"]Rob Williams[/COLOR]]("http://techgage.com/article/enabling_and_testing_ssd_trim_support_under_linux/2") pointed out that ...
> If the SSD in question does in fact support TRIM, and 'discard' is properly added to the /etc/fstab file, then it can be assumed that TRIM is in fact working. But for assurance, there's a simple test that can be performed...

Because TRIM purges all data found in affected 'sectors' after a file is deleted, the result should be sectors that read a bunch of 0s, not some sort of data ... On a non-TRIM drive, data found in a sector would remain the same even after the file is deleted.

So I followed his suggested sequence ...
```
seq 1 1000 > testfile
sync
hdparm --fibmap testfile
hdparm --read-sector ['begin_LBA' from prev. output] /dev/sda
rm testfile
sync
hdparm --read-sector ['begin_LBA' from prev. output] /dev/sda
```

... which should result in all 0's, IF TRIM works --- except that I keep getting an exact replicate of the first 'hdparm --read-sector' output. Subsequent sync's, and even reboots, make no difference whatsoever. 

So, according to all general instructions TRIM should work with a simple 'discard', yet the only test I can find keeps insisting that it doesn't. Is there something I just don't get? Does this work on your machine, or do you get the same result? Does anyone know of a different test?

---

### Post by yaronv99 on 2012-05-22

and found out that my Sentelic touch pad works!!
It now recognized as a touchpad and not just as a mouse.
So i can scroll with 2 fingers or at the sides of the pad.

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-05-22
@MikeBraxner:

I don't think that data in sectors is actually erased, that would be a completely unnecessary write.
Furthermore, if you read an unallocated sector from an SSD, you get a random sector and not the one that used to be there, since physical sectors are assigned logical sectors only if they are written (see wear leveling).

---

### Post by MikeBraxner on 2012-05-22
Peter, 
I'm grateful for the quick response, albeit having to disagree with two assertions.
> **PeterFeicht said:**
>  if you read an unallocated sector from an SSD, you get a random sector and not the one that used to be there

I fail to see how this could hold, since the second call of the above mentioned "hdparm --read-sector", i.e. *post*-delete, produces the **identical result** to the first call, i.e. *pre*-delete, even after a cold reboot, which guarantees that no buffer/cache could possibly be involved. 
Hence, the referenced LBA/sector read *is* (one of) the LBAs/sector(s) designated deleted. While your comment probably applies to OS/Filesystem assignation of *logical* sectors, if I understand the command-sequence correctly, the read access does not rely on these, so the concern is void. 

> **PeterFeicht said:**
> I don't think that data in sectors is actually erased, that would be a completely unnecessary write.

Actually, the [Wiki]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TRIM") points out that...
> a TRIM command allows an operating system to inform a solid-state drive (SSD) which blocks of data are no longer considered in use and can be **wiped internally** ... The command **irreversibly deletes** the data it affects.


In other words, the data is *supposed* to be 'gone', leaving behind nothing more than "sectors that read a bunch of 0s, not some sort of data" as [Williams]("http://techgage.com/article/enabling_and_testing_ssd_trim_support_under_linux/2") pointed out. This is the very core of the TRIM-test.

Did you try to run the suggested command sequence? Did you get a different result?

---

### Post by PeterFeicht on 2012-05-23
> **MikeBraxner said:**
> Peter, 
I'm grateful for the quick response, albeit having to disagree with two assertions.


I fail to see how this could hold, since the second call of the above mentioned "hdparm --read-sector", i.e. *post*-delete, produces the **identical result** to the first call, i.e. *pre*-delete, even after a cold reboot, which guarantees that no buffer/cache could possibly be involved. 
Hence, the referenced LBA/sector read *is* (one of) the LBAs/sector(s) designated deleted. While your comment probably applies to OS/Filesystem assignation of *logical* sectors, if I understand the command-sequence correctly, the read access does not rely on these, so the concern is void. 



Actually, the [Wiki]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TRIM") points out that...


In other words, the data is *supposed* to be 'gone', leaving behind nothing more than "sectors that read a bunch of 0s, not some sort of data" as [Williams]("http://techgage.com/article/enabling_and_testing_ssd_trim_support_under_linux/2") pointed out. This is the very core of the TRIM-test.

Did you try to run the suggested command sequence? Did you get a different result?

I didn't run the commands, what I wrote is indeed not to be considered fact, since I don't know much about how SSDs work internally.

As I understand it though (after reading the Wikipedia page) the TRIM command affects data  when garbage collection occurs on the SSD and not earlier. So one would have to trigger a GC in order to see changes.
Also this may be different from one SSD to another. I wouldn't worry too much about it...

Now, when I plug in the external display, I get a screen full of orange color on the external display, and the laptop display turns off. The only way to get things back is to force turn off the laptop and restart. Unplugging/replugging monitor doesn't work, and seems like ubuntu crashes.

Any suggestions?

### Post by MikeBraxner on 2012-05-24
> **PeterFeicht said:**
> So one would have to trigger a GC in order to see changes.
Also this may be different from one SSD to another. I wouldn't worry too much about it...

Don't worry? How do tell water not to be wet!? Anyway, on reading your comment, I figured that I did *not* know **when** the U100 triggered its' garbage collection. Discussions generally just sugested 'wait a few minutes' --- which I did to no avail. 

So, just for fit and giggles, I dug through my history until I found the first TRIM test I ran, and re-read the according LBA ... all 0's!! Apparently, the U100 doesn't run its garbage collection on a minute scale, but something significantly larger, but TRIM **definitely** works properly. Thanks for the help, Peter.

You really can't want to wait for the new UX31**A**? Should have (even) better display and better keyboard, probably also better wifi.

> **earthpigg said:**
> Overall, is [this documentation]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook") about accurate once 12.04 has been installed and fully updated?

Specifically, is [this]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Suspend.2C_Hibernate.2C_Shut-down_and_Reboot") the only hack that needs to be applied to have everything basically working?

Rather accurate, except for wifi, many ppl (me including) reports bad reception, bad transfer rates, no 5GHz and need minor hacks to get it to stop disconnecting (I disabled IPv6 and added BSSID in connection manager).

Regards

---

### Post by Oblong_Cheese on 2012-05-28
> **arune said:**
> You really can't want to wait for the new UX31**A**? Should have (even) better display and better keyboard, probably also better wifi.



Rather accurate, except for wifi, many ppl (me including) reports bad reception, bad transfer rates, no 5GHz and need minor hacks to get it to stop disconnecting (I disabled IPv6 and added BSSID in connection manager).

Regards

I would like to point out that the AR9485-WB WiFi chipset in the UX21/31 does not support 5Ghz WiFi radio frequency. [http://www.qca.qualcomm.com/networking/brand.php?brand=3&product=105](http://www.qca.qualcomm.com/networking/brand.php?brand=3&product=105)

It is not a Linux kernel problem; it doesn't work in Windows, or any operating system. The hardware is not physically capable! :-)

---

### Post by mrv on 2012-05-29
> **earthpigg said:**
> 
Specifically, is [this]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Suspend.2C_Hibernate.2C_Shut-down_and_Reboot") the only hack that needs to be applied to have everything basically working?

Yes. Also that should be apparently soon resolved, the kernel currently in 12.04 LTS's "proposed" updates (3.2.0-25.40) should have a fix so that you wouldn't need also that hack. I haven't yet tried it, though. The same update will apparently fix external microphone detection, so the "!" on the wiki page for that could be removed later.

Additionally, to get all out of Zenbook's battery you will probably want to do the power management tricks mentioned (kernel parameters + ALPM). It's pretty good also without, but better with those.

And if you get one with Sentelic touchpad instead of Elantech touchpad, you'd need 3.4 kernel from eg. [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.4-precise/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v3.4-precise/) to have proper touchpad functions like two finger scrolling.

---

### Post by Dan Again on 2012-06-06
So, I'm having an issue where my battery never shows 100% and someone in another thread mentioned it may be an issue with the BIOS. I saw in the Community Documentation that there were BIOS updates, but since I've never updated BIOS, I just skipped that stepped.

What will updating my BIOS accomplish?

I'm also terrified I am going to royally screw up my beloved new ultrabook. Is this the correct method to update BIOS...
[LIST]
[*]Go to [http://support.asus.com/download/download_item_mkt.aspx?model=UX31E](http://support.asus.com/download/download_item_mkt.aspx?model=UX31E)
[*]Select "Others" from the dropdown menu
[*]Download the latest BIOS (212) using the "Global (DLM)" link
[*]Extract the downloaded zip file to a freshly FAT formatted USB drive
[*]Reboot into BIOS and go into the BIOS update utility; select the file from the flash drive
[/LIST]
That's as far as I got because I didn't want to screw anything up. Am I on the wrong track?

### Post by nixpro on 2012-06-06
> **Dan Again said:**
> 
  Is this the correct method to update BIOS...
[LIST]
[*]Go to [http://support.asus.com/download/download_item_mkt.aspx?model=UX31E](http://support.asus.com/download/download_item_mkt.aspx?model=UX31E)
[*]Select "Others" from the dropdown menu
[*]Download the latest BIOS (212) using the "Global (DLM)" link
[*]Extract the downloaded zip file to a freshly FAT formatted USB drive
[*]Reboot into BIOS and go into the BIOS update utility; select the file from the flash drive
[/LIST]


In my experience (YMMV), step four need to be done just on a FAT formatted USB drive, not necessarily empty. The BIOS flash utility is smart enough to let you select the file. Also, the computer will have to be connected to the AC adapter for the operation to proceed.

Nobody can guarantee that this procedure will be 100% trouble-free always, but  it has worked flawlessly for me four times before. I am currently on BIOS version 212 (ASUS fixed the somewhat short delay when powering off the computer on pressing the power button) on an  UX31E originally shipped with BIOS version 206.

Asl always, caution and common sense are strongly recommended.

---

### Post by Dan Again on 2012-06-07

> In my experience (YMMV), step four need to be done just on a FAT formatted USB drive, not necessarily empty. The BIOS flash utility is smart enough to let you select the file. Also, the computer will have to be connected to the AC adapter for the operation to proceed.

Nobody can guarantee that this procedure will be 100% trouble-free always, but  it has worked flawlessly for me four times before. I am currently on BIOS version 212 (ASUS fixed the somewhat short delay when powering off the computer on pressing the power button) on an  UX31E originally shipped with BIOS version 206.

Asl always, caution and common sense are strongly recommended.

The method I described worked fine. Thanks for getting back to me.

### Post by tombh on 2012-06-12
I just found this advice ([https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook](https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook)) about setting the BSSID of your wireless connection to the same value as your router's MAC address. Has this been discussed on this thread before? Has anyone had any success with it?

That page also provides an Oneiric package for a patched version of network-manager that prevents network scanning whilst connected, which apparently prevents WiFi drop outs.

Shouldn't all this be on the Wiki if it works?

### Post by aspettl on 2012-06-13
> **tombh said:**
> I just found this advice ([https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook](https://launchpad.net/~aspettl/+archive/zenbook)) about setting the BSSID of your wireless connection to the same value as your router's MAC address. Has this been discussed on this thread before? Has anyone had any success with it?

That page also provides an Oneiric package for a patched version of network-manager that prevents network scanning whilst connected, which apparently prevents WiFi drop outs.

Shouldn't all this be on the Wiki if it works?
Yes, this has been discussed some time ago, that should be around end of last year / new year.
Setting the BSSID is the better solution to avoid the periodic stalls of the network connection (I created those packages to avoid the periodic scanning for networks before the BSSID solution was known). Both solutions disable roaming, which is required for company or university networks e.g. [eduroam](http://www.eduroam.org/). They are listed on the Wiki page, but under "Appendix: Older information".

I'm not sure whether the periodic network stalls (without specific BSSID) appear under 12.04 - didn't test that yet - can someone confirm?
With 11.10, I could see frequent disconnects with dmesg and e.g. SSH sessions were stuck for a short time.

Regards
Aaron

---

### Post by jeppebundsgaard on 2012-06-13
> **mdescharles said:**
> Hi,

I have a blank screen and i can't restart my ux31e with ubuntu 12.04 after an hibernation. 

What can I do for that ? I'va already unplugged the battery but don't do nothing.

Thank you for your help.

Did you try to press the power button and hold it down? If this doesn't work, I guess you have the same problem as I and a lot of others have had: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11767101&highlight=dead#post11767101](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11767101&highlight=dead#post11767101) - see also [http://forum.notebookreview.com/asus/637772-ux31-completely-dead-help.html#](http://forum.notebookreview.com/asus/637772-ux31-completely-dead-help.html#)

Sometimes it resolves itself if you keep on the power and make the battery discharge completely... 

I have had the computer in for repair three times... 

Best regards,
Jeppe

### Post by dancat on 2012-06-18
> **sojusnik said:**
> Did somebody test Ubuntu 12.04 with the new UX31A (Ivy Bridge!) aka Zenbook Prime?

I did. The same issues with the UX21A mentioned above were present. Add to this the fact that the keyboard light stops after the laptop wakes from sleep.

In general, it works just fine. I have none of the wifi issues mentioned by nexero. The startup and wake from sleep time is very small (i think this is expected given the fact that is uses an SSD).

Now waiting for some drivers update to have the keyboard shortcuts and light to work properly.

Edit: One strange thing was that at the installation (i used the automatic one letting the installer deleting everything and create new partitions) grub was installed on the USB key and not on the SSD. I had to manually install it using grub-install.

### Post by dpinna on 2012-06-20
So I find myself in a very peculiar situation.
I recently upgraded my kernel to 3.5-rc3 using instructions on 

[http://fenski.pl/2012/02/quick-howto-for-the-most-stable-linux-kernel-with-zenbook/](http://fenski.pl/2012/02/quick-howto-for-the-most-stable-linux-kernel-with-zenbook/)

Everything seems to be working fine (I even got the Saaros patch to work) except for one thing. My trackpad is frozen at startup.

But here's the really interesting thing, if I suspend the laptop and turn it back on, trackpad works perfectly along with two finger scrolling etc...

Does anyone have any clue about how this might be happening. It's as if the computer registers the trackpad only after suspending the laptop first.

Thanks for any help,
Daniele

### Post by lmarso on 2012-06-24
running a new asus ux31a under 12.10.  

much better than under 12.04 (i never tried custom kernel).

i have working suspend, right button mouse.  trackpad much better.

with:

synclient TapButton1=1 TapButton2=3 TapButton3=2

executed in the shell, I get left, right and middle click with 1, 2 and 3 finger tap.  nice.  two single finger taps allow drag and drop -- requires somewhat precision timing but about 90% of the time i can hit it on the first try.

unfortunately the lcd panel backlight is stuck at '5' and echoing other values into the usual places has no effect.  (lcd panel brightness control worked fine in 12.04).  and keyboard illumination stuck on.  these are about the only issues.  note that suspend meets my needs; i have no interest in hibernation so haven't tried it.  bootup from full off is something like 15 seconds.  incredible.

i'm happy to answer any questions, and would be grateful to hear any suggestions that might get lcd panel brightness working.  

btw, this is the most beautiful screen i have ever seen.

---

### Post by GodzillaMonster on 2012-06-25
> **dpinna said:**
> So I tried reverting to the 3.4.3 kernel to test it out. You were right regarding not needing the sentelic patch. I must say that the two finger scrolling is glitchier that in 3.5-rc3.

AND.... I still have the same weird problem. My mouse is frozen on startup. I can only get it to work if I let the laptop suspend and turn it back on.

Does anyone have any idea what could be causing this?

Not sure of your history with the UX31, but earlier experimentations in this thread turned off the tap-to-click functionality by adding lines to /etc/rc.local by echoing 0x90 to the touch device. 

With the sentelic driver now in the kernel, this echo will actually stop your touchpad working... it will restart properly after a suspend though.

So if you have played with that in the past, it might be worth checking /etc/rc.local (or any other startup scripts you might have done this in)

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-07-03
> **tazdevel said:**
> Forgive me if this has been mentioned elsewhere on this thread but I didn't see it.  I know there are issues with the wireless speed but has anyone seen issues with the wired Ethernet download speeds?

Every system I have at my house I am getting about 35-40Mb down and 30-35Mb up.  Except for my UX31.  It is getting the same speed for upload but my download speeds are only about 3-5Mb at best.  Is anyone else experiencing the same or have any ideas how to fix it?

I am running Ubuntu 12.04 w/ kernel 3.2.0-26

Looks like I have the same problem.

I'm on a really fast university connection. My desktop gets 580Mbps down, and 60Mbps up. 

My UX21 gets 7.3Mbps down, and 56Mbps up (on both USB 2 and USB 3 sockets).

My [FONT="Courier New"]dmesg[/FONT] is spammed with the "[FONT="Courier New"]eth0: asix_rx_fixup() Bad Header Length[/FONT]" messages during my speed-test runs.

This message has been noted in this thread many times, I think. 

**EDIT** [This bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/947723") seems related, and one comment even claims a fix noted in [our wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook").

**EDIT 2** I can confirm that the official driver does remove the Bad Header messages, though my speed is not greatly increased (at around 9Mbps down).

### Post by ToJa92 on 2012-07-13
