I don't have a special key for F4. What's this supposed to do for you?

A good hint for you is to look a the tool [FONT="Courier New"]xev[/FONT]. Run it in a terminal. It will show all sorts of input information. When running press [FONT="Courier New"]FN+C[/FONT] and just [FONT="Courier New"]C[/FONT] (for example) and compare the difference:

For reference, for FN+C I see:
```
KeyRelease event, serial 28, synthetic NO, window 0x1800001,
    root 0xa8, subw 0x0, time 155024, (55,117), root:(703,138),
    state 0x0, keycode 156 (keysym 0x1008ff41, XF86Launch1), same_screen YES,
    XLookupString gives 0 bytes: 
    XFilterEvent returns: False
```
and for just C I see:
```
KeyPress event, serial 28, synthetic NO, window 0x1800001,
    root 0xa8, subw 0x0, time 348960, (-340,56), root:(308,77),
    state 0x0, keycode 31 (keysym 0x63, c), same_screen YES,
    XLookupString gives 1 bytes: (63) "c"
    XmbLookupString gives 1 bytes: (63) "c"
    XFilterEvent returns: False
```

See that it says [FONT="Courier New"]XF86Launch1[/FONT]?

---

In an attempt to get ome reliable wifi, I upgraded my ux31 to the last quantal kernel (as advised by the wiki).

I think this recommandation should be removed or at least modified since this kernel broke the sound and slow quite a lot the boot process. Also, now the wifi doesn't experience any disconnection but also it's sower than before.

I just installed the 3.5.0-5-generic kernel (using the suggested PPA from the Wiki as you pointed out). My sound still works. Boot up was speedy too.

Nothing to report yet on the WiFi. I typically have lots of trouble though.

**Edit** I just walked around my building and watched it hop from one access point to another all without dropping my connection. I never once saw the infamous reason=3 message either. Definitely encouraging.

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-07-27
> **Nosferax said:**
> I have the UX31E with Elantech touchpad. Everything works fine, except when my computer goes to sleep, sometimes upon waking up the multitouch is gone (well almost, only the scrolling and tapping... the clickpad with 2-3 fingers still works). I can fix it by logging out and back in. Anyone else experience this? 

Happened to me for many different kernels, including 3.2.0.24-25-26-27, 3.3.0, 3.4.0...

Where are you setting these preferences? It's best to set them in a file in [FONT="Courier New"]/usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/[/FONT]. If you're making calls to [FONT="Courier New"]synclient[/FONT], your preferences will not be persistent.

For reference, here's a [link]("http://pastebin.com/mydf8eCx") to my [FONT="Courier New"]/usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/51-zenbook-synaptics.conf[/FONT].

### Post by Ben31 on 2012-07-31
> **Nosferax said:**
> I have the UX31E with Elantech touchpad. Everything works fine, except when my computer goes to sleep, sometimes upon waking up the multitouch is gone (well almost, only the scrolling and tapping... the clickpad with 2-3 fingers still works). I can fix it by logging out and back in. Anyone else experience this? 

Happened to me for many different kernels, including 3.2.0.24-25-26-27, 3.3.0, 3.4.0...

It seems that gnome is resetting touchpad parameters when resuming from sleep. 
If you have a script setting your touchpad parameters, you can force gnome to execute it everytime it wakes up with this command:

```
$ gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.peripherals.input-devices hotplug-command "/absolute_path_to_your/touchpad_settings"
```

### Post by tora201 on 2012-08-03
No power supply unplug issues like that here. Running latest 3.5 kernel with latest bios.
Only problem now to solve is trying get the right click button on the touchpad (Elantech) to work Everything else, including multi-touch, drag/grab works. Battery seems a lot better too.

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-08-03
> **tora201 said:**
> Only problem now to solve is trying get the right click button on the touchpad (Elantech) to work Everything else, including multi-touch, drag/grab works. Battery seems a lot better too.

I think the issue you're talking about has been fixed.
The [the Wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbook#Touchpad") contains a [link]("http://www.theorangenotebook.com/2012/02/call-for-testing-clickpad.html") to a couple scripts that give full functionality to the Elantech clickpad.

By the way, I have also [modified the [FONT="Courier New"]enable-rightbutton.sh[/FONT] script]("http://pastebin.com/v6au9R3a") to provide a small middle-click region on the bottom half of the middle line of the click-pad. Please let me know if more detail would be helpful.

---

### Post by Ben31 on 2012-08-03
> **tora201 said:**
> No power supply unplug issues like that here. Running latest 3.5 kernel with latest bios.
Only problem now to solve is trying get the right click button on the touchpad (Elantech) to work Everything else, including multi-touch, drag/grab works. Battery seems a lot better too.

You can check my custom [/usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/52-elantech-touchpad.conf]("http://pastebin.com/aTz9AfKm"), which enables the right-click button.

To be able to keep the TapButton settings after resuming from sleep, see my previous post.

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-08-03
> **Ben31 said:**
> You can check my custom [/usr/share/X11/xorg.conf.d/52-elantech-touchpad.conf]("http://pastebin.com/aTz9AfKm"), which enables the right-click button.

To be able to keep the TapButton settings after resuming from sleep, see my previous post.

Oh, awesome. But, why don't you put everything in your [FONT="Courier New"]52-elantech-touchpad.conf[/FONT] file? This way you only have one file, and it will work regardless of which window manager you use? 

[Mine]("http://pastebin.com/mydf8eCx"), for example, currently has all the button settings. I'll add the suggested tweaks for the right-click and click-pad support. Thanks!

---

### Post by Ben31 on 2012-08-03
The button settings are in the conf file, but Gnome 3, when resuming from sleep, resets the TapButton options to the default ones, that is the reason why I use an additional script (containing a simple synclient command) and I force Gnome to take it into account as with the gsettings command from [this post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12141766&postcount=1702").

### Post by GROwen on 2012-08-23
Hi all,

I'm looking for some help with setting up my new UX31-A.

I've done a few Ubuntu installations in the past but never a dual boot with Windows and the partitioning is throwing me, it was making mention of hidden partitions and more partitions than I'd dealt with previously.

It was for this reason that I went with the initial partitions that were suggested during the setup process.

At the end of the installation there was this error;

"It was not possible to install the bootloader at the specified location"

Then three options;
Choose another location (none were succesful)
Continue install without bootloader (the selection I chose)
The last I can't remember but the warning was I may not be able to boot anything if I chose it - so I didn't.

My next step was to reboot from the LiveCD (bootable USB created using unetbootin in Ubuntu and 12.04) and use Ubuntu to get a bit more info, which is where I got the attached screenshot of my partitions.

Does this look anything like other users dual boot partition table?

And where to now? Is it possible to place the bootloader into the partition that says it has the bootloader (sda6 - I think from memory) or is it such a mess that I should start from scratch.

Thanks in advance for any help you can give me with this.

---

### Post by Maya1000 on 2012-08-25
Hi everyone,

 I am using Mint 13 on an UX31A. After kernel upgrade to  3.4.0-030400 the touchpad worked. However After a while - maybe 3-5 min the touchpad it sudenly not working anymore! When I logout and back in it works again? 
I also tried the suggestions here: 


> **Ben31 said:**
> It seems that gnome is resetting touchpad parameters when resuming from sleep. 
If you have a script setting your touchpad parameters, you can force gnome to execute it everytime it wakes up with this command:

```
$ gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.peripherals.input-devices hotplug-command "/absolute_path_to_your/touchpad_settings"
```

> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Oh, awesome. But, why don't you put everything in your [FONT=Courier New]52-elantech-touchpad.conf[/FONT] file? This way you only have one file, and it will work regardless of which window manager you use? 

[Mine]("http://pastebin.com/mydf8eCx"), for  example, currently has all the button settings. I'll add the suggested  tweaks for the right-click and click-pad support. Thanks!

Anyone an idea what's going on?

---

### Post by Maya1000 on 2012-08-26
> **Maya1000 said:**
> Hi everyone,

 I am using Mint 13 on an UX31A. After kernel upgrade to  3.4.0-030400 the touchpad worked. However After a while - maybe 3-5 min the touchpad it sudenly not working anymore! When I logout and back in it works again? 
I also tried the suggestions here: 



Anyone an idea what's going on?

Sorry, found it - unchecked the disable mouse when typing option.    Reboot.

[http://askubuntu.com/questions/68176/touchpad-problem-shortly-after-login-it-stops-working](http://askubuntu.com/questions/68176/touchpad-problem-shortly-after-login-it-stops-working)

### Post by [Neurotic] on 2012-08-26
Hi all,

I'm trying to get the 12.10 daily to boot up from a live USB, and I get as far as the GUI and the window to select "Try Ubuntu" or "Install Ubuntu". I click "Try" and then I get a dialogue saying that a error occurred and I have no dash / side bar.

Anyone managed to get a Live USB running?

I'm trying different options on boot as well - nomodeset doesn't seem to help. Will try a few others, see if that makes any difference.

Thanks in advance for any help!

**EDIT::UPDATE**

Looks like the actual issue was that Unity wasn't installed by default in this daily build on the Live CD! An `apt-get install unity` and suddenly things are back to normal.

Bizarre!

### Post by marcusklaas on 2012-09-01
Does anyone else have problems with the touchpad on the UX31A? It does not seem to register my finger tips. Only when I use a larger part of my finger it recognizes my touch. Is there any setting which defines the smallest area which counts as a touch? I do not think it is a mechanical defect since it seemed to work fine in Windows.. (Running 12.10 daily build by the way with the xorg-edgers repository)

Edit 2: fixed it thanks to the kind people at askubuntu: [http://askubuntu.com/questions/106141/touchpad-insensitive](http://askubuntu.com/questions/106141/touchpad-insensitive)

Lowering FingerHigh does the trick :-D

### Post by MarcusMoeller on 2012-09-06
In 12.10 (20120906) the touchpad pressure problem re-occurs, which is maybe due to a bug that has been fixed. 

In 12.04 the pressure is fine but cannot be adjusted using: synclient FingerLow=9 FingerHigh=12 as this alwayes causes an error like: BadValue (integer parameter out of range for operation)

In 12.10 you are now again able to set it correctly, but the result is still not as good as on 12.04 (at least using the above settings).

So if anyone knows a way to improve this behavior to make it again as good as in precise, I would be glad.

### Post by lemsto on 2012-09-13
> **Ben31 said:**
> ux32a, isn't it with Nvidia GPU?

Nop. **NVIDIA** GPU is in thhe new UX32**VD** :).

UX21**A**, UX31**A**, UX32**A** are still with intel graphics, but the HD 4000 (**IvyBridge**).

And our good old UX21**E** & UX31**E** are with the Intel HD graphics 3000 (**SandyBridge**).

### Post by vivaeltopo on 2012-10-02
Hey,
setting boot parameters is still the same as befor, just put them in your "/etc/default/grub" and everything should be fine. I am currently using the parameters from the wiki "pcie_aspm=force drm.vblankoffdelay=1 i915.semaphores=1" with the latest 3.6 kernel. With alpm activated i get 8.23W with regular desktop usage (Browser, IM, Flash).

I just installed the latest Ubuntu Gnome Remix Beta and wanted to share some observations:

1) After a clean install the graphics driver was llvmpipe, but after installing all available updates i got the regular intel driver without any problems.

2) The Touchpad (Elantech) works out of the box with right click via the bottom right corner of the pad and the two finger tapping. Also two finger scrolling works.

3) The powercord (un)plug problem still occurs. But i dont know where this actually comes from... maybe some kernel issue.

4) One very anoying problem is, that DPMS (Display Power Management Signaling) doesn't really work and the settings in the control center are ignored. The screen goes black every 10min when idle.
I found a workaround, which disables DPMS, that i use while watching movies. I then run:
```
xset -dpms;xset s off
```
If it worked can be checked via
```
xset -q | grep DPMS
```
This is probably not the best way to deal with this, maybe some one has an idea.

5) Until yesterday going into suspend did not lock your screen (which i prefer) but this is fixed after an update.

Those are just some observations with the latest ubuntu release, maybe we can bring this thread back to life :)

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-11-05
> **Scream275 said:**
> First one is the power draining issue on Standby (where I don't got an idea how to Debug or even resolve it). Any ideas on how to start with?

This is not a fix, but rather a work-around. 

I am now using [hybrid suspend-hibernate mode]("http://askubuntu.com/questions/145443/how-do-i-use-pm-suspend-hybrid-by-default-instead-of-pm-suspend"). After some amount of time (for me, 10 minutes) it will switch from suspend to hibernate. Since we have an SSD, the hibernate resumes in < 10 seconds, and incurs 0% battery loss during that time. I definitely recommend it. 

Although this is not a fix, it definitely makes sense to use hibernate with an SSD, so I'm happy with this compromise.

---

### Post by SorryGoFish on 2012-12-03
> **CptPicard said:**
> Does anyone have this working with an external monitor?

I frequently use VGA and HDMI. I have not had the issue you describe. 

It's especially troubling that your HDMI is calling itself VGA1. I'm not sure how you can diagnose this. I suppose it would be good to make sure that you're using the Intel video driver, and check its version. Also, be sure that none of the cables are attached while booting up. Only attach them once you're fully logged in.

### Post by Scream275 on 2012-12-17
Hey,

I'm running my system with Ubuntu 12.10 and I'm getting nearly 6h of battery live (displayed) (~ 4.9W - 5.4W with Wifi and brightness level 3).

I've got kernel parameters, ALPM enabled and missing PowerTop tweaks activated AND I deactivated the SD-Card driver. This driver looks every (i think) 50ms for a new card --> drains energy!

I will post my configs later that day (I'm on another pc). 
But with my config i'm getting RW-Errors on my /home directory (but only with kernels above 3.4 O.o)...would be interesting if anyone else is also getting this (with my config)!

Edit:
/etc/pm/power.d/powersave
```

#!/bin/sh
case $1 in
	true)
	## SD-Cardreader
	rmmod rts5139
	## VM writeback timeout
	echo 1500 > /proc/sys/vm/dirty_writeback_centisecs
	## NMI-Watchdog
	echo 0 > /proc/sys/kernel/watchdog
	## Autosuspend for USB devices

	### USB2.0-CRW (Generic)
	echo auto > /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.7/power/level
	
	### Bluetooth USB Host Controller (Atheros Communications)
	#echo auto > /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.8/power/level

	## Runtime PM for PCI devices

	### Atheros Communications Inc. AR9485 Wireless Network Adapter
	#echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:02:00.0/power/control

        ### Fresco Logic Device 1009
        #echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:03:00.0/power/control
        # Currently this turns the USB3 port off, awaiting response

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### 6 port SATA AHCI Controller
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1f.2/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
	### USB Enhanced Host Controller #1
        #echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1d.0/power/control
        # Causes machine to freeze on shutdown, should work with kernels 3.0.11, 3.1.3 or newer

        ### Intel Corporation 2nd Generation Core Processor Family
        ### DRAM Controller
        #echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:00.0/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### MEI Controller #1
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:16.0/power/control

	### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### High Definition Audio Controller
        #echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1b.0/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### PCI Express Root Port 1
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.0/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### PCI Express Root Port 2
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.1/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 6 Series/C200 Series Chipset Family
        ### PCI Express Root Port 4
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:1c.3/power/control

        ### Intel Corporation 2nd Generation Core Processor Family
        ### Integrated Graphics Controller
        echo auto > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/power/control

        ## SATA link power management
        echo max_performance > /sys/class/scsi_host/host0/link_power_management_policy

        # 50% screen brightness on battery
        #echo 5 > /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:00:02.0/backlight/acpi_video0/brightness

        # Set powersave CPU governor
        #for i in 0 1 2 3; do
        #	echo ondemand > /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu${i}/cpufreq/scaling_governor
        #done
	;;
	false)
	## NMI-Watchdog
	echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/watchdog
	## VM writeback timeout
	echo 60000 > /proc/sys/vm/dirty_writeback_centisecs
	# Set powersave CPU governor
        #for i in 0 1 2 3; do
        #	echo ondemand > /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu${i}/cpufreq/scaling_governor
        #done
	;;
esac

exit 0

```

/etc/pm/config.d/sata_alpm 
```

SATA_ALPM_ENABLE=true

```

Kernel Parameters:
```

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="i915.semaphores=1 pcie_aspm=force drm.vblankoffdelay=1 acpi_osi=Linux quiet splash"

```

### Post by pumo on 2012-12-27
I disabled VTd from bios and use suspend hybrid.
and now overnight about 9 hours drain is just 6%.
booting up from this hybri is quite fast, only annoyning thing, it ask grub to choose where to boot..

I didnt do any setting to suspend hybrid, just edited  /etc/acpi/lid.sh
from original to:
```

#!/bin/bash

LID_CLOSED=$(cat /proc/acpi/button/lid/LID/state | grep open)
if [ "$LID_CLOSED" = "" ]; then
  /usr/sbin/pm-suspend-hybrid
fi

```
kernel is back to 3.6.3-030603-generic and Iam using kubuntu 12.10.
little confused about this hybrid how it could work, I just remembered that my swap partition is just 1gb :)
but it works

---

I had the same problem + the problem where it would power off when the plug was pulled. Then I set the kernel params as in [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/989191](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/989191) and now it powers off at about 4%.  I filed a bug for it:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1096498](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1096498)

### Post by Scream275 on 2013-01-09
If your using my powersave script in /etc/pm/power.d/ the sd-card reader will be disabled when the system turns into powersave mode. The sd-card driver polls every 50ms the port --> drains useless energy.

This is the entry "rmmod ..." in /etc/pm/power.d/powersave

---

### Post by pumo on 2013-01-10
> **Scream275 said:**
> If your using my powersave script in /etc/pm/power.d/ the sd-card reader will be disabled when the system turns into powersave mode. The sd-card driver polls every 50ms the port --> drains useless energy.

This is the entry "rmmod ..." in /etc/pm/power.d/powersave

Thanks! it was that power.d/powersave.
I checked almost every conf file but not that :o
I made script to desktop for use reader, rmmod rts5139 is still in powersave, but I enable only when I need it.

Still having the sudden shutdowns, running Kubuntu 12.10 on my UX21E. What did *not* resolve the issue for me:
-- upgrading to newer kernel to 3.8.*
-- upgrading to newer BIOS to 2.14
-- kernel boot parameters whatsoever
-- power tweaks

### Post by pezed on 2013-01-15
The shutdowns occur in windows as well on my UX31, but seem to only occur in windows after doing a warm reboot from linux.  It's as if linux is doing something in ACPI that corrupts the power table.  Its not a big issue for me though, I normally just close the lid, then unplug power, this seems to prevent shutdown.  Otherwise everything is perfect.  My battery life is right on track with windows, if not a little better.

BTW I had to revert to BIOS 212.  The newest bios from Asus broke a lot of things in linux (backlight control for example)

Hope this will help someone of you! ):P

### Post by psmedley on 2013-01-20
Hi All,

kernel 3.7.3 has the following wireless fixes in it:
commit 71351c91f95f986507a3bf05732284ad3e31dc85
Author: Sujith Manoharan <c_manoha@qca.qualcomm.com>
Date:   Wed Dec 26 12:27:39 2012 +0530

    ath9k_hw: Fix RX gain initvals for AR9485
    
    commit a796a1dd5da9645ad77aa687d1a890ecd63ab5a6 upstream.
    
    Populate iniModesRxGain with the correct initvals
    array for AR9485 v1.1
    
    Signed-off-by: Sujith Manoharan <c_manoha@qca.qualcomm.com>
    Signed-off-by: John W. Linville <linville@tuxdriver.com>
    Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>

commit 1ceaa1eb68534c48bf9107b6fe5ccc45222a3981
Author: Felix Fietkau <nbd@openwrt.org>
Date:   Mon Dec 10 14:03:17 2012 +0100

    ath9k_hw: Fix signal strength / channel noise reporting
    
    commit b7c0c238898d200e80487516e2b67aba2a522cc0 upstream.
    
    While AR_PHY_CCA_NOM_VAL_* does contain the expected internal noise floor
    for a chip measured in clean air, it refers to the lowest expected reading.
    
    Depending on the frequency, this measurement can vary by about 6db, thus
    causing a higher reported channel noise and signal strength.
    
    Factor in the 6db offset when converting internal noisefloor to channel noise.
    
    This patch makes the reported values more accurate for all chips without
    affecting NF calibration behavior.
    

I just installed, so too early to tell if it really helps

### Post by floyd0815aut on 2013-02-08
Hi!

I use the powersaving script someone posted here (big THX!!!) but it was inconvenient to load the sd-cardreader module every time I wanted to use it.
So I made a (really) little script to load/unload the module (rts5139) and notify my about it. (activated/deactivated) And it really just does that - not more. (not unmounting the card etc.)

If someone knows a better/easier way to do that then plz let me know - also if you emprove my script.

PS: You have to set the execute permission and start it with superuser permissions: I made a startmenu entry like "gksu sdCardReader.sh". Also you have to edit the phrase showing in the notification. (it's in German)

### Post by Xgen on 2013-02-23
Perhaps try 'xgamma -gamma (NUMBER LESS THAN 1.00)' e.g. 'xgamma -gamma .6' in a terminal. Experiment until you find the gamma setting that you like and add the command to your Startup Applications. Hopefully that works...(WFM)
Note: If it works in terminal, but not on startup you might have to delay the command by adding 'sleep (NUMBER OF SECONDS) before the command.

### Post by floyd0815aut on 2013-03-24
Try this: (disable wifi-n)
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbookPrime#Wireless](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AsusZenbookPrime#Wireless)

To make it permanent, make a file like "/etc/modprobe.d/51-disable-6235-11n.conf" and write "options iwlwifi 11n_disable=1" into it.

### Post by pumo on 2013-04-23
ok maybe time to call supplier. warranty not over yet (under 1 year) and battery is so bad...

---

### Post by Scream275 on 2013-04-25
> **pumo said:**
> ok maybe time to call supplier. warranty not over yet (under 1 year) and battery is so bad...
and I use almost every time battery to near empty (about 2-5%) and after full charge remove it from charger..

That's not the best way to use a battery, because it gets way too much stress.
Modern batteries (since 10 years, or more) got some charge-circuits. The battery isn't recharged if it's full (or down to 90-95%), so your laptop only uses the power adapters supply.
Btw: Batteries don't lose capacity in a linear way. On the first few cycles a battery loses more capacity.
From the Smartphone area i know, that after two years a battery should have nearly 80% of the capacity when it was new. And you don't know if the battery was good, when you bought it (100%).
If you don't have any issues with the uptime of your laptop: don't send it in. Asus hasn't the best and fastest Support ;)

### Post by Ben31 on 2013-05-01
> **GPif said:**
> No suspend works fine for me.

Check that you don't have a file named "/etc/pm/sleep.d/20_zenbook" remaining. This is a hand-made file recommended by the AsusZenbook page on the Ubuntu wiki. That was working well in 12.04 but prevents the Zenbook to suspend in 13.04.

### Post by gfmichaud on 2013-05-02
Hi !

I don't know if this would help someone but in 12.10 and 13.04 I had problems with brightness settings, after upgrading the BIOS to the latest version (214).
Brightness settings would not be saved and brightness was set to max every the screen was switched on (after rebooting or locking the screen up, for example).

I solved it yersterday by adding > acpi_osi='!Windows 2012' acpi_backlight=vendor in /etc/default/grub
> GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_osi='!Windows 2012' acpi_backlight=vendor"
(Kernel 3.8.0-19-generic)

Now the backlight settings work perfectly fine.

---

### Post by Scream275 on 2013-05-05
Indeed, Wifi quality on Zenbook with Linux is poor. Atheros isn't working (as i know) on Linux drivers...so someone has to find out how this chip works. Apparently this isn't easy!
The only tips i know: 
* don't place your AP behind the screen. Apparently the aluminium shields the Wifi signal...you can test this with your Smartphone (and AP mode).
* deactivate hardware encryption for ath9k (driver module) (was postet some pages before)
* use Windows XP driver with ndiswrapper --> wasn't helpfull for me :(

---

### Post by chrisboo on 2013-05-05
While not being great, my wifi is all right and actually never disconnects while not moving with:
 - 3.6.11 Kernel (From mainline: [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/))
 - Powermanagement off (In shell: "sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off")

This worked for me on 12.10 and 13.04. Any kernel newer than the above which I tried was really bad. Not only was the signal poor but it disconnected frequently.
Btw. my router is behind a thick concrete  wall.

- switching to WPA2 (TKIP) instead of WPA
- switching to a channel with a greater number (from 4 to 11)
- disabling ANI
- disabling power management

Feel free to provide additional information to the Linux Kernel bug as well as the Launchpad bug :
[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=49201](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=49201)

### Post by gfmichaud on 2013-06-14
1) Install all the packages necessary to build the kernel
[FONT=courier new]sudo apt-get install git-core libncurses5 libncurses5-dev libelf-dev asciidoc binutils-dev linux-source qt3-dev-tools libqt3-mt-dev libncurses5 libncurses5-dev fakeroot build-essential crash kexec-tools makedumpfile kernel-wedge kernel-package[/FONT]


2) Download the kernel sources from kernel.org and unzip them ; then go in the kernel sources folder


3) Download the patches and apply them ([https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/) and [https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/))
[FONT=courier new]patch -p1 < PATCH_TO_APPLY[/FONT]


4) Configure the kernel (be sure everything you use is plugged or some modules may be missing - in my case, I didn't plug my USB key and now it doesn't work) ; if asked anything you don't understand, press "enter" to keep the default value
[FONT=courier new]cp -vi /boot/config-`uname -r` .config[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]yes "" | make oldconfig[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]yes "" | make localmodconfig[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]./scripts/config --file .config -d ATH9K_RATE_CONTROL[/FONT]


5) Compile
[FONT=courier new]make -j5[/FONT]


6) Install
[FONT=courier new]sudo make modules_install[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]sudo make install[/FONT]


7) If you want to remove the kernel later on you have to remove :
[FONT=courier new]/boot/vmlinuz*KERNEL-VERSION*[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]/boot/initrd*KERNEL-VERSION*[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]/boot/System-map*KERNEL-VERSION*[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]/boot/config-*KERNEL-VERSION*[/FONT]
[FONT=courier new]/lib/modules/*KERNEL-VERSION*/[/FONT]
then do :
[FONT=courier new]sudo update-grub[/FONT]

---

### Post by Scream275 on 2013-06-20
With the patches i got WAY better wifi performance.

Just a little (different and simpler) tutorial for kernel compilation from me:

1) Install needed packages:
```
sudo apt-get install build-essential kernel-package fakeroot libncurses5-dev git iasl
```

2) Download kernel-sources from kernel.org, unzip them and go into the kernel directory:
[https://www.kernel.org/](https://www.kernel.org/) --> I've took 3.9.6 stable


[COLOR=#000000]*3) Download the two patches and apply them *[/COLOR][https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/)[COLOR=#000000]* , *[/COLOR][https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/)
[FONT=arial]```
patch -p1 < PATCH_TO_APPLY
```[/FONT]


[COLOR=#000000][I]4) Copy your actual kernel config and rebuild it (just exit menuconfig immediately):
[/I][/COLOR]```
[FONT=arial]cp **/**boot**/**config-**`**uname -r**`** ./.config
[I]./scripts/config --file .config -d ATH9K_RATE_CONTROL
[/I][/FONT][FONT=courier new]*[FONT=arial]make menuconfig[/FONT]*[/FONT]
```


[COLOR=#000000]*5) Compile*[/COLOR]

```

fakeroot make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg [COLOR=#660033]--jobs[/COLOR]=[COLOR=#000000]4[/COLOR] [COLOR=#660033]--initrd[/COLOR] [COLOR=#660033]--append-to-version[/COLOR]=-NAME [COLOR=#660033]--revision[/COLOR]=[COLOR=#000000]20130620[/COLOR] kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

```

[COLOR=#000000][I]6) Install the deb packages (make sure that only the kernel deb packages are in this directory)
[/I][/COLOR][FONT=arial]```

cd ../
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
sudo update-grub
```[/FONT]


*7) If you want to remove the kernel later on, just use apt-get and at least do:*
[FONT=arial]```
sudo update-grub
```[/FONT]

---

### Post by gfmichaud on 2013-07-10
It is possible to backport the wifi fix to your current kernel instead of compiling a new kernel :
1) get the lateste archive  here : [https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/](https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/)
2) uncompress the archive and go in the uncompressed folder
3) run "make defconfig-ath9k", then "make" and then "sudo make install"
4) reboot and enjoy the patched version of the driver :)

---

### Post by tuxkillbill on 2013-07-11
> **gfmichaud said:**
> It is possible to backport the wifi fix to your current kernel instead of compiling a new kernel :
1) get the lateste archive  here : [https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/](https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/)
2) uncompress the archive and go in the uncompressed folder
3) run "make defconfig-ath9k", then "make" and then "sudo make install"
4) reboot and enjoy the patched version of the driver :)

Thanks a lot, it works fine.
Ubuntu 13.04 with kernel 3.8.0-26-generic (stock kernel)

---

### Post by denpos on 2013-07-14
> **gfmichaud said:**
> It is possible to backport the wifi fix to your current kernel instead of compiling a new kernel :
1) get the lateste archive  here : [https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/](https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/backports/2013/06/18/)
2) uncompress the archive and go in the uncompressed folder
3) run "make defconfig-ath9k", then "make" and then "sudo make install"
4) reboot and enjoy the patched version of the driver :)

> **Scream275 said:**
> With the patches i got WAY better wifi performance.

Just a little (different and simpler) tutorial for kernel compilation from me:

1) Install needed packages:
```
sudo apt-get install build-essential kernel-package fakeroot libncurses5-dev git iasl
```

2) Download kernel-sources from kernel.org, unzip them and go into the kernel directory:
[https://www.kernel.org/](https://www.kernel.org/) --> I've took 3.9.6 stable


[COLOR=#000000]*3) Download the two patches and apply them *[/COLOR][https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2696121/raw/)[COLOR=#000000]* , *[/COLOR][https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/2718041/raw/)
[FONT=arial]```
patch -p1 < PATCH_TO_APPLY
```[/FONT]


[COLOR=#000000][I]4) Copy your actual kernel config and rebuild it (just exit menuconfig immediately):
[/I][/COLOR]```
[FONT=arial]cp **/**boot**/**config-**`**uname -r**`** ./.config
[I]./scripts/config --file .config -d ATH9K_RATE_CONTROL
[/I][/FONT][FONT=courier new]*[FONT=arial]make menuconfig[/FONT]*[/FONT]
```


[COLOR=#000000]*5) Compile*[/COLOR]

```

fakeroot make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg [COLOR=#660033]--jobs[/COLOR]=[COLOR=#000000]4[/COLOR] [COLOR=#660033]--initrd[/COLOR] [COLOR=#660033]--append-to-version[/COLOR]=-NAME [COLOR=#660033]--revision[/COLOR]=[COLOR=#000000]20130620[/COLOR] kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

```

[COLOR=#000000][I]6) Install the deb packages (make sure that only the kernel deb packages are in this directory)
[/I][/COLOR][FONT=arial]```

cd ../
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
sudo update-grub
```[/FONT]


*7) If you want to remove the kernel later on, just use apt-get and at least do:*
[FONT=arial]```
sudo update-grub
```[/FONT]

I tried both methods (applying patch on stock kernel and applying patch on latest stable kernel), but both methods did not work for me. After installing the kernel my wifi module does not seem to find any wireless networks anymore...


EDIT: none of my modules seem to work, although I am sure that I did everything right according to the instructions. Anyone having the same problem?

EDIT2: solved the wifi problem by just installing another kernel than the stock kernel (3.0.8-26-generic) without applying the patches. When installing kernel 3.9.x or 3.10.x, the wifi problem is solved but the brightness keys don't function properly. Both wifi and brightness keys are solved using kernel 3.8.13-03081304-generic: got a stable download speed of 64mbit/s with brightness keys functioning as they should.

### Post by gfmichaud on 2013-09-13
> **teeedubb said:**
> I'm not 100% sure, but the wifi has been problem free since installing 3.11-rc6.

Yes the fix **is** included in kernel 3.11 (and will therefore be in Saucy ! :))

You can backport it to your current 3.8 kernel but will have to backport again every time there is a kernel update.

### Post by DeusExMachinae on 2013-10-18
Hey there people!

I update to Ubuntu 13.10 today and almost immediately ran into a problem with it. When I suspend my laptop by closing the lid and resume work by opening it I get no WiFi whatsoever. When I click the icon on the top bar it says that "network not enabled" (or something similar, translating from Finnish here. Not turned on, not connected etc.) and even if I click the option that says "Enable network" it doesn't do anything. I've tried using the keyboard hotkeys, but they don't do squat. It seems as if my wireless adapter was disabled when suspending the computer but was not re-enabled after that.

Everything worked perfectly in 13.04. 

How should I begin to troubleshoot this problem? Is there a way to revert back to 13.04 without reinstalling everything? What to do, what to do, what to do...

### Post by andrea-arricale on 2013-11-07
Hi everybody,

I'm not sure if this was already been said but, as I couldn't find anything about it, I'll take the risk of giving a duplicate advice.
I found myself fairly well with the **touchpad synclient script** suggested in the wiki, but... The touchpad wasn't really responsive as it should on such a powerful machine.
Of course, it was a synclient settings problem.
Basically, the suggested command "synclient FastTaps" doesn't exist anymore. After a really quick search and test, I found this working awesomely good.

```
synclient SingleTapTimeout=150
```

You can, of course, search for the solution that fits the best for you. 
Lower values get the touchpad to respond instantly (which is awesome) but be careful not to set it too low because you could lose the drag and drop feature.

Let me know how it goes.

I hope this helps!


P.S. Why don't we test it and put this script in the Wiki?

---

### Post by yaronv99 on 2013-12-22
Ok so after more then a year experiencing shutdowns when unplugging the charger or when plugging it back, i found a solution online.
(and it works for me on 100% on the time).
(i'm a ux21 owner)
I purchased a screwdriver to open my ux21 from ebay, then found the battery and the battery cabel that connects it to the board, 
i unplugged it, waited a minute and plugged it back. (really simple, no reasons to be afraid).
After turning back on the computer, the issue is gone!!

Hope it helps

---

### Post by Kornl on 2014-03-18
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Wait, what? Really? Can you please post the source of this? Has anybody tried this? Why would it work? What are the risks?

If you look [here]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/989191"), they say the following:

[INDENT][COLOR=#333333][FONT=Ubuntu Mono]Yes, this is definitely a product of that old memory controller problem. If you're experiencing this bug, the only solution is to find a torx T5, open the case, and disconnect the battery for a moment. This is a hardware problem more than it is a software problem.[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=#333333][FONT=Ubuntu Mono]Opening the case is fairly easy. There is a useful description with pictures here: [http://www.ski-epic.com/2012_asus_zenbook_ux31_disassembly_teardown/](http://www.ski-epic.com/2012_asus_zenbook_ux31_disassembly_teardown/)[/FONT][/COLOR]
[COLOR=#333333][FONT=Ubuntu Mono]You don't have to remove the battery. Just pull straight up on the connector to the mainboard. There's a helpful little black pull tab for this.[/FONT][/COLOR][/INDENT]

### Post by Kornl on 2014-03-19
> **SorryGoFish said:**
> Wow. If this works, I'll be so happy. The little spike of fear I get every time my power cord falls out is inching me to an early grave.

I tried it myself today. Opening the Zenbook is easy (if you have a T5 screwdriver). And this is the cable connecting the battery and the board:

[IMG]http://algorithm-forge.com/techblog/wp-content/uploads/2014/03/Asus-Zenbook-UX31E-Battery-cable.jpg[/IMG]

Unplugging it was not that easy. (Perhaps they used some glue? Or it was simply stuck.) You have to pull it straight up. I was really worried to damage the board if I use to much force, but in the end I managed to unplug it. Now everything seems to work fine. :p 
(Disclaimer: I have no idea what I'm talking about - do everything at your own risk - but I hope this picture and comment is nevertheless useful.)
